findings.title: ConfigManager mixes file I/O, encryption, settings, credentials, tunnel state, and full tunnel-group CRUD
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read: `class ConfigManager` spans exactly lines 87-808 (the entire rest of the file). It genuinely owns 6 separable concerns: (1) atomic file I/O + backup/restore/rotation (_write_config_atomic_unlocked L144, _create_backup L274, _cleanup_old_backups L303, restore_backup L372), (2) stale-snapshot merge logic (_merge_snapshot_changes L201, _merge_setting_values L188), (3) import/export validation (_validate_import_data L451, _validate_port L441, export_config L409, import_config L501), (4) app settings/timeouts (get_app_setting L552, get_network_timeout_check/download L557/565, set_app_setting L573), (5) credential decryption (get_tunnel_credentials L589, encryptor property L582) + active-tunnel state (save_active_tunnels L600, get_last_active_tunnels L608), and (6) tunnel group management (get_groups L617 through save_group_collapsed_state L789-808, ~192 lines) which is a distinct feature.
findings.recommendation: Extract lines 617-808 (get_groups, add_group, update_group, delete_group, move_tunnel_to_group, get_tunnel_group, save_group_collapsed_state) into a new `TunnelGroupManager` class in `src/core/group_manager.py`, constructed with a `ConfigManager` instance so it reuses load_config/save_config. Keep ConfigManager scoped to file lifecycle + the settings/credential accessors that are genuinely about the config file.
findings.verification_note: Line range and all cited method names/line numbers verified exact against the file. Severity (high) is justified: 722-line class with 6 genuinely independent responsibilities, one of which (group CRUD) has essentially no coupling to config-file mechanics beyond calling load/save.

findings.title: db_core_service.py bundles three distinct architectural layers in one 782-line file
findings.detail: Confirmed: file is exactly 782 lines. Layer (a) subprocess transport: DbCoreServiceClient (L110-253), _format_error_event (L25-42). Layer (b) product facade: DbCoreFacade (L256-422) + get_shared_db_core_facade/shutdown_shared_db_core_facade singleton (L424-447). Layer (c) DB-API-compatibility shim: RustDbConnector (L450-615), create_rust_db_connector (L617-638), RustDbConnection (L641-718), RustDbCursor (L721-777), quote_mysql_ident (L780-781). These have different audiences (subprocess plumbing vs. UI/worker call sites vs. legacy cursor-shaped call sites).
findings.recommendation: Split into `db_core_client.py` (DbCoreServiceClient, _format_error_event, parse_db_version_tuple/normalize_db_engine/default_database_for_engine helpers), `db_core_facade.py` (DbEndpoint, DbCoreFacade, shared-facade singleton), and `db_core_dbapi_shim.py` (RustDbConnector, RustDbConnection, RustDbCursor, quote_mysql_ident, create_rust_db_connector). Re-export from db_core_service.py during the transition to avoid breaking the many existing import sites (db_connector.py, postgres_connector.py, scheduler.py, tunnel_monitor.py, etc.).
findings.verification_note: Line boundaries for each layer verified exact against the file content.

findings.title: sql_validator.py bundles two unrelated product features (validation, autocomplete) plus shared SQL-parsing utilities
findings.detail: Confirmed: file is exactly 869 lines. Contains standalone parsing utilities (extract_cte_names L109, extract_derived_table_aliases L169, extract_table_aliases L200, _read_identifier L39, _skip_balanced_parentheses L67, _normalize_identifier L32), schema-metadata cache (SchemaMetadata L256, SchemaMetadataProvider L305), SQLValidator (L389-697, ~309 lines), and SQLAutoCompleter (L700-869, ~169 lines) which is a different feature (editor autocomplete) reusing the same parsing helpers.
findings.recommendation: Split into `sql_identifier_utils.py` (extract_cte_names/extract_derived_table_aliases/extract_table_aliases/_read_identifier/_skip_balanced_parentheses/_normalize_identifier/ALIAS_STOP_WORDS), `sql_validator.py` (SQLValidator, ValidationIssue, IssueSeverity, SchemaMetadata, SchemaMetadataProvider), and `sql_autocompleter.py` (SQLAutoCompleter). Both consumers import the shared parsing module.
findings.verification_note: Line boundaries verified exact (SQLValidator class body 389-697, SQLAutoCompleter 700-869).

findings.title: Read-modify-write pattern (`with _CONFIG_LOCK: load_config() -> mutate -> save_config()`) repeated verbatim 7 times
findings.detail: Confirmed all 7 sites match the described lock/load/mutate/save shape exactly: set_app_setting (573-580), save_active_tunnels (600-606), add_group (636-657), update_group (671-688), delete_group (703-718), move_tunnel_to_group (734-768), save_group_collapsed_state (799-806, followed by an outer `return False` at 808 which is not part of the pattern). Any future change to the transaction shape (e.g. retry-on-stale-write) must currently be replicated across all 7.
findings.recommendation: Add a private helper `_mutate_config(self, mutator: Callable[[dict], T]) -> T` doing `with _CONFIG_LOCK: config = self.load_config(); result = mutator(config); self.save_config(config); return result`, and have all 7 call sites pass a small closure instead of repeating the lock/load/save scaffolding.
findings.verification_note: All 7 line ranges cross-checked against the file; the read-modify-write shape is identical at each site.

findings.title: MySQLConnector re-implements schema listing/existence queries that its own RustDbConnector delegate already provides
findings.detail: Confirmed: MySQLConnector.__init__ (L115-118) constructs `self._delegate = RustDbConnector("mysql", ...)`, which has working get_schemas (db_core_service.py L518-544) and schema_exists (L494-516). Instead of delegating, MySQLConnector.get_schemas (165-198) re-issues `SHOW DATABASES` filtered by SYSTEM_SCHEMAS itself, and MySQLConnector.schema_exists (200-210) re-issues `SHOW DATABASES LIKE %s` itself. PostgresConnector.schema_exists (postgres_connector.py 47-58) also independently re-implements the postgresql information_schema.schemata query verbatim, and does not use a RustDbConnector delegate at all (unlike MySQLConnector) ‚ÄĒ confirmed by reading postgres_connector.py, which builds its own DbEndpoint/RustDbConnection directly.
findings.recommendation: Have MySQLConnector.get_schemas/schema_exists call self._delegate.get_schemas()/self._delegate.schema_exists() (wrapping only the TTL cache around the delegate call), and give PostgresConnector a RustDbConnector delegate the same way MySQLConnector does, instead of hand-rolling the information_schema.schemata query a third time.
findings.verification_note: Verified all three implementations (MySQLConnector L165-210, RustDbConnector L494-544, PostgresConnector L47-58) exist as described with the claimed near-duplicate SQL.

findings.title: MySQLConnector.get_db_version manually re-parses version strings instead of reusing parse_db_version_tuple
findings.detail: Confirmed: MySQLConnector.get_db_version (300-327) hand-parses via `version_str.split('-')[0]` then `.split('.')` then manual `int(parts[i])` with bounds checks. db_core_service.py defines `parse_db_version_tuple` (L48-67) with a more robust regex fallback, and RustDbConnector.get_db_version (L567-568) already does exactly `return parse_db_version_tuple(self.get_db_version_string())`. MySQLConnector's hand-rolled version has weaker behavior (no regex fallback for odd version strings).
findings.recommendation: Replace the body of MySQLConnector.get_db_version with `return parse_db_version_tuple(self.get_db_version_string())`, importing `parse_db_version_tuple` from `src.core.db_core_service`.
findings.verification_note: All cited line numbers (db_connector.py 300-327, db_core_service.py 48-67 and 567-568) verified exact.

findings.title: DbEndpoint is manually reconstructed field-by-field instead of using dataclasses.replace
findings.detail: Confirmed: RustDbConnector.get_tables (546-557) and RustDbConnection.select_db (707-716) each rebuild a `DbEndpoint(engine=..., host=..., port=..., user=..., password=..., database=..., schema=...)`, re-listing all 7 fields to change only 1-2. DbEndpoint is `@dataclass(frozen=True)` (L88-107), exactly the case `dataclasses.replace()` targets. However, both occurrences today correctly list every field with no divergence, so there is no current behavioral bug ‚ÄĒ the risk is purely that a future new field on DbEndpoint could be silently omitted at one of the two sites (if the field has a default) rather than failing loudly.
findings.recommendation: Replace both reconstructions with `dataclasses.replace(self.endpoint, database=..., schema=...)` (computing the new database/schema values first via the existing conditional logic, then calling replace), eliminating the duplicated 7-field list at both call sites.
findings.verification_note: Severity corrected from medium to low: only 2 call sites exist, both are currently correct (no live behavioral divergence), and the fix is purely stylistic risk-reduction against future field additions rather than fixing an existing bug ‚ÄĒ this is a lower-impact case than the other 'medium' duplication findings (#5, #6) which involve actual divergent/weaker logic today.

findings.title: RustDbConnector.__init__ takes 7 loose primitive params that are immediately repackaged into the DbEndpoint it already has
findings.detail: Confirmed: `RustDbConnector.__init__(self, engine, host, port, user, password, database=None, schema="", facade=None)` (453-463) builds a DbEndpoint from them in its first statement (464-472). `create_rust_db_connector` (617-638) has an identical 7-param signature and does the same repackaging one level up. Grep confirms this exploded-param signature is called directly from at least 7 production sites (db_connector.py:115, test_worker.py:244/246/285, scheduler.py:789, sql_editor_workers.py:16, test_dialogs.py:188, tunnel_monitor.py:473), so this is a real, widespread API smell, not an isolated one-off.
findings.recommendation: Change RustDbConnector.__init__ to accept `endpoint: DbEndpoint` (plus `facade`) directly, and have create_rust_db_connector build the DbEndpoint once (it already computes resolved_engine/default_database_for_engine) and pass it straight through.
findings.verification_note: Line ranges verified exact; confirmed via grep that the 7-param constructor pattern is used across at least 7 distinct production files, supporting medium severity.

findings.title: SQLValidator keeps its own private SYSTEM_SCHEMAS list instead of importing the shared constant, and it has already drifted
findings.detail: Confirmed: SQLValidator.SYSTEM_SCHEMAS (406-414) = {'INFORMATION_SCHEMA','MYSQL','PERFORMANCE_SCHEMA','SYS','NDBINFO'}. src/core/constants.py defines a shared `SYSTEM_SCHEMAS = frozenset({'information_schema','mysql','performance_schema','sys'})` (constants.py L13-18) which db_connector.py (L187) and db_core_service.py (L537) both import and use for the same exclusion purpose. The shared constant is missing 'NDBINFO', confirming the two lists have already diverged.
findings.recommendation: Delete SQLValidator.SYSTEM_SCHEMAS and import SYSTEM_SCHEMAS from src.core.constants instead (adding NDBINFO to the central constant if it should be excluded from schema listings too), establishing one source of truth for system-schema exclusion.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed both lists exist as described via direct read of both files; confirmed the shared constant is actually consumed in db_connector.py and db_core_service.py for the same purpose.

findings.title: _validate_version_compatibility contains two near-identical scan loops for keywords vs. functions
findings.detail: Confirmed: keyword-check loop (606-620: iterate MYSQL8_KEYWORDS, `rf'\b{keyword}\b'`, skip string regions, compute line/col, append ValidationIssue) and function-check loop (623-637: iterate MYSQL8_FUNCTIONS, `rf'\b{func}\s*\('`, same skip/compute/append) are structurally identical apart from source collection, regex suffix, and message wording.
findings.recommendation: Extract a shared helper `_flag_unsupported_items(sql, items, pattern_fn, message_fn, string_regions, line_offsets, major, minor)` and call it twice (once for MYSQL8_KEYWORDS, once for MYSQL8_FUNCTIONS), removing the duplicated loop body.
findings.verification_note: Line ranges verified exact; loop bodies confirmed structurally identical apart from the noted differences.

findings.title: SQLAutoCompleter.get_completions inlines context detection, table completion, two column-completion branches, and keyword/function completion
findings.detail: Confirmed: the method (727-809, 83 lines) does context analysis (_analyze_context, _get_current_word), branches for table completion (746-754), then a column-completion branch that splits into target-table sub-branch (760-772) and all-from-tables sub-branch (773-784), and finally (guarded by `if not context.get('table')`) appends keyword completions (789-797) and function completions (799-807).
findings.recommendation: Extract `_complete_tables(metadata, prefix)`, `_complete_columns_for_table(sql, metadata, target_table, prefix)`, `_complete_columns_from_from_clause(sql, metadata, prefix)`, and `_complete_keywords_and_functions(prefix)` helpers; have get_completions dispatch to them based on context.
findings.verification_note: Line ranges and internal branch structure verified exact against the file.

findings.title: sql_history.py never uses the shared logger and silently swallows corrupted/unwritable history with no trace
findings.detail: Confirmed: sql_history.py has no `from src.core.logger import get_logger` import (unlike config_manager.py, db_core_service.py, db_connector.py). _save_history (48-54) catches IOError and only does `print(f"Ūěąžä§ŪÜ†Ž¶¨ ž†Äžě• žė§Ž•ė: {e}")` (line 54). _load_history (36-46) catches `(json.JSONDecodeError, IOError)` and returns [] with no logging at all (lines 45-46). The module docstring (line 5) states history is permanent/never-deletable, making the silent discard on corruption a real gap.
findings.recommendation: Import `get_logger('sql_history')` as other core modules do, log a warning/error in _load_history's except-branch before returning [], and replace the bare print() in _save_history with logger.error(...).
findings.verification_note: Confirmed no logger import anywhere in the file (grepped/read in full); confirmed the print() and silent-return behavior exactly as described.

findings.title: The 'match entry by id-or-timestamp' fallback lookup is re-implemented three times
findings.detail: Confirmed: update_status (107-111, condition at 109), update_status_batch (127-133, condition at 131-132), and toggle_favorite (280-286, condition at 281) all duplicate the same id-or-timestamp matching rule as inline conditionals.
findings.recommendation: Extract a helper `_matches_history_id(entry, history_id) -> bool` and use it in all three methods; for update_status_batch, build a single ids_set check via that helper instead of the redundant `entry_id in ids_set or entry.get('timestamp') in ids_set` expression.
findings.verification_note: All three call sites verified exact against the file; the redundant double-check in update_status_batch (line 132) confirmed as described.

findings.title: parse_sql_statement_ranges is a single ~135-line state machine handling six distinct scanning rules inline
findings.detail: Confirmed: the main while loop (69-169) inlines DELIMITER directive handling (73-84), line comments (86-91), block comments (93-101), dollar-quoted strings (103-111), quoted strings (113-122), custom-delimiter splitting (124-127, 161-164), dollar-quote detection (129-135), and plain accumulation (166-167), all sharing mutable closure state (quote, dollar_quote, line_comment, block_comment, escape_next, i).
findings.recommendation: Factor each scanning rule into its own state-transition function (e.g. _try_consume_line_comment, _try_consume_block_comment, _try_consume_dollar_quote, _try_consume_quoted_string) receiving/returning the parser cursor/state, keeping the top-level loop a thin dispatcher.
findings.verification_note: Function boundaries and each named scanning rule verified exact against the file. Low severity is reasonable ‚ÄĒ the loop is already broken into clear guarded blocks with early continues, so it's more a testability/readability improvement than an urgent risk.

findings.title: RustDbCursor.execute branches on a facade capability that every real facade in this codebase has, and its fallback references a non-existent attribute
findings.detail: Confirmed: RustDbCursor.execute (738) checks `hasattr(self.connection.facade, "execute_on_connection_result")`. DbCoreFacade (the only facade constructed anywhere in production code) always defines execute_on_connection_result (332-348). Grepped every RustDbConnection construction site (db_core_service.py:483, postgres_connector.py:33, and all test fixtures) ‚ÄĒ no production facade lacks the method. The else branch (747-753) reads `getattr(self.connection.facade, "last_rows_affected", len(self._rows))`; grep confirms `last_rows_affected` is never defined as a real attribute anywhere except as a misleading class attribute on a test FakeFacade (tests/test_db_core_service.py:328) whose test (test_rust_db_cursor_rowcount_uses_call_local_rows_affected) still defines execute_on_connection_result, so it never actually exercises the else branch either ‚ÄĒ confirming the fallback path is untested and effectively dead in this codebase.
findings.recommendation: If DbCoreFacade is the only supported facade, drop the hasattr branch and the last_rows_affected fallback entirely and call execute_on_connection_result unconditionally. If a lighter facade is genuinely expected in tests, define last_rows_affected explicitly on that test double rather than relying on getattr's silent default.
findings.verification_note: Verified via grep that no production or test facade actually lacks execute_on_connection_result, and that the one test with a misleadingly-named last_rows_affected class attribute doesn't exercise the else branch ‚ÄĒ fully substantiating the dead-code claim.

findings.title: SQLHistory.search_advanced takes 7 filter parameters
findings.detail: Confirmed: `search_advanced(self, keyword=None, date_from=None, date_to=None, success_only=None, favorites_only=False, limit=50, offset=0)` has 7 parameters (204-213), all optional filter criteria plus pagination.
findings.recommendation: Bundle the filter criteria (keyword, date_from, date_to, success_only, favorites_only) into a small HistorySearchFilter dataclass, keeping limit/offset as separate pagination parameters: `search_advanced(self, filter: HistorySearchFilter, limit=50, offset=0)`.
findings.verification_note: Signature verified exact against the file at the given lines.

findings.title: Fuzzy-match cutoff 0.5 is repeated as an unnamed literal
findings.detail: Confirmed: get_similar_tables (293-295) and get_similar_columns (297-302) both call `get_close_matches(..., cutoff=0.5)` with the same unexplained literal repeated in two places.
findings.recommendation: Extract a module-level constant `FUZZY_MATCH_CUTOFF = 0.5` and reference it from both call sites.
findings.verification_note: Both call sites verified exact against the file.

findings.title: Windows FILE_ATTRIBUTE_HIDDEN flag is an unexplained literal 0x02
findings.detail: Confirmed: `ctypes.windll.kernel32.SetFileAttributesW(KEY_FILE, 0x02)` (line 66) uses the raw value 0x02 with only a Korean comment above it (line 63: 'Windows žą®ÍĻÄ ŪĆĆžĚľ žĄ§ž†ē') rather than a named constant.
findings.recommendation: Define `FILE_ATTRIBUTE_HIDDEN = 0x02` (or import from a platform constants module) and use the named constant in the SetFileAttributesW call.
findings.verification_note: Line range and literal usage verified exact against the file.

findings.title: BackupScheduler mixes scheduling engine, backup export, ad-hoc SQL execution, result serialization, and retention policy in one 1078-line file
findings.detail: Confirmed by reading the full class body (lines 242-1078, end of file). BackupScheduler owns: cron/queue scheduling mechanics (start/stop/_run_loop/_execution_worker_loop/_ensure_execution_thread, lines 311-531), shared connection resolution (_resolve_connection, line 533), full DB backup export via RustDumpExporter (_execute_backup + _cleanup_old_backups, lines 583-706), arbitrary multi-statement SQL execution (_execute_sql_query/_execute_single_query, lines 773-938), result serialization to CSV/JSON (_save_as_csv/_save_as_json, lines 982-1016), a second independent retention/cleanup routine (_cleanup_old_results, lines 1018-1077), and its own text-log persistence (_log_backup/get_backup_logs, lines 708-767). None of these depend on each other's internals.
findings.recommendation: Split into cooperating collaborators: keep BackupScheduler as a thin orchestrator over the cron/queue mechanics, and extract BackupTaskExecutor (_execute_backup + _cleanup_old_backups), SqlQueryTaskExecutor (_execute_sql_query/_execute_single_query/_save_as_csv/_save_as_json/_cleanup_old_results), and an ExecutionLogWriter (_log_backup/get_backup_logs) into their own modules/classes composed via dependency injection.
findings.verification_note: Re-read lines 242-1078; class runs to end of file exactly as described. All named methods and their line ranges confirmed accurate. Severity 'high' is justified given the size (~836 lines) and genuinely unrelated concerns bundled together.

findings.title: Retention-cleanup algorithm duplicated between _cleanup_old_backups and _cleanup_old_results
findings.detail: Confirmed both methods implement the identical 3-step algorithm: sort ascending by timestamp, build cutoff = now - timedelta(days=retention_days) and mark stale entries, then compute 'remaining' and mark the oldest excess beyond retention_count. _cleanup_old_backups spans exactly lines 654-706 (verified: def at 654, ends at 706 with the outer except/log). _cleanup_old_results spans lines 1018-1077 (verified: def at 1018, ends at 1077). Only the listing source (os.listdir dirs vs files) and timestamp source (parsed folder name vs os.path.getmtime) differ.
findings.recommendation: Extract a shared helper `_select_paths_for_retention(entries: List[Tuple[str, datetime]], retention_days: int, retention_count: int) -> List[str]` that both methods call after building their own (path, timestamp) list; keep only the listing/deletion side effects in each caller.
findings.verification_note: Both cited line ranges (654-706 and 1018-1077) checked and match exactly. Anchoring the finding's line_start/line_end at the first occurrence while describing the duplicate counterpart in the detail text is acceptable for a duplication finding.

findings.title: _log_backup is used to log both backup runs and unrelated SQL-query task runs
findings.detail: Confirmed via grep: _log_backup (defined lines 708-725, docstring 'ŽįĪžóÖ Ž°úÍ∑ł ž†Äžě•') is called from _execute_backup (lines 597, 640, 645, 651) AND from _execute_sql_query (lines 785, 803, 812, 826, 847, 856) for SQL-query schedules that have nothing to do with backups. All cited call-site line numbers verified exact.
findings.recommendation: Rename to `_log_execution` (update docstring and log-file naming/variable names accordingly) so the method's name matches its actual scope of use across both task types.

findings.title: Unused import DEFAULT_MYSQL_PORT
findings.detail: Confirmed via grep across the whole file: `from src.core.constants import DEFAULT_MYSQL_PORT, DEFAULT_LOCAL_HOST` (line 23) is the only occurrence of DEFAULT_MYSQL_PORT in scheduler.py; it is never referenced elsewhere. DEFAULT_LOCAL_HOST is used at line 575.
findings.recommendation: Remove the unused DEFAULT_MYSQL_PORT import from scheduler.py.

findings.title: Variable named 'engine' actually holds an endpoint object, then reused to derive 'engine_name'
findings.detail: Confirmed at lines 885-886: `engine = getattr(getattr(connector, "connection", None), "endpoint", None)` then `engine_name = getattr(engine, "engine", "mysql")`. Also confirmed _execute_sql_query (which calls _execute_single_query at lines 820-822) already computed `resolved.engine` via _resolve_connection but does not pass it through -- _execute_single_query re-derives the engine name from connector internals instead.
findings.recommendation: Rename the intermediate variable to `endpoint` and/or thread `resolved.engine` (already computed in _execute_sql_query) into _execute_single_query as a parameter instead of re-deriving it from the connector's internals.

findings.title: SSHTunnelForwarder construction plus connection-log building repeated 3 times
findings.detail: Confirmed all three sites (_start_ssh_tunnel lines 112-164, create_temp_tunnel lines 202-230, _test_ssh_tunnel_connection lines 358-414) construct SSHTunnelForwarder with identical bastion_host/bastion_port/ssh_username/ssh_pkey/remote_bind_address kwargs, differing only in local_bind_address and set_keepalive. Two of the three (_start_ssh_tunnel and _test_ssh_tunnel_connection) additionally build a near-identical `connection_logs` list of Korean/emoji status strings before joining it into the failure message.
findings.recommendation: Extract a private helper `_build_forwarder(config, local_bind_address, set_keepalive=None) -> SSHTunnelForwarder` that resolves the shared kwargs from `config` once, reused by all three call sites; factor the repeated connection-log accumulation into a small helper/class.

findings.title: Parameter/dict key 'tid' is abbreviated inconsistently with 'tunnel_id' used elsewhere in the codebase
findings.detail: Confirmed start_tunnel (77-110), stop_tunnel (166-180), is_running (182-189), get_connection_info (191-200), and get_active_tunnels (316-331) all use `tid`, while scheduler.py (ScheduleConfig.tunnel_id, _resolve_connection) and tunnel_monitor.py (_measure_latency, _attempt_reconnect, on_tunnel_connected, etc.) consistently spell out `tunnel_id`.
findings.recommendation: Rename the `tid` parameters/locals in TunnelEngine to `tunnel_id` to match the naming already used by its callers (BackupScheduler, TunnelMonitor).
findings.verification_note: Original line_end of 200 only covers start_tunnel/stop_tunnel/is_running/get_connection_info; get_active_tunnels (also named in the finding's own detail) lives at lines 316-331, outside that range. Widened line_end to 331 so the cited range covers all five methods actually described.

findings.title: TunnelMonitor combines state-machine monitoring, DB health-check connection management, and auto-reconnect orchestration
findings.detail: Confirmed class spans lines 81-648 (end of file). It owns the state machine/event history (_check_all_tunnels, _add_event, get_recent_events), a health-check connection cache (_health_connections, _create_health_connection, _cleanup_health_connection(s), _measure_latency, _get_health_credentials), and a recursive auto-reconnect state machine with backoff (_attempt_reconnect + nested reconnect()). Counted exactly 14 public (non-underscore) methods: add_callback, remove_callback, start_monitoring, stop_monitoring, is_running, get_status, get_all_statuses, get_recent_events, set_auto_reconnect, is_auto_reconnect_enabled, set_max_reconnect_attempts, get_max_reconnect_attempts, on_tunnel_connected, on_tunnel_disconnected -- matching the finding's count precisely.
findings.recommendation: Extract a `TunnelHealthChecker` (owns _health_connections, _measure_latency, _create_health_connection, _get_health_credentials) and a `TunnelReconnector` (owns _attempt_reconnect and its backoff schedule) as separate collaborators composed by TunnelMonitor, leaving TunnelMonitor responsible only for state transitions and the event/callback log.

findings.title: Nested reconnect() closure mixes low-level threading/sleep with high-level reconnect business logic and recurses on itself
findings.detail: Confirmed _attempt_reconnect (512-604) defines a nested `reconnect()` closure spanning lines 549-602 that sleeps for the backoff delay, re-reads tunnel_engine.tunnel_configs, calls stop_tunnel/start_tunnel, updates TunnelStatus fields under lock, and then recursively calls `self._attempt_reconnect(tunnel_id)` to schedule the next retry -- all captured by `threading.Thread(target=reconnect, daemon=True).start()` at line 604.
findings.recommendation: Pull the closure out into a named method, e.g. `_reconnect_after_delay(self, tunnel_id, delay, status)`, invoked via `threading.Thread(target=self._reconnect_after_delay, args=(tunnel_id, delay, status), daemon=True).start()`; this makes the recursive scheduling explicit and testable in isolation.
findings.verification_note: Original line_end of 599 cut off the closure's own except-block tail (lines 600-602, still inside reconnect()); corrected to 602, the true end of the nested function body (line 603 is blank, line 604 starts the enclosing thread-spawn call).

findings.title: Reconnect backoff schedule is an inline unexplained literal list
findings.detail: Confirmed line 537: `backoff = [1, 2, 5, 10, 30, 60]` is a bare literal with no unit/rationale comment.
findings.recommendation: Hoist it to a module-level named constant `RECONNECT_BACKOFF_SECONDS = (1, 2, 5, 10, 30, 60)` with a short comment on the growth policy, and reference that constant from _attempt_reconnect.

findings.title: Unnamed timeout/poll-interval literals in notify_existing_instance

findings.title: Windows and macOS base-directory resolution is copy-pasted verbatim across app_support_dir, data_dir, and log_dir
findings.detail: Confirmed all three functions (app_support_dir 24-41, data_dir 44-61, log_dir 64-81) repeat the identical Windows branch (`base = Path(env.get("LOCALAPPDATA") or home_path / "AppData" / "Local")`) and identical Darwin branch pattern, differing only in the Linux/XDG env-var name and trailing path segment.

findings.title: StartupRegistrar implements two unrelated OS-specific startup mechanisms (Windows registry Run key and macOS LaunchAgent plist) in a single class
findings.detail: Confirmed class spans lines 140-298 (end of file). is_registered()/set_registered() branch on is_macos() with inline winreg calls for Windows vs. dedicated plist methods for macOS (_macos_launch_agent_path, _macos_launch_agent_log_dir, _macos_launch_agent_matches_current_app, _set_macos_launch_agent, _macos_startup_arguments, _macos_working_directory, _macos_launch_agent_plist -- 7 methods, plus _home_path which by grep is used only by the macOS-path builders, bringing the macOS-only surface to ~8 methods). findings.title: SchemaConfirmDialog._init_ui builds the entire dialog (header, labels, input, buttons, all with inline QSS) in one 131-line method
findings.detail: Confirmed _init_ui spans lines 76-207 (~132 lines): window title/size, header QFrame with inline stylesheet, env label, operation label, warning label, optional details label with its own stylesheet, separator line, instruction label, schema-name display label with its own stylesheet, QLineEdit with its own stylesheet and connected signal, and a button row with cancel/execute buttons (execute button conditionally styled).

findings.title: Unused import QFont

findings.title: Hardcoded hex color literals scattered outside the ENV_COLORS lookup table
findings.detail: Confirmed: details_label style (lines 124-129) hardcodes #f8f9fa/#dee2e6, and the QLineEdit style (lines 160-172) hardcodes #bdc3c7/#3498db, all separate from the centralized ENV_COLORS dict (lines 49-53).

findings.title: is_available()'s docstring claims the result is always True, contradicting its own except-branch
findings.detail: Confirmed lines 222-228: docstring says '(žĚėž°īžĄĪžúľŽ°ú Ūē≠žÉĀ True)' yet the method still has a live `except ImportError: return False` branch. Cross-checked pyproject.toml: `cryptography>=40.0.0,<49.0.0` is listed in the hard `dependencies` list (not optional-dependencies), confirming the docstring is actually correct and the except branch is genuinely dead/unreachable code in any properly-installed environment.

findings.title: AES block size 128 repeated as a bare literal in both padder and unpadder construction
findings.detail: Confirmed `PKCS7(128).unpadder()` (line 104) and `PKCS7(128).padder()` (line 148) both hardcode the AES block size in bits with no named constant explaining the relationship to the AES-128 cipher used elsewhere in the file.

findings.title: Build-time secret-embedding tooling (_obfuscate/_deobfuscate/generate_embedded_code) is bundled into the runtime auth class
findings.detail: Confirmed GitHubAppAuth (runtime JWT/installation-token logic) also carries _obfuscate (323-330), _deobfuscate (332-342), and generate_embedded_code (344-358) -- a code generator meant to be invoked once at build time to produce the `_EMBEDDED_*` class attributes baked into a release build.

findings.title: get_installation_token() reports failures via print() instead of the shared logger used elsewhere in the codebase
findings.detail: Confirmed line 248: `print(f"Installation Token ŽįúÍłČ žč§ŪĆ®: {e}")`. Grep confirms this is the only print()/logger occurrence in github_app_auth.py -- there is no `from src.core.logger import get_logger` anywhere in this file, unlike tunnel_engine.py (14 logger.* call sites confirmed) and other src/core modules which use the shared logger consistently.

findings.title: GitHubIssueReporter combines error-text sanitization/parsing, markdown report generation, similarity-based dedup, and a GitHub API client with retry logic
findings.detail: Confirmed class spans lines 23-494 (get_reporter_from_config at line 497 is a module-level function, outside the class). Four largely independent concerns confirmed present: text processing (_sanitize_error_message 125, _extract_core_error 158, _generate_fingerprint 231), markdown authoring (summarize_error 78, _generate_issue_body 192), similarity dedup (find_similar_issue 241), and a GitHub REST client with auth-refresh retry (create_issue 310, add_comment 363, report_error/_do_report 420/475, _refresh_headers_if_needed 64).
findings.recommendation: Split into an `ErrorSummaryBuilder` (pure functions: sanitize/extract_core_error/generate_issue_body/generate_fingerprint/summarize_error, no network dependency) and a `GitHubIssueClient` (find_similar_issue/create_issue/add_comment/report_error, taking a pre-built summary dict), composed by whatever currently instantiates GitHubIssueReporter.

findings.title: add_comment() re-derives the raw error text by string-splitting the rendered markdown body instead of reusing already-available data
findings.detail: Confirmed line 388: `summary.get('body', '').split('## žÉĀžĄł žė§Ž•ė Ž©ĒžčúžßÄ')[1].split('```')[1][:1000] if '## žÉĀžĄł žė§Ž•ė Ž©ĒžčúžßÄ' in summary.get('body', '') else summary.get('core_error', '')`. There IS a guard for the heading being entirely absent (falls back to core_error), but the code-fence split (`.split('```')[1]`) has no such guard -- if _generate_issue_body ever changes the wrapping around full_message (e.g. drops the code fence or changes its structure) while keeping the heading text, this line would raise an IndexError instead of gracefully degrading. This silently couples add_comment's correctness to the literal markdown layout emitted by _generate_issue_body.
findings.recommendation: Have summarize_error() also store the raw/sanitized full message in the returned dict (e.g. `summary['full_message'] = sanitized_message`), and have add_comment build its preview from `summary['full_message'][:1000]` directly, removing the fragile split-based re-parsing entirely.
findings.verification_note: Original claim slightly overstates the crash risk since a guard exists for the heading-absent case; the fence-based extraction remains fragile to format changes, which is the real, narrower risk. Recommendation is still directly actionable and correct.

findings.title: Several unrelated string-truncation lengths (80, 100, 1000, 2000) are scattered as bare literals
findings.detail: Confirmed: title truncation `core_error[:80]` (line 101), _generate_issue_body truncates full message to 2000 chars (line 204), add_comment truncates its preview to 1000 chars (line 388). The [:100] truncation actually appears in only TWO places, not three as originally stated: line 178 (`fk_error.group(1)[:100]`) and line 190 (`first_line[:100] ...`) -- line 188 is merely a Korean comment ('# ÍłįŽ≥ł: ž≤ę ž§Ą ŽėźŽäĒ 100žěź') describing the logic, not a third code occurrence of the literal.
findings.recommendation: Define named constants near the top of the class: `TITLE_PREVIEW_LEN = 80`, `CORE_ERROR_MAX_LEN = 100`, `BODY_PREVIEW_LEN = 2000`, `COMMENT_PREVIEW_LEN = 1000`, and reference them at lines 101, 178, 190, 204, and 388 respectively.
findings.verification_note: Corrected 'three places' to 'two places' for the [:100] literal (line 188 is a comment, not code) and widened line_end from 204 to 388 to include the add_comment truncation (line 388) that the recommendation itself references.

findings.title: i18n.py bundles three unrelated systems into one 1627-line module
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read. The module's docstring (line 1) claims it is a 'Small runtime i18n layer for user-facing app chrome', but it actually contains three independent, large subsystems: (1) a structured key->string lookup system (DEFAULT_LANGUAGE, SUPPORTED_LANGUAGES, _TRANSLATIONS, tr(), language_label, normalize_language, detect_system_language, configure_language, installer-hint helpers ‚ÄĒ lines 1-250), (2) a bespoke Korean->English machine-translation approximation engine built from four separate literal tables (_EN_TEXT_TRANSLATIONS at line 253 with ~122 entries, _EN_PHRASE_TRANSLATIONS at line 407 with ~771 entries, _EN_REGEX_TRANSLATIONS at line 1181 with ~52 regex pairs, _EN_WORD_TRANSLATIONS at line 1236 with ~107 single-word entries) plus translate_text() and Korean grammar-particle-stripping regexes (lines 253-1389), and (3) a global PyQt6 monkey-patching system (install_qt_i18n, patch_init/patch_method/patch_all_string_args_method helpers, plus 8 manually written patch blocks for QTreeWidget/QTableWidget/QToolTip/QMenu/QMessageBox/QFileDialog) that rewrites method behavior on roughly 26 distinct Qt widget/class targets at import time (lines 1392-1627). These three concerns have almost no shared code path and different failure modes (data-lookup bugs vs. regex/grammar bugs vs. Qt monkey-patch bugs), yet anyone touching any one of them has to load and reason about the entire 1627-line file.
findings.recommendation: Split into a package: src/core/i18n/keys.py (DEFAULT_LANGUAGE, SUPPORTED_LANGUAGES, _TRANSLATIONS, tr, normalize_language, detect_system_language, configure_language, installer_language_hint_path/read_installer_language_hint/consume_installer_language_hint/language_from_args), src/core/i18n/legacy_translate.py (the four _EN_*_TRANSLATIONS tables, _has_hangul, translate_text, _translate_sequence), and src/core/i18n/qt_hooks.py (install_qt_i18n and all patch_* helpers, including the 8 inline monkey-patch blocks). Keep src/core/i18n.py (or an __init__.py) as a thin re-export shim so existing `from src.core.i18n import tr` call sites keep working.
findings.verification_note: Structure and 3-way split claim confirmed by reading the full file. Corrected two factual inaccuracies from the original finding: (1) file is 1627 physical lines, not 1628 (confirmed via wc -l and direct read of the last line, 'return True'). (2) Entry-count estimates were substantially wrong and are corrected above: _EN_TEXT_TRANSLATIONS has ~122 entries (not ~500), _EN_PHRASE_TRANSLATIONS has ~771 entries (not ~250) ‚ÄĒ these two were effectively swapped/inflated in the original ‚ÄĒ _EN_REGEX_TRANSLATIONS has ~52 pairs (not ~30), _EN_WORD_TRANSLATIONS has ~107 entries (close to the original's ~100, no material change). Also corrected 'about ~15 Qt widget classes' to ~26 distinct classes/targets, counted directly from the QtGui/QtWidgets imports and patch_init/patch_method/patch_all_string_args_method/manual-block call sites in install_qt_i18n. None of these corrections change the core verdict ‚ÄĒ the god-file split recommendation is sound and actionable as originally proposed.

findings.title: Module docstring 'Small runtime i18n layer' no longer describes the file
findings.detail: Confirmed: line 1 reads `"""Small runtime i18n layer for user-facing app chrome."""`. The file has grown to 1627 lines (corrected from the original claim of 1628) including a full machine-translation substitution engine (4 lookup tables totaling roughly 1,050 entries ‚ÄĒ corrected from the original's ~850 estimate: 122 + 771 + 52 + 107 ‚Čą 1052) and a global Qt widget monkey-patching layer touching ~26 widget/class targets ‚ÄĒ nothing about that remaining description reads as 'small'.
findings.recommendation: Update the docstring to describe what's actually here (e.g. 'Runtime i18n: structured tr() keys, a legacy Korean-string auto-translation shim, and PyQt widget-text patching'), or better, let the split described in the god-file finding make each new file's docstring accurate on its own.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed line 1 text verbatim. Corrected the total-line-count claim (1627, not 1628) and the total-entry-count estimate (~1052, not ~850) to match the actual table sizes measured directly from the file. The core observation ‚ÄĒ that the docstring is stale ‚ÄĒ stands unchanged.

findings.title: Eight near-identical hand-rolled monkey-patch blocks in install_qt_i18n
findings.detail: Confirmed line-for-line. Beyond the patch_init/patch_method/patch_all_string_args_method helpers, install_qt_i18n contains 8 separate manually inlined blocks that each repeat the same scaffolding: save `original = Cls.method`, guard with `getattr(original, "_tf_i18n_wrapped", False)`, define a wrapper closure that translates specific argument positions, set `wrapped._tf_i18n_wrapped = True`, then reassign via attribute/setattr. Verified exact line ranges: QTreeWidget.setHeaderLabels (1517-1523), QTableWidget.setHorizontalHeaderLabels (1525-1531), QTableWidget.setVerticalHeaderLabels (1533-1539), QToolTip.showText (1541-1550), QMenu.addAction (1552-1563), QMenu.addMenu (1565-1576), the QMessageBox static-methods loop (1578-1598), and the QFileDialog static-methods loop (1604-1624). Any future widget/method needing the same treatment will likely be added by copy-pasting one of these blocks, and a bug fixed in one instance (e.g. the wrapped-guard check) would have to be manually re-applied to the other seven.
findings.recommendation: Extract a single generic helper, e.g. `_patch_free_function(container, name, positional_indices=(), kwarg_names=(), translate_all_args=False)`, that covers the wrapped-guard + closure + reassignment logic once, and have all 8 call sites (plus patch_method/patch_all_string_args_method) call it with different arguments instead of re-implementing the scaffolding.
findings.verification_note: Every line range in the original finding was checked against the actual file and matches exactly (1517-1523, 1525-1531, 1533-1539, 1541-1550, 1552-1563, 1565-1576, 1578-1598, 1604-1624). No corrections needed.

findings.title: translate_text() mixes dictionary lookups with generic Korean-grammar regex surgery in one function
findings.detail: Confirmed. translate_text() runs, in sequence: an exact-match dict lookup (1360-1361), a stripped-string dict lookup (1363-1365), a loop applying ~52 regex substitution pairs from _EN_REGEX_TRANSLATIONS (1368-1369, corrected from the original's ~30 estimate), a length-sorted substring-replace loop over ~771 phrase entries from _EN_PHRASE_TRANSLATIONS (1371-1372, corrected from the original's ~250 estimate), a length-sorted word-boundary regex loop over ~107 word entries from _EN_WORD_TRANSLATIONS (1374-1379), and finally three bespoke regexes that strip Korean grammatical particles (žĚÄ/ŽäĒ/žĚī/ÍįÄ/žĚĄ/Ž•ľ/...) and rewrite verb endings ('ŪēėžčúÍ≤†žäĶŽčąÍĻĆ' -> '?', 'ŪēėžĄłžöĒ' -> '.') based on the preceding character class (1381-1383). The first two steps are simple, predictable data lookups; the last step is effectively an ad-hoc Korean morphology engine operating on the output of the previous steps. All of this lives in one function with no named sub-steps.
findings.recommendation: Break the function into named steps that mirror the strategies: _lookup_exact(value), _apply_regex_pairs(value), _apply_phrase_substitutions(value), _apply_word_substitutions(value), _strip_korean_particles(value). Have translate_text() become a short pipeline that calls each in order and returns early on the first two. This makes each strategy independently testable and makes the particle-stripping regexes (the riskiest, most surprising part) visible as a distinct, nameable operation instead of being buried at the tail of a generic-sounding function.
findings.verification_note: All cited line numbers (1360-1361, 1363-1365, 1368-1369, 1371-1372, 1374-1379, 1381-1383) match the actual file exactly. Corrected the regex-pair count (~52, not ~30) and the phrase-entry count (~771, not ~250) based on direct counting of the underlying tables; the word-entry estimate (~107 vs. the original's ~100) needed no material correction. The structural observation and recommendation are unaffected by these count corrections.

findings.title: translate_text() re-sorts ~878 dictionary entries on every single call that reaches this code path
findings.detail: Confirmed. `sorted(_EN_PHRASE_TRANSLATIONS.items(), key=lambda item: len(item[0]), reverse=True)` (line 1371) and `sorted(_EN_WORD_TRANSLATIONS.items(), key=lambda item: len(item[0]), reverse=True)` (line 1374) recompute the same sort from scratch every time translate_text() runs past the two dict-lookup fast paths. Because install_qt_i18n patches setText/setWindowTitle/setToolTip/addTab/etc. across roughly 26 widget/class targets, translate_text() is on the hot path for a large share of the UI text the app renders when running in English mode with untranslated Korean source strings, so this sort of a combined ~878 items (771 phrase + 107 word entries ‚ÄĒ corrected from the original's ~350 estimate, which undercounted by roughly 2.5x) is redone far more often than the tables themselves ever change.
findings.recommendation: Precompute the sorted views once at module load time, e.g. `_SORTED_PHRASE_TRANSLATIONS = tuple(sorted(_EN_PHRASE_TRANSLATIONS.items(), key=lambda kv: len(kv[0]), reverse=True))` and the word-table equivalent, and iterate those precomputed tuples inside translate_text() instead of calling sorted() on the raw dicts each time.
findings.verification_note: Line references (1371, 1374) confirmed exact. Corrected the combined item-count estimate from ~350 to ~878 (771 phrase entries + 107 word entries, counted directly from the tables) ‚ÄĒ the original underestimated the actual cost of the repeated sort by more than 2x, making this finding somewhat more justified than originally stated, though still a modest (not severe) per-call cost in absolute terms for a table this size.

findings.title: Hangul Unicode block boundaries are unexplained literals duplicated in two places
findings.detail: _has_hangul (line 1348) hardcodes the Hangul syllable block boundary as `"ÍįÄ" <= char <= "Ūě£"` (the escape-sequence form of the characters ÍįÄ/Ūě£, not the literal characters themselves as the original finding stated), and the word-boundary regex built inside translate_text (line 1376, not scattered across 1347-1381 as originally described) duplicates the same boundary as `[ÍįÄ-Ūě£]` inside `rf"(?<![ÍįÄ-Ūě£]){{...}}(?![ÍįÄ-Ūě£])"`. Neither site has a comment explaining these are the first/last code points of the Hangul syllable block, and a reader unfamiliar with the exact code points has to look them up. If the range ever needed adjusting it would have to change in two places.
findings.recommendation: Introduce named constants, e.g. `_HANGUL_RANGE_START = "ÍįÄ"`, `_HANGUL_RANGE_END = "Ūě£"` (with a comment noting these bound the Hangul Syllables Unicode block), and build both _has_hangul's comparison and the regex's character class from those constants instead of repeating the raw escapes.
findings.verification_note: Underlying issue (duplicated, unexplained Hangul range boundary) confirmed real. Corrected two inaccuracies: (1) the code uses the Unicode escape literals `ÍįÄ`/`Ūě£`, not the literal Hangul characters "ÍįÄ"/"Ūě£" as the original finding stated (same code points, different source-text representation ‚ÄĒ matters for anyone trying to grep for the exact string). (2) The second occurrence is specifically at line 1376 (inside the word-boundary regex at 1374-1379), not spread across the full 1347-1381 range as originally cited; narrowed line_end from 1381 to 1379 to tightly bound both occurrences.

findings.title: Unreachable ERROR branch in filter_log_by_level
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read. filter_log_by_level's loop is: `if f'] {level_upper} [' in line: ... elif level_upper == 'ERROR' and '] ERROR [' in line: ...` (lines 171-174). When `level_upper == 'ERROR'`, the first `if` condition literally becomes `'] ERROR [' in line` ‚ÄĒ identical to the elif's condition. So the elif can only be reached when the if-condition is false, i.e. when `'] ERROR [' not in line`, which makes the elif's own `'] ERROR [' in line` check false too. The branch at lines 173-174 can never execute.
findings.recommendation: Delete the dead `elif level_upper == 'ERROR' and '] ERROR [' in line: filtered_lines.append(line)` branch (lines 173-174); the preceding generic `if` already handles the ERROR case correctly, matching the cascading pattern used for WARNING/INFO.
findings.verification_note: Read the exact function (lines 153-180). The dead-code logic and line numbers are exactly as described; no corrections needed.

findings.title: download_installer() hardcodes timeout=30 instead of using the class's configurable timeout
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read. UpdateDownloader exposes a `timeout` property (lines 108-113) that reads `self._config_manager.get_network_timeout_download()` when a config manager is supplied (method confirmed to exist in src/core/config_manager.py:565), falling back to `DEFAULT_TIMEOUT = 10`, and get_installer_info() correctly uses `self.timeout` (line 131). But download_installer(), in the same class, passes a bare literal `timeout=30` to `requests.get(...)` (line 204) for the actual file download, bypassing the configurable timeout entirely and with no comment explaining the discrepancy. A user who configures a shorter or longer network timeout via settings would see it applied to the metadata call but silently ignored for the (usually much longer) download call.
findings.recommendation: Either reuse `self.timeout` for the download request too, or ‚ÄĒ if a genuinely different timeout is intended for streaming downloads ‚ÄĒ add an explicit named class constant (e.g. `DOWNLOAD_CONNECT_TIMEOUT = 30`) with a comment explaining why it differs from the API timeout, instead of an unexplained bare literal.
findings.verification_note: Verified the timeout property (108-113), its correct use at line 131, and the hardcoded literal at line 204, plus confirmed get_network_timeout_download exists in config_manager.py. No corrections needed.

findings.title: Unused import: typing.Optional
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read of the full 71-line file. `from typing import Optional` is imported at line 13 but never referenced anywhere else ‚ÄĒ none of the three function signatures (`_get_migration_log_dir() -> str`, `create_oneclick_logger(schema: str) -> tuple`, `close_oneclick_logger(logger: logging.Logger) -> None`) use it.
findings.recommendation: Remove the unused `from typing import Optional` import.
findings.verification_note: Read the entire file (71 lines); confirmed Optional appears only in the import statement and nowhere else. No corrections needed.

findings.title: MigrationAnalyzer mixes FK analysis, 14 unrelated MySQL-8.4 compatibility checks, cleanup-SQL generation, and tree visualization
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read of lines 170-1277. The class owns: (1) FK metadata/orphan analysis (get_foreign_keys:201, build_fk_tree:239, find_orphan_records:262, get_fk_visualization:1239), (2) exactly 14 independent MySQL 8.0->8.4 compatibility scans (check_charset_issues:363, check_reserved_keywords:417, check_deprecated_in_routines:461, check_sql_modes:504, check_auth_plugins:832, check_zerofill_columns:885, check_float_precision:915, check_fk_name_length:947, check_invalid_date_values:978, check_int_display_width:1059, check_year2_type:1091, check_deprecated_engines:1124, check_enum_empty_value:1162, check_timestamp_range:1195), (3) cleanup SQL generation/execution (generate_cleanup_sql:536, execute_cleanup:585), and (4) ASCII FK-tree rendering (get_fk_visualization:1239). Four genuinely different reasons to change (new 8.4 rule, orphan-detection strategy, cleanup semantics, visualization formatting) all touch this single >1100-line class.
findings.recommendation: Split into collaborators composed by a thin MigrationAnalyzer facade: ForeignKeyAnalyzer (get_foreign_keys, build_fk_tree, find_orphan_records, get_fk_visualization), MySQLUpgradeCompatibilityChecker (all 14 check_* methods, ideally as a list of small Check objects/strategies so adding a rule doesn't touch the class), and OrphanCleanupPlanner (generate_cleanup_sql, execute_cleanup). analyze_schema becomes orchestration only.

findings.title: MySQL 8.4 reserved-keyword list is hand-duplicated instead of reusing the single source of truth
findings.detail: Confirmed: migration_analyzer.py:179-186 hardcodes NEW_RESERVED_KEYWORDS as a literal 26-item list (not 25 as originally stated -- counted precisely: 22 items matching RESERVED_KEYWORDS_80 plus 4 items matching NEW_RESERVED_KEYWORDS_84) that is byte-for-byte identical to migration_constants.py's RESERVED_KEYWORDS_80 (line 71-76) + NEW_RESERVED_KEYWORDS_84 (line 68), which migration_constants.py already exposes pre-combined as ALL_RESERVED_KEYWORDS (line 79), correctly imported and used by src/core/migration_rules/schema_rules.py:783. Instead, migration_analyzer.py keeps its own copy (used at line 422 in check_reserved_keywords), and migration_dump_analyzer.py does `from src.core.migration_analyzer import MigrationAnalyzer` inside `_analyze_sql_file` (line 140, function-scoped specifically to dodge a circular import since migration_analyzer.py imports DumpFileAnalyzer from migration_dump_analyzer.py at module scope at line 36) purely to read `MigrationAnalyzer.NEW_RESERVED_KEYWORDS` at line 258. Two parallel sources of truth for the same keyword list currently agree by coincidence, but nothing enforces that; this is a maintainability/DRY smell rather than an active bug today, so I've downgraded severity from the original 'high' to 'medium'.
findings.recommendation: Delete MigrationAnalyzer.NEW_RESERVED_KEYWORDS entirely; import and use ALL_RESERVED_KEYWORDS from migration_constants.py directly in both check_reserved_keywords (migration_analyzer.py:422) and _analyze_sql_file (migration_dump_analyzer.py:258), removing the local `from src.core.migration_analyzer import MigrationAnalyzer` import in migration_dump_analyzer.py:140.
findings.verification_note: Finding is real and the core claim (two parallel sources of truth) is exactly confirmed byte-for-byte. Corrected the item count from '25' to '26' (verified by manual enumeration) and downgraded severity from high to medium: the two lists are currently identical (no live divergence bug), so the impact today is a maintenance/architecture smell rather than a high-severity defect.

findings.title: Every MySQL-8.4 compatibility check repeats the same log/query/build-issues/log-summary boilerplate
findings.detail: Confirmed by full read of lines 885-1237: check_zerofill_columns, check_float_precision, check_fk_name_length, check_year2_type, check_deprecated_engines, check_enum_empty_value, check_timestamp_range and check_int_display_width all follow the identical shape: `self._log('ūüĒć ... ŪôēžĚł ž§Ď...')` -> one INFORMATION_SCHEMA query -> loop rows appending a CompatibilityIssue -> `if issues: self._log(f'  ‚ö†ÔłŹ ... {len(issues)}Íįú ŽįúÍ≤¨') else: self._log('  ‚úÖ ... žóÜžĚĆ')`. Only the query text, IssueType, severity, and message templates differ.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a small declarative spec per check, e.g. a dataclass CheckSpec(query, issue_type, severity, describe_fn, suggest_fn), and a single `_run_column_scan(schema, spec)` helper that all these methods call (or are replaced by), so a new 8.4 rule is one new spec entry instead of a new ~15-line method.

findings.title: analyze_schema / _analyze_schema_impl take 16 parameters, 15 of them boolean flags, duplicated across two signatures
findings.detail: Confirmed: analyze_schema(schema, check_orphans=True, check_charset=True, check_keywords=True, check_routines=True, check_sql_mode=True, check_auth_plugins=True, check_zerofill=True, check_float_precision=True, check_fk_name_length=True, check_invalid_dates=True, check_year2=True, check_deprecated_engines=True, check_enum_empty=True, check_timestamp_range=True, check_int_display_width=True) at lines 639-657 is boolean-flag-soup, and the exact same 15-flag list is re-declared verbatim in _analyze_schema_impl (lines 714-732) and passed through by keyword one by one (lines 693-710).
findings.recommendation: Bundle the flags into a single SchemaCheckOptions dataclass (or a Set[str]/FrozenSet of enabled check names defaulting to 'all') and change both signatures to analyze_schema(schema: str, options: SchemaCheckOptions = SchemaCheckOptions()), eliminating the 15-parameter pass-through duplication between the two methods.

findings.title: find_orphan_records duplicates the NOT-EXISTS-vs-LEFT-JOIN branch twice (count query and sample query)
findings.detail: Confirmed: the `if is_large: ... NOT EXISTS ... else: ... LEFT JOIN ...` branch used to build count_query (lines 290-311) is copy-pasted almost verbatim to build sample_query (lines 322-342), differing only in the SELECT clause/DISTINCT and an added LIMIT. The module's own comment on generate_cleanup_sql (lines 545-549) documents that this exact predicate previously had a NOT IN vs NOT EXISTS NULL-safety bug, confirming this duplication is a real, already-manifested drift risk, not just theoretical.
findings.recommendation: Extract a private helper, e.g. `_build_orphan_query(schema, fk, is_large, select_expr, limit=None)`, that returns the correct SQL text for either strategy, and call it once for the COUNT(*) form and once (with a LIMIT and DISTINCT column) for the sample form.

findings.title: execute_cleanup has a dead `if dry_run:` guard left over from the Rust-core migration, and the docstring never mentions the new raise
findings.detail: Confirmed: lines 600-604 do `if not dry_run: raise RuntimeError(...)`, immediately followed at line 606 by `if dry_run:` wrapping the rest of the function body. Since execution reaches line 606 only when dry_run is already guaranteed True, that check is dead/always-true. Confirmed via caller search (src/ui/dialogs/migration_dialogs.py:504, tests/test_migration_analyzer.py:606) that dry_run=False is still exercised deliberately as a fail-closed regression test/disabled UI path, so the outer raise is intentional and load-bearing -- only the inner `if dry_run:` wrapper is genuinely redundant. The docstring (lines 592-598) documents dry_run's meaning but never states dry_run=False always raises.
findings.recommendation: Remove the now-redundant `if dry_run:` wrapper and dedent its body directly after the `if not dry_run: raise` guard. Update the docstring to state that dry_run=False always raises RuntimeError because DB mutations are owned by Rust Core.
findings.verification_note: Downgraded severity from medium to low: this is purely a readability/redundant-branch issue with zero behavioral impact (confirmed the guard is otherwise correctly enforced and intentionally tested), not a maintenance-risk-bearing duplication like the other medium findings.

findings.title: Unused `Path` import and a redundant unused local `datetime` import
findings.detail: Confirmed via grep: `from pathlib import Path` (line 13) has zero other references anywhere in migration_analyzer.py. Separately, `analyze_schema` does `from datetime import datetime` at line 682 but that method's body (lines 682-712) only calls `self._analyze_schema_impl(...)` and never references `datetime` itself -- the actual usage (`datetime.now().isoformat()`, line 745) lives in `_analyze_schema_impl`, which has its own separate `from datetime import datetime` at line 734.
findings.recommendation: Delete the unused `from pathlib import Path` import at line 13, and delete the redundant `from datetime import datetime` at line 682 (keep only the one inside `_analyze_schema_impl` at line 734, or move it to module scope since it's used by the actual implementation).
findings.verification_note: Both sub-claims confirmed accurate. Note the line_start/line_end (13-682) is not a contiguous problem region -- it's two isolated single-line dead imports at 13 and 682; kept the range as given since it anchors both cited lines.

findings.title: Leftover section-header comment for a 'dump file analyzer' that no longer lives in this file
findings.detail: Confirmed: the file (1285 lines total) ends with get_fk_visualization closing at line 1277, then a section banner `# Žć§ŪĒĄ ŪĆĆžĚľ Ž∂ĄžĄĚÍłį (Task 3)` at lines 1280-1282 followed by nothing but blank lines through 1285. DumpFileAnalyzer was already extracted into migration_dump_analyzer.py and is merely imported at line 36, so this trailing comment describes code that isn't here.
findings.recommendation: Delete the stale section-header comment block at lines 1280-1285.

findings.title: Two different unexplained 'large table' thresholds (500000 vs 100000) control related decisions
findings.detail: Confirmed: `find_orphan_records(..., large_table_threshold: int = 500000, ...)` (line 266) uses 500,000 rows to pick the NOT EXISTS vs LEFT JOIN query strategy, while a separate inline literal `100000` at line 283 independently decides whether to log size info. A table between 100k-500k rows gets a 'size info' log line implying it's noteworthy, yet still uses the plain LEFT JOIN path. This doesn't produce incorrect query results (both paths are functionally correct), only a slightly confusing/unlinked pair of magic numbers.
findings.recommendation: Name both thresholds as constants in migration_constants.py (e.g. LARGE_TABLE_ROW_THRESHOLD = 500_000, SIZE_INFO_LOG_THRESHOLD = 100_000) or, simpler, reuse the same `large_table_threshold` value for both the strategy switch and the logging decision so they can't silently diverge.
findings.verification_note: Downgraded severity from medium to low: confirmed both literals exist as described, but since they only affect a diagnostic log line vs. a query-optimization switch (not correctness), the actual impact is cosmetic/readability rather than a functional risk.

findings.title: Hardcoded '[N/15]' step counter repeated literally 15 times
findings.detail: Confirmed: _analyze_schema_impl logs progress as `ūüďĆ [1/15] ...` (line 753) through `ūüďĆ [15/15] ...` (line 814), with both the step number and the total '15' typed out by hand at each of the 15 call sites (also each with a matching '‚úÖ [N/15]' completion line for some). Adding, removing, or reordering a check requires manually renumbering every one of these strings.
findings.recommendation: Iterate over a declarative list of (flag, log_message, check_fn) tuples with enumerate(...) so the step number and total are computed, not hand-maintained -- this pairs naturally with the check_* boilerplate refactor recommended above.

findings.title: _analyze_sql_file performs 8 unrelated compatibility scans inline in one ~155-line function
findings.detail: Confirmed by full read of lines 130-285: the single method sequentially runs ZEROFILL detection, FLOAT/DOUBLE precision detection, FK-name-length detection, auth-plugin detection (with its own severity branching), FTS_ table-prefix detection, SUPER-privilege detection, removed-system-variable detection, and reserved-keyword detection for both table names and column names -- 8 distinct checks, each as its own regex loop appending CompatibilityIssue objects, all inside one function body guarded by a single outer try/except.
findings.recommendation: Split into one small private helper per check (e.g. `_check_zerofill(content, file_name)`, `_check_auth_plugins(content, file_name)`, ...), each returning List[CompatibilityIssue], with `_analyze_sql_file` reduced to reading the file, calling each helper, and concatenating results.

findings.title: Two regex patterns are defined ad-hoc inline instead of following the codebase's convention of centralizing patterns in migration_constants.py
findings.detail: Confirmed: every other detection pattern used in this function (ZEROFILL_PATTERN, FLOAT_PRECISION_PATTERN, FK_NAME_LENGTH_PATTERN, AUTH_PLUGIN_PATTERN, FTS_TABLE_PREFIX_PATTERN, SUPER_PRIVILEGE_PATTERN, SYS_VAR_USAGE_PATTERN) is imported as a precompiled module-level constant from migration_constants.py (import block at lines 9-21). But `table_pattern` (line 249) and `column_pattern` (line 253) are compiled locally inside `_analyze_sql_file` on every call, breaking the established single-source-of-truth pattern used everywhere else in this file.
findings.recommendation: Move `table_pattern`/`column_pattern` into migration_constants.py as named precompiled constants (e.g. CREATE_TABLE_NAME_PATTERN, TYPED_COLUMN_NAME_PATTERN) alongside the other detection regexes, and import them the same way.

findings.title: A module documented as pure 'constants' contains two non-trivial parsing/matcher classes
findings.detail: Confirmed: migration_constants.py's module docstring (lines 1-6) describes it as 'MySQL 8.4 Upgrade Checker žÉĀžąė Ž™®Žďą' (a constants module) ported from mysql-upgrade-checker. It also defines `_IdentifierIssuePattern` (lines 433-485) and `_ContextualDotPattern` (lines 488-512) -- two classes implementing real algorithmic logic (masking string literals with `_mask_string_literals`, scanning DDL-context spans with `_iter_scan_spans`, offset-preserving regex re-matching via `finditer`/`search`). This mixes flat data (tuples/dicts/Enum) with stateful matcher behavior in the same module. Confirmed the module-level instances DOLLAR_SIGN_PATTERN (line 590), TRAILING_SPACE_PATTERN (593), CONTROL_CHAR_PATTERN (598) depend on _IdentifierIssuePattern, and INVALID_57_NAME_MULTIPLE_DOTS_PATTERN (668) depends on _ContextualDotPattern.
findings.recommendation: Move `_IdentifierIssuePattern` and `_ContextualDotPattern` (and the module-level instances DOLLAR_SIGN_PATTERN, TRAILING_SPACE_PATTERN, CONTROL_CHAR_PATTERN, INVALID_57_NAME_MULTIPLE_DOTS_PATTERN that depend on them) into a new module, e.g. migration_identifier_matchers.py, keeping migration_constants.py limited to the plain data its docstring promises.

findings.title: Type annotation uses builtin `any` instead of `typing.Any`
findings.detail: Confirmed: `STORAGE_ENGINE_STATUS: Dict[str, any] = {...}` at line 302 uses the builtin function `any` where `typing.Any` was clearly intended (values in the dict are a list, a string, and another list). Confirmed `Any` is not present in the module's typing import (`from typing import Dict, List, Optional, Tuple`, line 10), so this would fail a static type check if one were run over this module.
findings.recommendation: Add `Any` to the existing `from typing import Dict, List, Optional, Tuple` import (line 10) and change the annotation to `Dict[str, Any]`.

findings.title: extract_create_table_statements / extract_create_user_statements / extract_grant_statements are structurally identical
findings.detail: Confirmed by full read of lines 738-781: all three SQLParser methods follow the exact same shape: declare `statements = []`, `re.compile(...)` a pattern inline, `for match in pattern.finditer(content): statements.append(match.group(0))`, `return statements`. Only the regex literal differs between them, and each regex is recompiled from scratch on every call instead of being a precompiled constant.
findings.recommendation: Replace the three methods with a single private helper `_extract_statements(self, pattern: re.Pattern, content: str) -> List[str]`, and hoist the three regexes into precompiled class-level constants (e.g. `_CREATE_TABLE_STMT_PATTERN`, `_CREATE_USER_STMT_PATTERN`, `_GRANT_STMT_PATTERN`) so each public method becomes a one-line call.

findings.title: Single 1472-line file bundles five unrelated responsibilities
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read of the full file. Five independently substantial classes live in one module: SmartFixGenerator (lines 30-438, per-issue-type fix-option business rules), CollationFKGraphBuilder (441-633, generic BFS/topological-sort graph algorithm over FK relationships), FKSafeCharsetChanger (636-837 ‚ÄĒ note: the original finding said 636-796, which stops before execute_safe_charset_change; the class actually continues through line 837), BatchFixExecutor (840-1287, dry-run batch orchestration with its own clustering/merging), and CharsetFixPlanBuilder (1290-1438, a higher-level FK-graph planning facade). Each has its own lazy-init/caching pattern for a CollationFKGraphBuilder instance (see the related duplication finding), confirming they are only loosely coupled via that one shared dependency, not tightly integrated. This matches the shape of god-files the project has already split elsewhere (PRs #212-#215).
findings.recommendation: Split into: migration_fix_option_generator.py (SmartFixGenerator), migration_fk_graph.py (CollationFKGraphBuilder), migration_fk_safe_charset.py (FKSafeCharsetChanger), migration_batch_fix_executor.py (BatchFixExecutor), migration_charset_fix_plan.py (CharsetFixPlanBuilder). Keep migration_fix_wizard.py as a thin facade re-exporting create_wizard_steps() for backward compatibility, mirroring the split already done for migration_fix_models.py / migration_rollback_sql_generator.py.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate; only correction is the FKSafeCharsetChanger end line (837, not 796 ‚ÄĒ the original range omitted execute_safe_charset_change). Severity high is justified given five distinct, substantial, independently-changing concerns in one file.

findings.title: BatchFixExecutor.execute_batch is a ~254-line function doing four distinct jobs inline
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read: execute_batch (941-1194, exactly 254 lines) sequentially (a) FK-orders steps via _sort_steps_by_fk_order (982), (b) clusters and batch-processes COLLATION_FK_SAFE steps into per-schema/per-cluster frozensets with nested loops (993-1045), (c) groups and merges COLLATION_SINGLE column-level steps into per-table ALTER statements with its own dict grouping and 2-phase bookkeeping (1047-1116), (d) runs a third per-step loop handling SKIP/MANUAL/normal execution with independent logging/aggregation (1118-1184), and (e) assembles BatchExecutionResult (1186-1194). Each phase maintains independent local state (fk_safe_processed, merged_steps, results/success_count/fail_count/skip_count/total_affected) that must stay consistent across the whole function.
findings.recommendation: Extract three private helpers: _execute_fk_safe_clusters(steps) -> Tuple[List[FixExecutionResult], Set[int]], _execute_collation_single_merges(steps) -> Tuple[List[FixExecutionResult], Set[int]], and _execute_remaining_steps(steps, already_handled_ids) -> List[FixExecutionResult]. Have execute_batch call these in sequence, concatenate results, and compute aggregate counts/BatchExecutionResult from the combined list.
findings.verification_note: Line range and phase breakdown confirmed exactly against source. High severity is warranted given the length and the amount of independently-tracked local state.

findings.title: _execute_single is an intentional fail-closed guard, not vestigial dead code as originally claimed ‚ÄĒ but rollback_sql is genuinely a dead always-empty literal
findings.detail: The original finding's supporting evidence is wrong: it claims '_execute_single is never called anywhere in the codebase (verified via repo-wide grep for `_execute_single(` ‚ÄĒ only the definition matches)'. A repo-wide grep shows this is false ‚ÄĒ tests/test_migration_fix_wizard.py:1280 calls `executor._execute_single(...)` directly inside `test_private_single_execution_hook_is_fail_closed`, and docs/current_status.md:104-114 explicitly documents `BatchFixExecutor._execute_single` as an intentional second fail-closed gate ('is also fail-closed if called directly') alongside execute_batch's own dry_run=False guard. So this is deliberate defense-in-depth, verified by a dedicated regression test, not leftover vestigial code ‚ÄĒ deleting it as recommended would remove tested safety behavior and contradict documented design intent. Separately, the rollback_sql claim is accurate: `rollback_sql = ""` is declared at line 974 and never reassigned before being passed to `BatchExecutionResult(..., rollback_sql=rollback_sql)` at line 1193 ‚ÄĒ this part is a real, if very minor, dead-variable indirection.
findings.recommendation: Do NOT delete _execute_single ‚ÄĒ it is an intentional, tested fail-closed guard (see test_private_single_execution_hook_is_fail_closed and docs/current_status.md). If anything, its one-line docstring could be updated to say '(defense-in-depth: fails closed even if called directly, bypassing execute_batch's own guard)' so future readers don't mistake it for dead code. For rollback_sql, simply inline the literal `""` at the BatchExecutionResult(...) call site (line 1193) instead of carrying an always-empty local variable through the whole function, or drop the field until Rust Core actually starts returning rollback SQL.
findings.verification_note: Corrected: the _execute_single-is-dead-code claim is factually wrong (there is a direct test call) and its 'delete it' recommendation is actively contrary to documented intent. Severity downgraded from medium to low since the only remaining actionable item (the empty rollback_sql literal) is a trivial readability nit.

findings.title: Identical lazy FK-graph-builder init/cache logic repeated verbatim in four classes
findings.detail: Confirmed verbatim duplication of the 5-line pattern `if self._fk_graph_builder is None: self._fk_graph_builder = CollationFKGraphBuilder(self.connector, self.schema); self._fk_graph_builder.build_graph(); return self._fk_graph_builder` in: SmartFixGenerator.get_fk_graph_builder (44-49), FKSafeCharsetChanger._get_fk_graph_builder (655-660), BatchFixExecutor._get_fk_graph_builder (866-871), and CharsetFixPlanBuilder._get_fk_graph_builder (1321-1326). All four checked and match exactly.
findings.recommendation: Add a shared helper, e.g. a `CollationFKGraphBuilder.get_or_create(connector, schema, cache: dict)` classmethod or a module-level `get_or_build_fk_graph(cache_holder, connector, schema)` function, and have all four classes call it instead of reimplementing the lazy-init.
findings.verification_note: All four line ranges confirmed exact matches of the identical code pattern.

findings.title: Invalid-date WHERE clause duplicated verbatim three times in one function
findings.detail: Confirmed: `_get_invalid_date_options` (166-220) builds three FixOption.sql_template strings (184-188 DATE_TO_NULL, 197-201 DATE_TO_MIN, 210-214 DATE_TO_CUSTOM) each repeating the identical WHERE clause `WHERE `{column}` = '0000-00-00' OR `{column}` = '0000-00-00 00:00:00' OR (MONTH(`{column}`) = 0 OR DAY(`{column}`) = 0);` word for word; only the SET clause differs.
findings.recommendation: Factor the WHERE clause into a helper, e.g. `_invalid_date_where_clause(column: str) -> str`, and build each sql_template as f"UPDATE ... SET ... {_invalid_date_where_clause(column)}" so the condition only needs to change in one place.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed exact triplication at the cited lines.

findings.title: Table charset/collation lookup query duplicated near-identically in two files
findings.detail: Confirmed: `CharsetFixPlanBuilder._get_table_charset` (migration_fix_wizard.py:1328-1345) and `RollbackSQLGenerator.capture_table_charset` (migration_rollback_sql_generator.py:48-75) both run the same INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES + COLLATION_CHARACTER_SET_APPLICABILITY join with the same schema/table filter and identical fallback defaults ('utf8mb3' / 'utf8mb3_general_ci'), differing only in return shape (tuple vs. dict).
findings.recommendation: Extract one shared function, e.g. `get_table_charset(connector, schema, table) -> Tuple[str, str]` in migration_fix_models.py next to `_format_default_sql_clause`, and have both call sites use it (RollbackSQLGenerator can keep its own dict-shaped cache wrapper around the shared call).
findings.verification_note: Both queries read side by side and confirmed near-identical, including the fallback default literals.

findings.title: FixQueryGenerator duplicates SmartFixGenerator's fix logic for 7 overlapping IssueTypes, but it currently has zero production callers ‚ÄĒ the real user-facing duplication is between MigrationAnalyzer's own inline fix_query strings and SmartFixGenerator
findings.detail: The overlap claim is correct: FixQueryGenerator.generate() and SmartFixGenerator.get_fix_options() both cover CHARSET_ISSUE, ZEROFILL_USAGE, FLOAT_PRECISION, INVALID_DATE, DEPRECATED_ENGINE, ENUM_EMPTY_VALUE, and INT_DISPLAY_WIDTH with materially different SQL (e.g. _gen_invalid_date_fix at line 101 uses an unqualified table name and only matches '0000-00-00', while SmartFixGenerator._get_invalid_date_options is schema-qualified and covers three more patterns/strategies; _gen_charset_fix's column branch at line 80 emits only a non-executable comment while SmartFixGenerator's column branch emits a real executable ALTER). However, a repo-wide grep for `FixQueryGenerator` shows it is instantiated ONLY in tests/test_migration_fix_generator.py and tests/test_migration_mapping_coverage.py ‚ÄĒ there is no import or call site anywhere under src/, so the claim 'depending on which pipeline a UI surface calls... the user gets inconsistent guidance' does not currently hold; no UI surface calls FixQueryGenerator at all. Separately, a repo-wide grep for `.fix_query` shows the CompatibilityIssue.fix_query field is set directly (not via FixQueryGenerator) by src/core/migration_analyzer.py at 5 call sites (e.g. line 1041's own independent, THIRD implementation of the invalid-date fix SQL, which is schema-qualified but omits the '0000-00-00 00:00:00' / MONTH=0/DAY=0 patterns that both other implementations include), and that field is never read anywhere under src/ui/ ‚ÄĒ so even MigrationAnalyzer's fix_query values are not currently displayed to users either. The real, live duplication risk is therefore between MigrationAnalyzer's inline SQL construction and SmartFixGenerator's wizard options (both are exercised in production), not FixQueryGenerator, which appears to be orphaned/dead code kept alive only by its own test suite.
findings.recommendation: First confirm intent: if FixQueryGenerator has no planned caller, delete it as dead legacy code (consistent with this codebase's stated pattern of removing vestigial pre-Rust-Core Python logic, e.g. AutoRecommendationEngine per tests/test_migration_mapping_coverage.py's docstring) along with its two dedicated test files.
