If it is meant to eventually back a fix_query display surface, do not maintain a third copy ‚ÄĒ have MigrationAnalyzer's issue-detection methods (migration_analyzer.py lines 1041/1114/1152/1229) delegate to SmartFixGenerator(connector, schema).get_fix_options(issue) for the 7 overlapping IssueTypes instead of building their own inline SQL, and drop FixQueryGenerator's duplicate handlers for those 7 types.
findings.verification_note: Corrected: original 'high' severity was calibrated on a user-impact claim that repo-wide grep does not support (FixQueryGenerator has zero production callers; fix_query is never read in src/ui/). Downgraded to medium because the underlying triple-implementation duplication is still a genuine, real maintenance hazard, but it is not currently reaching end users as originally described, and the original finding missed the more important production-live duplication (MigrationAnalyzer vs SmartFixGenerator).

findings.title: Target charset/collation "utf8mb4"/"utf8mb4_unicode_ci" hardcoded as literals in 8+ places
findings.detail: Confirmed the literal pair is repeated at lines 238-239, 270, 284-286, 306-307, 735-736 (default params), 801-802 (default params), 1024-1025, and 1411-1412 (default params) in this file, plus lines 76 and 161 of migration_fix_generator.py.
findings.recommendation: Define DEFAULT_TARGET_CHARSET = "utf8mb4" and DEFAULT_TARGET_COLLATION = "utf8mb4_unicode_ci" once in migration_fix_models.py (no existing constant found there via grep) and import/reuse them everywhere instead of literals, including as default parameter values for generate_safe_charset_sql/execute_safe_charset_change/generate_fix_sql.
findings.verification_note: All cited line numbers confirmed against source; no pre-existing shared constant found.

findings.title: FixWizardStep.included_by is never assigned anywhere, making its skip-check permanently unreachable ‚ÄĒ but its would-be duplicate-rollback scenario is already covered by an independent table-level dedup mechanism
findings.detail: Confirmed: `generate_batch_rollback` skips any step where `step.included_by is not None` (372-373). A repo-wide grep for `included_by` confirms it is declared in migration_fix_models.py:85 with default None and is never assigned anywhere in src/ ‚ÄĒ only read here, and only referenced elsewhere in a test asserting it is always None (tests/test_migration_fix_wizard.py:1115). So this branch can never trigger. However, checking the surrounding code shows generate_batch_rollback already has an independent table-level dedup (`processed_tables` set, lines 381-397) for COLLATION_FK_CASCADE/COLLATION_FK_SAFE steps that prevents duplicate rollback SQL for overlapping FK-related table sets regardless of included_by ‚ÄĒ so in practice this dead branch has caused no observed duplicate-rollback defect; it is dead code with a misleading/aspirational comment rather than a source of incorrect output today.
findings.recommendation: Either wire up the field for real (have the FK-cascade auto-include path set `step.included_by = originating_table_name`) or remove the dead field from FixWizardStep and the corresponding dead check here, since the scenario it targets is already handled by the existing processed_tables dedup.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed dead via grep. Severity downgraded from medium to low because the existing processed_tables table-level dedup already prevents the duplicate-rollback scenario this field was meant to guard against, so current real-world impact is nil.

findings.title: BatchFixExecutor class docstring claims transactional execution and FK_CHECKS wrapping it no longer does
findings.detail: Confirmed the docstring says 'ŪäłŽěúžě≠žÖė ÍłįŽįėžúľŽ°ú žąėž†ē SQLžĚĄ žĚľÍīĄ žč§ŪĖČŪē©ŽčąŽč§' and lists 'FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS=0žúľŽ°ú ž†Ąž≤ī ÍįźžčłÍłį' as an improvement of this class. In reality execute_batch/_execute_single unconditionally raise RuntimeError for dry_run=False (963-967, 1198-1201) ‚ÄĒ there is no transactional execution path in this class, only dry-run estimation. The FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS=0 wrapping the docstring attributes to this class actually lives in SmartFixGenerator._get_charset_options' COLLATION_FK_CASCADE sql_template (lines 281, 287), a different class in the same file.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite the docstring to state this class only produces a dry-run/estimate preview (real execution is owned by Rust Core; dry_run=False raises), and remove the FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS bullet since that behavior belongs to SmartFixGenerator, not BatchFixExecutor.
findings.verification_note: Docstring text, the two RuntimeError raise sites, and the actual FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS location all confirmed exactly as described.

findings.title: Broad except-Exception blocks silently swallow both expected and unexpected failures
findings.detail: Confirmed: `_sort_steps_by_fk_order` (function starts at line 888, not 900 as originally stated) wraps the FK-graph build + grouping + topological sort in one `except Exception as e:` (937-939) that logs a generic message and silently falls back to original step order ‚ÄĒ a real bug (e.g. AttributeError from malformed step.location) is treated identically to an expected/benign failure. Separately confirmed: `_estimate_affected_rows` (1203-1287) has two more bare except blocks around session sql_mode save/restore ‚ÄĒ `except Exception: pass` at 1232-1233 for the get/set, and another `except Exception: pass` at 1245-1246 inside the finally block guarding `set_session_sql_mode(_saved_mode)` restoration. If the restore itself fails, that failure is swallowed with no signal, so subsequent dry-run COUNT queries could silently continue under an unexpectedly relaxed sql_mode.
findings.recommendation: Narrow the except clauses to specific expected failure types where possible, and at minimum use logging.exception (not just str(e)) so failures are diagnosable. For the sql_mode restore specifically, emit a warning log if restoration fails, since a silently-altered session mode can affect correctness of later dry-run estimates in the same connection.
findings.verification_note: Corrected line_start from 900 to 888 (the actual start of _sort_steps_by_fk_order); all except-block line numbers otherwise confirmed exact.

findings.title: render_result_report nests ~8-9 levels deep while rendering 5 unrelated report sections in one function
findings.detail: Confirmed this 107-line function (exact span 269-375) handles issues, mismatches, plan/DDL, direction readiness, and per-table migration-guide rendering inline. Counting indentation in the directions branch: if isinstance(directions, list) (318, level 1) -> for direction in directions (321, level 2) -> if isinstance(direction, dict) (322, level 3) -> if isinstance(guide, dict) (331, level 4) -> if isinstance(tables, list) (347, level 5) -> for table in tables (348, level 6) -> if isinstance(columns, list) (355, level 7) -> for column in columns (356, level 8) -> if isinstance(column, dict) (357, level 9). The sibling row_samples loop nests similarly. This matches the finding's claim precisely.
findings.recommendation: Extract one helper per report section (_render_issues, _render_mismatches, _render_plan, _render_direction_guide, _render_table_guide), each taking its own sub-payload and returning List[str], using guard clauses (if not isinstance(x, dict): continue) to keep each helper to 2-3 nesting levels; render_result_report then just calls them in sequence and concatenates.
findings.verification_note: Nesting depth independently recounted from indentation and confirmed to match the ~8-9 level claim; line range exact.

findings.title: Module docstring promises only wire-format isolation, but the file also owns executable discovery, disk persistence, and report rendering
findings.detail: Confirmed the module docstring (lines 3-4) states it 'keeps that wire format isolated from widgets and worker orchestration', yet the file also contains Rust-core executable discovery (db_core_executable, _db_core_frozen_candidate_dirs, _db_core_executable_names, project_root; 137-192), on-disk resume-state persistence (cross_engine_state_dir, save_resume_state, load_resume_state, app_data_dir; 141-235), and a full text report renderer (render_result_report, 269-375, 107 lines) ‚ÄĒ none of which is wire-format modeling.
findings.recommendation: Split per concern: keep enums/dataclasses/parse/build (DatabaseEngine, MigrationDirection, MigrationIssue, HelperEvent, parse_helper_event, build_helper_request) in this file; move executable discovery to cross_engine_executable.py; move resume-state persistence to cross_engine_state.py; move render_result_report into src/core/migration_report_renderer.py, which already exists in this repo as the connector-free renderer for MigrationReport.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed all cited line ranges and that migration_report_renderer.py already exists (via Glob), making the consolidation target concrete and immediately actionable.

findings.title: Local variable `issues` is reused for two semantically different lists in the same function
findings.detail: Confirmed: in render_result_report, `issues = payload.get("issues")` (285) refers to the top-level payload's issue list. Later, inside the directions loop, `issues = direction.get("issues")` (324) shadows it with a per-direction list used only for issue_count. Checked whether this causes a live bug: no code after the directions loop references the outer `issues` again, so there is currently no active defect ‚ÄĒ only a latent trap for future edits.
findings.recommendation: Rename the inner variable to `direction_issues` (and `issue_count = len(direction_issues) if isinstance(direction_issues, list) else 0`) so it can never be confused with the outer `issues`.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed the shadowing exists exactly as described. Severity downgraded from medium to low because no current code path is actually affected by the shadowing ‚ÄĒ it is a latent/future risk, not a live bug.

findings.title: Mismatch-list truncation limit "50" hardcoded twice
findings.detail: Confirmed `mismatches[:50]` (300) and `len(mismatches) > 50` (306) both hardcode the same display-truncation threshold as a bare literal.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a module-level constant, e.g. MAX_MISMATCHES_DISPLAYED = 50, and use it in both places.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed exact duplication at cited lines.

findings.title: Redundant local `import re` shadowing the existing module-level import
findings.detail: Confirmed `_gen_removed_sysvar_fix` does `import re` locally at line 182 even though `re` is already imported at module scope (line 7) and used elsewhere in the same file (e.g. line 62's `_gen_auth_plugin_fix`) without a local re-import.
findings.recommendation: Remove the local `import re` at line 182; the module-level import already covers this function.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed exact ‚ÄĒ harmless but confusing local shadow-import.

findings.title: generate_rollback_sql mixes 4 unrelated rollback strategies in one ~146-line function
findings.detail: Confirmed the function (exact span 180-325) handles: date-rollback as a no-op explanation (216-221), single-table/column collation rollback with two different code-definition builders (224-263), and FK-cascade/FK-safe multi-table rollback with its own 3-phase (DROP FK / restore charset / re-ADD FK) SQL assembly and pre-state lookup fallback chain (265-323) ‚ÄĒ all as one if/elif chain sharing local `lines` list mutation.
findings.recommendation: Extract one private helper per strategy: _rollback_date(step), _rollback_collation_single(step, original_state), _rollback_collation_fk(step, original_state, all_pre_states), each returning its own SQL string; have generate_rollback_sql become a short dispatcher on strategy plus the shared location-parsing preamble.
findings.verification_note: Line range and phase boundaries confirmed exactly against source.

findings.title: INVALID_ENGINE_FK_PATTERN imported but never used; check_invalid_engine_fk reimplements its own inline regex instead
findings.detail: Verified by reading storage_rules.py in full and grepping the whole src/core tree for INVALID_ENGINE_FK_PATTERN. The import at lines 17-23 (`from ..migration_constants import ... INVALID_ENGINE_FK_PATTERN`) is confirmed dead ‚ÄĒ the only other occurrence of the name in the entire src/core tree is its definition in migration_constants.py:652-656. check_invalid_engine_fk (lines 196-223) instead builds its own local `create_table_pattern` at lines 202-205: `r'CREATE\s+TABLE\s+`?(\w+)`?\s*\([^;]+?\)\s*[^;]*ENGINE\s*=\s*(MyISAM|MEMORY|ARCHIVE|CSV)[^;]*;'`. Comparing against the actual constant (migration_constants.py:652-656: `r'CREATE\s+TABLE\s+[^;]+?FOREIGN\s+KEY[^;]+?ENGINE\s*=\s*(...)\b|CREATE\s+TABLE\s+[^;]+?ENGINE\s*=\s*(...)\b[^;]+?FOREIGN\s+KEY'`), the two patterns are not drop-in equivalents: the local version captures the table name as its own group (used to build `table_name` for the issue), while the shared constant does not capture a table name at all and instead pre-filters for FOREIGN KEY inside its own alternation. So this is real duplicate-logic-with-drift-risk, but downgraded from high to medium because (a) it is currently a dead import with no functional bug today, and (b) a literal 'just use the constant' swap is not fully mechanical since the constant lacks the table-name capture group the call site needs.
findings.recommendation: Either delete the unused `INVALID_ENGINE_FK_PATTERN` import, or extend `INVALID_ENGINE_FK_PATTERN` in migration_constants.py to also capture the table name (e.g. add a named group `(?P<table>\w+)` after `CREATE\s+TABLE\s+`?`) so `check_invalid_engine_fk` can adopt the shared constant directly via `match.group('table')`, eliminating the private duplicate and making this the single source of truth as the file's existing `_engine_policy` docstring already promises for `ENGINE_POLICIES`.
findings.verification_note: Finding is accurate (import confirmed unused via grep; two patterns confirmed non-identical). Severity corrected from high to medium: this is a latent duplication/drift risk with no currently observable bug, not an active correctness defect.

findings.title: Hand-rolled SQL tokenizer (quote/paren state machine) embedded as private methods of DataIntegrityRules
findings.detail: Confirmed by reading lines 166-342 in full: `_find_statement_end` (166-213), `_iter_create_table_statements` (215-227), `_iter_values_rows` (229-291), and `_split_sql_values` (292-342) form a quote-and-paren-aware SQL tokenizer tracking `in_single`/`in_double`/`in_backtick` state, backslash escapes, doubled-quote escapes, and nesting `depth`. Grep across src confirms all four are private to `DataIntegrityRules` and used only within this same class (specifically by `check_enum_numeric_index`, lines 344-414). The file already imports `CreateTableParser` from `..migration_parsers` (line 36) for table-shape parsing, and `migration_parsers.py` separately contains a much simpler, non-quote-aware `SQLParser.extract_create_table_statements` (lines 738-753) that a maintainer could easily mistake for equivalent ‚ÄĒ reinforcing that this quote-aware tokenizer is genuine reusable infrastructure that is currently locked inside one rule class.
findings.recommendation: Move `_find_statement_end`, `_iter_create_table_statements`, `_iter_values_rows`, and `_split_sql_values` out of `DataIntegrityRules` into `migration_parsers.py` as module-level functions or a small `SqlStatementScanner` class, placed near `CreateTableParser` since `_iter_create_table_statements` already delegates parsing to it. `DataIntegrityRules.check_enum_numeric_index` should then import and call the shared tokenizer instead of owning it.
findings.verification_note: Finding is accurate. Severity corrected from high to medium: this is a real reuse/architecture smell (confirmed by the existing naive duplicate in migration_parsers.py), but it does not currently produce incorrect behavior ‚ÄĒ it is a maintainability concern, not a live bug.

findings.title: Module docstring documents D12/D13 (FK 2-Pass checks) that have no implementation anywhere in this file
findings.detail: Confirmed by reading the full docstring (lines 1-19, listing '13Íįú Í∑úžĻô ÍĶ¨ŪėĄ' with D01-D13) and grepping the entire file for FK/foreign-key-related terms ‚ÄĒ the only hits are the two docstring lines themselves (17-18). Cross-checked all `def check_*` methods (61, 104, 127, 344, 419, 460, 542, 600, 654, 726, 826, 948) and the three `check_all_*` dispatch methods (1017, 1030, 1038): only D01-D11 are wired up. There is no FK-uniqueness or FK-target-existence logic anywhere in this file or its call graph.
findings.recommendation: Remove D12 and D13 from the docstring and correct '13Íįú Í∑úžĻô ÍĶ¨ŪėĄ' to '11Íįú Í∑úžĻô ÍĶ¨ŪėĄ (D01-D11)' until FK 2-pass validation is actually implemented (either in this file or in a dedicated module, e.g. a new `fk_rules.py`, whose location should then be cross-referenced from this docstring once it exists).
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate as described. Kept at high severity: this is a DB-migration-safety tool, and a false claim of FK-reference coverage could lead a user to believe referential-integrity risks are already screened for an 8.0->8.4 upgrade when they are not.

findings.title: Module docstring claims '36Íįú Í∑úžĻô ÍĶ¨ŪėĄ' including S19-S22, none of which exist in this file
findings.detail: Confirmed by reading the full docstring (lines 1-28) and grepping the entire 850-line file (case-insensitive) for TRIGGER/EVENT/SPATIAL/JSON_TABLE ‚ÄĒ the only matches are the docstring lines themselves (17-19). Cross-checked all 22 `def check_*` methods: they jump directly from `check_blob_text_default` (S18, line 517) to `check_mysql_schema_conflict` (S23, line 540) with nothing in between implementing S19-S22.
findings.recommendation: Remove S19-S20 (trigger/event syntax), S21 (spatial type changes), and S22 (JSON_TABLE syntax) from the docstring, and correct the '36Íįú Í∑úžĻô ÍĶ¨ŪėĄ' count to reflect what is actually implemented (S01-S09, S16-S18, S23-S31 ‚ÄĒ 22 checks), or implement the missing S19-S22 rules and wire them into `check_all_live_db`/`check_all_sql_content` if trigger/event/spatial/JSON_TABLE coverage is actually required for this migration path.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate. Kept at high severity for the same reason as the D12/D13 finding: overclaiming compatibility-check coverage in a migration-safety tool risks a user shipping an 8.0->8.4 upgrade with undetected trigger/event/spatial/JSON_TABLE incompatibilities.

findings.title: check_latin1_non_ascii and check_zerofill_data_dependency duplicate the same batch-scan/groupby/partial-scan skeleton almost line-for-line
findings.detail: Verified by reading both methods in full (726-821 and 826-943). Confirmed identical structure: INFORMATION_SCHEMA query -> early-return with '‚úÖ žóÜžĚĆ' log if empty -> `_MAX_COLUMNS_TO_CHECK` partial-scan cap with matching warning log format -> a local `from itertools import groupby` re-imported identically at both line 759 and line 873 (module-level imports at the top of the file do not include it) -> an identical local helper `def _table_key(col): return col['TABLE_NAME']` at both 761-762 and 875-876 -> per-table batch query in try/except with matching log-and-skip on failure -> identical `severity="info"` partial-scan disclosure issue appended at the end (804-814 and 925-936). Only the per-column SQL fragment (REGEXP vs LENGTH/CAST width check) and issue text actually differ.
findings.recommendation: Extract the shared skeleton into a private helper `_batch_scan_columns(self, schema, columns, *, build_query, issue_type, severity, describe)` that owns the partial-scan cap, `groupby`/`_table_key` grouping, per-table query execution/exception handling, and the partial-scan info issue. Both `check_latin1_non_ascii` and `check_zerofill_data_dependency` would then only supply their column-selection query and a small per-column SQL-fragment/issue-description callback.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate exactly as described, including the two independent `groupby` imports and duplicated `_table_key` helper.

findings.title: Four file-scanning rule checks (D06/D07/D08/D11) duplicate the same read-loop/truncation/magic-number scaffolding
findings.detail: Verified by reading all four methods in full: check_4byte_utf8_in_data (542-595), check_null_byte_in_data (600-649), check_timestamp_range (654-714), and check_invalid_datetime (948-1012). Confirmed each independently re-declares `max_lines = 10000` / `max_samples = 3` as local variables (unlike `_MAX_COLUMNS_TO_CHECK`/`_MAX_ZEROFILL_ROWS_TO_SCAN`, which are class constants at lines 721 and 724), each flips a `truncated` flag once `line_num > max_lines`, each appends a near-identical `SCAN_TRUNCATED` info issue with the same 'žä§žļĒžĚī {max_lines}ŪĖČžóźžĄú ž§ĎŽč®Žź®' wording, and each wraps the loop in `try/except Exception as e: self._log(f"  ‚ö†ÔłŹ ŪĆĆžĚľ žĚĹÍłį žė§Ž•ė: {file_path.name} - {str(e)}")`. Only the per-line detection logic differs.
findings.recommendation: Promote `max_lines`/`max_samples` to class constants (e.g. `_MAX_SCAN_LINES = 10000`, `_MAX_SAMPLE_VALUES = 3`) matching the existing `_MAX_COLUMNS_TO_CHECK` convention, and extract a shared `_scan_file_lines(self, file_path, mode, per_line_check)` template method owning the loop/truncation/exception/SCAN_TRUNCATED-issue boilerplate. Each of the four `check_*` methods then supplies only its line-matching predicate and issue-construction callback.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate as described.

findings.title: check_invalid_datetime silently swallows file-read errors while its three sibling scan functions surface them as an issue
findings.detail: Verified by reading check_invalid_datetime's except block (1009-1011: `except Exception as e: self._log(...); ` followed by bare `return issues` at 1012 with nothing appended) versus its three siblings' except blocks: check_4byte_utf8_in_data (585-593), check_null_byte_in_data (639-647), and check_timestamp_range (704-712), all of which append a `severity="info"` `CompatibilityIssue` describing the scan failure. This means a file-read failure in D11 (invalid datetime scan) is indistinguishable from 'file was clean', whereas the same failure in D06/D07/D08 is visibly reported.
findings.recommendation: Add an info-severity `CompatibilityIssue` (using `issue_type=IssueType.INVALID_DATE`, matching the pattern used in the other three functions) to check_invalid_datetime's except block, e.g. `description=f"DATETIME žä§žļĒ ŽĮłžôĄŽ£Ć: {str(e)[:80]}"`, so all four D06/D07/D08/D11 file scans consistently surface read failures.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate exactly as described.

findings.title: SchemaRules bundles ~29 methods / 850 lines spanning at least five unrelated rule families
findings.detail: Verified: `wc -l` confirms schema_rules.py is 850 lines (not 851 as originally stated ‚ÄĒ corrected). Grep for method definitions confirms 29 methods inside the class, covering: identifier-naming rules (dollar-sign S07, trailing-space S08, control-char S09, empty-dot-syntax S27, consecutive-dots S31, reserved-keyword routine names S30 at line 777), charset/index-sizing rules (S02 latin1 at 141, `calculate_column_byte_size` at 199, S03-S04 at 249), object/security rules (S23 mysql-schema conflict at 540, S24-S25 definer validation with its own `_fetch_existing_definers_or_issue` helper at 571), and deprecated-syntax rules (S05/S06, S16 generated-column functions with its own `_matches_sql_function_call` matcher at 421, S17 geometry, S18 blob/text default, S28 row format, S29 temporal delimiter). Each family is internally cohesive, but a change to definer-validation logic and a change to index-byte-size math are unrelated changes landing in the same class/file.
findings.recommendation: Split `SchemaRules` into cohesive sub-modules under `migration_rules/`, e.g. `identifier_rules.py` (S07-S09, S27, S30, S31), `index_charset_rules.py` (S02-S04, including `calculate_column_byte_size`), `definer_rules.py` (S23-S25, including `_fetch_existing_definers_or_issue`), and `syntax_rules.py` (S05, S06, S16-S18, S28, S29, including `_matches_sql_function_call`), each exposing its own `check_all_live_db`/`check_all_sql_content`, composed by a thin facade or directly by the caller ‚ÄĒ mirroring how `migration_rules/__init__.py` already composes `StorageRules`, `SchemaRules`, `DataIntegrityRules`.
findings.verification_note: Finding is accurate; corrected line_end from 851 to 850 (actual file length per `wc -l`) and corrected method count from '~26' to the actual grep count of 29 top-level methods in the class.

findings.title: Identical 3-line 'extract source line around match' snippet copy-pasted 7 times
findings.detail: Verified all 7 occurrences by direct line reads: check_year2_in_sql (123-125), check_groupby_asc_desc (318-320), check_blob_text_default (522-524), check_partition_prefix_key (689-691), check_empty_dot_table_syntax (712-714), check_innodb_row_format (736-738), and check_deprecated_temporal_delimiter (759-761) ‚ÄĒ each contains the exact same three lines: `line_start = content.rfind('\n', 0, match.start()) + 1`, `line_end = content.find('\n', match.end())`, `line = content[line_start:line_end].strip()`.
findings.recommendation: Extract a helper `_extract_source_line(content: str, match: re.Match) -> str` defined once on `SchemaRules` (or as a shared module-level function if `DataIntegrityRules`/`StorageRules` need it too), and have all 7 call sites use it instead of re-deriving line boundaries inline.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate at all 7 cited line ranges, exact text match.

findings.title: Identical progress-logging boilerplate and 'summary log' pattern duplicated verbatim across all three rule classes
findings.detail: Verified `__init__`, `set_progress_callback`, and `_log` are byte-for-byte identical across SchemaRules (schema_rules.py:64-75), StorageRules (storage_rules.py:32-43), and DataIntegrityRules (data_rules.py:45-56) ‚ÄĒ same signatures, docstrings, and bodies. Spot-checked several of the cited 'summary log' tail-pattern occurrences (`if issues: self._log(warn) else: self._log(success)`) and confirmed exact matches at schema_rules.py:108-111, 632-635, and data_rules.py structure is consistent with the same pattern seen throughout both files during this review.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a shared base class (e.g. `ProgressLoggingRuleBase` in a new `migration_rules/_base.py`) providing `__init__`, `set_progress_callback`, `_log`, and a `_log_summary(issues, item_label)` helper encapsulating the 'if issues: warn else: success' pattern. Have `SchemaRules`, `StorageRules`, and `DataIntegrityRules` inherit from it, replacing ~14 duplicated 3-4 line blocks with single calls.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate for the __init__/set_progress_callback/_log triplet across all three files, and spot-checks of the summary-log duplication claim were consistent.

findings.title: Rule ID 'S16' is documented for two different, unrelated checks in two different modules
findings.detail: Confirmed: schema_rules.py:14 docstring lists 'S16: žÉĚžĄĪ žĽ¨Žüľ Ūē®žąė' (implemented by check_generated_column_functions, line 437), while storage_rules.py:11 docstring lists 'S16: ŽĻĄInnoDB žóĒžßĄžóź FK žā¨žö© (žĚīžäą #63)' (implemented by check_invalid_engine_fk, line 196). Both docstrings independently assign the same catalogue number to unrelated checks.
findings.recommendation: Renumber the non-InnoDB-engine-FK check (storage_rules.py, introduced later per the 'žĚīžäą #63' annotation) to a distinct unused ID such as S32, keeping S16 for the original generated-column-function rule which was documented first in the S01-S31 schema catalogue.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate ‚ÄĒ both docstring lines verified to independently claim S16.

findings.title: check_enum_numeric_index nests 5 levels deep with flag-based loop breaking
findings.detail: Verified by reading the full method (344-414). Confirmed nesting: `for insert_match in ...finditer` (372) -> `for row_body in self._iter_values_rows(...)` (390) -> `for idx in enum_col_indices` (392) -> `if idx < len(values)` (393) -> `if val.isdigit() and int(val) > 0` (396) ‚ÄĒ 5 levels deep. Confirmed the `found_in_current_insert` boolean flag (389) plus two separate `break` statements (410, 412) used purely to escape both the row-loop and column-loop once one numeric-index hit is found for the current INSERT statement.
findings.recommendation: Extract the innermost row/column scanning into a helper, e.g. `_find_numeric_enum_value(self, values: list, enum_col_indices: list, cols: list) -> Optional[Tuple[str, str]]` returning the first `(column_name, value)` hit or `None`. Call it from a flat `for row_body in self._iter_values_rows(values_segment): result = self._find_numeric_enum_value(self._split_sql_values(row_body), enum_col_indices, cols); if result: ...; break`, removing the boolean flag and dropping nesting from 5 levels to 2-3.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate exactly as described.

findings.title: Unused import: `from dataclasses import dataclass`
findings.detail: Confirmed via grep across the whole file: the only occurrence of `dataclass` in data_rules.py is the import statement at line 22 itself. No `@dataclass` decorator or dataclass definition appears anywhere in the file.
findings.recommendation: Remove the unused `from dataclasses import dataclass` import at line 22.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate.

findings.title: Hardcoded '16' byte estimate for DECIMAL columns lacks a named constant
findings.detail: Confirmed at lines 226-229 in `calculate_column_byte_size`: `if data_type == 'decimal': # ÍįĄŽč®Ūěą žĶúŽĆÄÍįí ž∂Ēž†ē (simple max-value estimate) return 16`. Contrasted with the adjacent `numeric_sizes` dict (218-222) and `datetime_sizes` dict (232-235), which are clearly-named lookup tables ‚ÄĒ the DECIMAL branch is a bare literal with no named constant and no explanation of where 16 comes from.
findings.recommendation: Promote this to a named constant near the other size tables, e.g. `_DECIMAL_ESTIMATED_MAX_BYTES = 16`, with a short comment referencing MySQL's DECIMAL storage-byte formula (max precision 65 digits -> up to 30 bytes, but commonly approximated at 16 for typical precision/scale) so the estimate is discoverable and explainable.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate.

findings.title: _compare_indexes and _compare_foreign_keys implement the same ~100-line algorithm twice
findings.detail: Confirmed by reading lines 202-403 in full. SchemaComparator._compare_indexes (202-300) and _compare_foreign_keys (302-403) both: (1) build lower-cased name->object maps, (2) match by exact name and diff attributes into MODIFIED/UNCHANGED, (3) index unmatched-by-name items by a content key (_index_content_key at 187-189 / _fk_content_key at 192-200) to detect RENAMED entries via identical 'first unclaimed candidate wins' logic, (4) fall back to ADDED for unmatched source and REMOVED for unmatched target, with identical sorted() iteration order throughout. Only the field names diffed (columns/unique vs ref_table/columns/on_delete/on_update) and the Korean message text differ.
findings.recommendation: Extract a generic private helper `_compare_named_entities(source_map, target_map, content_key_fn, diff_fields_fn, diff_ctor)` in SchemaComparator that runs the 3-stage match/rename/added-removed algorithm once. `diff_fields_fn(src, tgt) -> List[str]` supplies the entity-specific difference messages; `diff_ctor` builds the IndexDiff/ForeignKeyDiff. _compare_indexes and _compare_foreign_keys become ~15-line wrappers passing IndexInfo/ForeignKeyInfo-specific closures.

findings.title: generate_sync_script is a 160-line, 5-phase god-method with 4-level nesting
findings.detail: Confirmed by reading lines 12-173 in full. SyncScriptGenerator.generate_sync_script inlines five phases: FK drops (33-61), table drops (63-72), table creates (74-85), column/index ALTERs (87-144, nesting `for diff -> if MODIFIED -> for col_diff/idx_diff -> if/elif diff_type` 4 levels deep), and FK adds (146-167). Each phase builds its own local list (fk_drops, table_drops, table_creates, alter_statements, fk_adds) before being joined into `lines` at the end.
findings.recommendation: Split into `_generate_fk_drops(diffs, target_schema)`, `_generate_table_drops(diffs, target_schema)`, `_generate_table_creates(diffs, target_schema)`, `_generate_alter_statements(diffs, target_schema)`, `_generate_fk_adds(diffs, target_schema)`, each returning a List[str] of SQL lines. `generate_sync_script` becomes an orchestrator calling each phase and joining results with section headers, reducing nesting to 2 levels per phase.

findings.title: Severity classification silently depends on hard-coded diff-message prefixes defined in a different file
findings.detail: Confirmed by reading both files. `_classify_column` (88-100) matches `d.startswith("ŪÉÄžěÖ:")` / `"Nullable:"` / `"Default:"` / `"Extra:"` / `"Charset:"`/`"Collation:"`, and `_classify_extra_change` (140-145) checks `'auto_increment' in diff_text.lower()`. These exact literals are produced only in schema_comparator.py's `_compare_columns`: `f"ŪÉÄžěÖ: {src.data_type} ‚Üí {tgt.data_type}"` (line 143), `f"Nullable: ..."` (150), `f"Default: ..."` (153), `f"Extra: ..."` (158), `f"Charset: ..."` (163), `f"Collation: ..."` (166) ‚ÄĒ verified verbatim match. No shared enum/constant ties the two files; if the producer text changes, `_classify_column` falls through to `else: sev = DiffSeverity.WARNING` (99-100) with no error, silently downgrading what should be a CRITICAL type/auto_increment change in a schema-migration tool where severity drives go/no-go decisions.
findings.recommendation: Add a `field` (e.g. enum `DiffField.TYPE/NULLABLE/DEFAULT/EXTRA/CHARSET/COLLATION`) to each difference entry ‚ÄĒ either promote ColumnDiff.differences from List[str] to List[a small dataclass with field+text], or add a parallel `difference_fields: List[DiffField]` ‚ÄĒ set by schema_comparator.py's _compare_columns when it builds each difference, and have `_classify_column` switch on the structured field instead of parsing text. Keep the Korean/English string only for display.

findings.title: rust_dump_exporter.py bundles 6 unrelated responsibilities in one 922-line module
findings.detail: Confirmed by reading the full 922-line file. Contents: (1) path-safety helpers `_safe_dump_child_dir`/`_safe_dump_child_file` (23-49); (2) Rust-core install checking `RustDumpChecker` (93-127); (3) a direct-SQL orphan-record analysis subsystem `OrphanRecordInfo`/`ForeignKeyResolver` (130-324) using `MySQLConnector` directly, unrelated to Rust dump/import; (4) dump/import orchestration `RustDumpExporter`/`RustDumpImporter` (327-725); (5) UI progress-metadata helper `TableProgressTracker` (728-761); (6) event dispatcher `emit_core_event` plus 4 legacy module-level functions `check_rust_dump`/`export_schema`/`export_tables`/`import_dump` (764-921). File length confirmed at exactly 922 lines.
findings.recommendation: Split into: `src/core/foreign_key_resolver.py` (move OrphanRecordInfo + ForeignKeyResolver ‚ÄĒ direct-SQL analysis unrelated to Rust dump), `src/exporters/dump_progress.py` (move TableProgressTracker + emit_core_event), and keep `rust_dump_exporter.py` focused on RustDumpConfig/RustDumpChecker/RustDumpExporter/RustDumpImporter plus the path-safety helpers they use directly.

findings.title: RustDumpExporter and RustDumpImporter duplicate __init__ and _endpoint verbatim
findings.detail: Confirmed: RustDumpExporter.__init__/_endpoint (330-343) and RustDumpImporter.__init__/_endpoint (571-584) are byte-for-byte identical ‚ÄĒ `self.config = config; self.facade = facade if facade is not None else DbCoreFacade(); self._owns_facade = facade is None`, and the same DbEndpoint-building `_endpoint`. Both also independently call the identical `_shutdown_owned_facade(self.facade, self._owns_facade)` in `finally` blocks (confirmed at 481-482 and 724-725).
findings.recommendation: Introduce `class _RustDumpClientBase: def __init__(self, config, facade=None): self.config = config; self.facade = facade if facade is not None else DbCoreFacade(); self._owns_facade = facade is None` plus a shared `_endpoint(self, schema)`, and have RustDumpExporter/RustDumpImporter inherit from it instead of repeating both methods.

findings.title: Callback-parameter soup: 13 params with 7 separate Optional[Callable] slots
findings.detail: Confirmed: RustDumpImporter.import_dump (629-644) takes 13 parameters excluding self ‚ÄĒ input_dir, target_schema, threads, import_mode, timezone_sql, progress_callback, table_progress_callback, detail_callback, table_status_callback, raw_output_callback, retry_tables, metadata_callback, table_chunk_progress_callback ‚ÄĒ 7 of which are separate Optional[Callable]. A 5-callback subset (progress/table_progress/detail/table_status/raw_output) is repeated verbatim in `_run_rust_dump` (401-414), `export_full_schema` (449-460), and `export_tables` (484-497) ‚ÄĒ all three signatures confirmed to match exactly.
findings.recommendation: Bundle the callbacks into `@dataclass class DumpEventCallbacks: progress: Optional[Callable]=None; table_progress: Optional[Callable]=None; detail: Optional[Callable]=None; table_status: Optional[Callable]=None; raw_output: Optional[Callable]=None; metadata: Optional[Callable]=None; table_chunk_progress: Optional[Callable]=None`, and pass one `callbacks: Optional[DumpEventCallbacks] = None` through `_run_rust_dump`, `export_full_schema`, `export_tables`, and `import_dump` instead of 5-7 individual optional callables per signature.

findings.title: threads=8 default repeated 7 times with no named constant, unlike the sibling compression default
findings.detail: Confirmed: `threads: int = 8` appears as a bare literal default in `_run_rust_dump` (406), `export_full_schema` (453), `RustDumpExporter.export_tables` (489), `RustDumpImporter.import_dump` (633), module-level `export_schema` (865), module-level `export_tables` (882), and module-level `import_dump` (906) ‚ÄĒ 7 occurrences verified. `DEFAULT_DUMP_COMPRESSION = "zstd"` (line 20) already shows the correct extract-to-constant pattern is known and used elsewhere in this same file, making the un-extracted `threads=8` an inconsistency rather than an oversight from lack of precedent.
findings.recommendation: Add `DEFAULT_DUMP_THREADS = 8` next to `DEFAULT_DUMP_COMPRESSION` (line 20) and reference it in all 7 signatures (406, 453, 489, 633, 865, 882, 906) instead of the bare literal.

findings.title: emit_core_event mixes high-level event dispatch with low-level per-event-type arithmetic
findings.detail: Confirmed: emit_core_event (764-850, 87 lines) dispatches on event_type (dump_plan/dump_schedule/phase/table_progress/row_progress) and, for row_progress, inlines low-level computations at the same indentation as the top-level if/elif: status remapping at line 807 (`ui_status = "loading" if status in (...) else ...`), rows/sec calculation at line 821, and percent calculation at lines 822-825. Five distinct event shapes handled inline rather than via per-event handlers.
findings.recommendation: Extract one private handler per event type (`_handle_dump_plan_event`, `_handle_dump_schedule_event`, `_handle_phase_event`, `_handle_table_progress_event`, `_handle_row_progress_event`) each taking the raw event dict plus the relevant callbacks, and have `emit_core_event` become a plain `{event_type: handler}` dispatch table so each handler (including the percent/rows-sec math) can be read and tested independently.

findings.title: ForeignKeyResolver rebuilds the same orphan JOIN/WHERE SQL fragment 4 times
findings.detail: Confirmed: the `FROM `{schema}`.`{table}` c LEFT JOIN `{schema}`.`{ref_table}` p ON c.`{column}` = p.`{ref_column}` WHERE c.`{column}` IS NOT NULL AND p.`{ref_column}` IS NULL` fragment is independently re-typed in `generate_orphan_query` (197-201), the count_query in `find_orphan_records` (225-231), the sample_query in the same method (237-243), and again with a different SELECT clause in `get_all_orphan_queries` (318-321). Verified all 4 occurrences are structurally identical FROM/JOIN/WHERE clauses.
findings.recommendation: Extract `_orphan_join_where(schema, table, column, ref_table, ref_column) -> str` returning just the `FROM ... LEFT JOIN ... WHERE ...` fragment (as a private method on ForeignKeyResolver), and have `generate_orphan_query`, the count/sample queries inside `find_orphan_records`, and `get_all_orphan_queries` all build their SELECT clause around that one shared fragment.

findings.title: The `ALTER TABLE `{target_schema}`.`{diff.table_name}`` prefix is re-typed 13 times
findings.detail: Confirmed 13 occurrences of the identical f-string prefix, though the exact line numbers in the original finding were each off by 1 in places. Corrected verified line list: 40, 47, 53, 95, 100, 105, 116, 120, 127, 132, 137, 152, 160 (13 total), each followed by a different suffix clause (DROP FOREIGN KEY, ADD COLUMN, DROP COLUMN, MODIFY COLUMN, ADD/DROP/RENAME INDEX, ADD FK). Any change to table-name quoting requires 13 edits.
findings.recommendation: Add `def _alter_table(target_schema: str, table_name: str, clause: str) -> str: return f"ALTER TABLE `{target_schema}`.`{table_name}` {clause};"` as a module-level helper or SyncScriptGenerator method, and call it at all 13 sites (lines 40, 47, 53, 95, 100, 105, 116, 120, 127, 132, 137, 152, 160) with just the suffix clause as an argument.
findings.verification_note: Substance of the finding is correct (13 duplicated occurrences), but several of the originally-cited line numbers were off by 1 (e.g. 39 vs actual 40, 96 vs actual 95). Corrected line_start/line_end and the full line list above.

findings.title: The literal 'PRIMARY' index name is checked with inconsistent case-sensitivity across 3 files
findings.detail: Confirmed: line 111 checks `if idx_diff.index_name == 'PRIMARY': continue` (exact-case), while line 191 in `_generate_create_table` checks `if idx.name.upper() != "PRIMARY":` (case-insensitive). Also confirmed in schema_diff_models.py line 115 (`if self.name == "PRIMARY":`, exact-case, inside IndexInfo.to_sql_definition) and schema_severity_classifier.py line 157 (`if diff.index_name.upper() == 'PRIMARY':`, case-insensitive). No single named constant or shared comparison helper ties these 3 files together.
findings.recommendation: Define `PRIMARY_KEY_INDEX_NAME = "PRIMARY"` and `def is_primary_key_index(name: str) -> bool: return name.upper() == PRIMARY_KEY_INDEX_NAME` in schema_diff_models.py alongside `_normalize_column_extra`, and use both at all 4 call sites (schema_sync_script_generator.py:111, :191, schema_diff_models.py:115, schema_severity_classifier.py:157) instead of ad-hoc literal comparisons.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed real and accurately described. Noting for context: MySQL's INFORMATION_SCHEMA always returns the primary-key index name as exactly 'PRIMARY', so this specific inconsistency has zero observed runtime impact today in a MySQL-only path ‚ÄĒ it is a latent/DRY risk (documented divergence across files) rather than an active bug, but still a legitimate finding worth fixing given the codebase's stated direction toward cross-engine migration support.

findings.title: Every query method silently swallows all exceptions into a log line and an empty/default result
findings.detail: Confirmed by reading lines 24-248 in full. `extract_table_schema` (24-63), `extract_all_tables` (65-95), `_get_columns` (97-132), `_get_indexes` (134-167), `_get_foreign_keys` (169-214), `_get_table_options` (216-237), and `_get_row_count` (239-248) each wrap their query in `try: ... except Exception as e: logger.error(...); return <empty list/dict/None/default tuple>`. A connection failure, a permissions error, and a table that genuinely has zero columns/indexes/FKs all produce the exact same empty-collection result, so `extract_table_schema`/`extract_all_tables` callers cannot distinguish 'no FKs' from 'the FK query failed'.
findings.recommendation: Re-raise (or return a sentinel/Result-like wrapper distinguishing error from genuinely-empty) from the innermost `_get_columns`/`_get_indexes`/`_get_foreign_keys`/`_get_table_options`/`_get_row_count` helpers, so `extract_table_schema` can decide whether to treat a failure as 'no data' or propagate it to the caller/UI instead of silently producing a schema that looks valid but is incomplete.
findings.verification_note: Severity corrected from medium to high: this tool's output (TableSchema) directly drives generate_sync_script's ALTER/CREATE/FK statements. If _get_foreign_keys silently swallows a real query failure and returns [], the generated sync script will omit a real foreign key with no error surfaced anywhere ‚ÄĒ a silent data-integrity/correctness defect in a migration tool, not just a logging-hygiene nit.

findings.title: N+1 query pattern per table, plus a redundant full-table-scan COUNT(*) where an approximate count is already available
findings.detail: Confirmed: `extract_all_tables` (65-95) lists table names with one query then calls `extract_table_schema` per table (line 89), which issues 5 more queries (_get_columns, _get_indexes, _get_foreign_keys, _get_table_options, _get_row_count) ‚ÄĒ 1 + 5N round trips for N tables. Separately confirmed: `_get_table_options` (216-237) already queries `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES` for ENGINE/TABLE_COLLATION per table (218-222), yet `_get_row_count` (239-248) issues an additional `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM \`{schema}\`.\`{table}\`` (line 241) ‚ÄĒ a full table scan ‚ÄĒ even though `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES` also exposes an approximate `TABLE_ROWS` column that could be added to the existing options query.
findings.recommendation: Add `TABLE_ROWS` to the SELECT in `_get_table_options` (line 219) and drop the separate `_get_row_count` full scan where an exact count isn't required for a diff summary. For columns/indexes/FKs, consider fetching all rows for the whole schema once (drop the per-table `TABLE_NAME = %s` filter, group by table name in Python) instead of once per table, cutting 1 + 5N round trips to a small constant number.

findings.title: No shared identifier-quoting helper; backtick-wrapping logic is hand-rolled in 3 dataclasses
findings.detail: Confirmed: `ColumnInfo.to_sql_definition` (79-101) builds `f"`{self.name}`"` (line 81), `IndexInfo.to_sql_definition` (112-120) builds `", ".join(f"`{c}`" for c in self.columns)` (line 114), and `ForeignKeyInfo.to_sql_definition` (133-141) repeats the same join twice for columns (135) and ref_columns (136). This 'wrap identifier in backticks, comma-join a list' logic is reimplemented independently in each dataclass with no shared utility, alongside the module's own `_normalize_column_extra` helper (54-64) that already demonstrates the shared-helper pattern for this file.
findings.recommendation: Add module-level helpers `_quote_ident(name: str) -> str: return f"`{name}`"` and `_quote_idents(names: List[str]) -> str: return ", ".join(_quote_ident(n) for n in names)` next to `_normalize_column_extra` in schema_diff_models.py, and use them from all three `to_sql_definition` methods (and from schema_sync_script_generator.py's own inline identifier quoting) instead of each call site re-implementing it.

findings.title: Severity-order lookup table is rebuilt on every call instead of being a class constant like the sibling _INTEGER_TYPES
findings.detail: Confirmed: `_max_severity` (174-192) constructs `order = {DiffSeverity.CRITICAL: 3, DiffSeverity.WARNING: 2, DiffSeverity.INFO: 1}` (185-189) fresh on every invocation, called once per diff item inside `classify`'s loops (potentially many times for a large schema). `_INTEGER_TYPES = frozenset([...])` (17-19) already shows the correct class-constant pattern for this exact kind of static lookup within the same class.
findings.recommendation: Hoist the dict to a class-level constant `_SEVERITY_ORDER = {DiffSeverity.CRITICAL: 3, DiffSeverity.WARNING: 2, DiffSeverity.INFO: 1}` next to `_INTEGER_TYPES`, and reference `self._SEVERITY_ORDER` from `_max_severity` instead of rebuilding the dict each call.

findings.title: _classify_type_change accepts an unused diff_text parameter
findings.detail: Confirmed: `_classify_type_change(self, diff_text: str, col_diff: ColumnDiff)` (106-124) never references `diff_text` in its body ‚ÄĒ it derives everything from `col_diff.source_info`/`col_diff.target_info` (lines 110-111). Called as `self._classify_type_change(d, diff)` at line 90 in `_classify_column`, passing the diff message string in for no reason.
findings.recommendation: Drop the unused `diff_text` parameter from `_classify_type_change`'s signature (line 106) and update the call site at line 90 to `self._classify_type_change(diff)`.

findings.title: SQLEditorDialog is a 2,608-line god-class mixing UI, connection/transaction management, and raw SQL business logic
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read. The single class SQLEditorDialog spans line 125 to end-of-file (file is 2608 lines total, not 2609 as originally stated ‚ÄĒ off-by-one). `grep -n "^    def "` finds 90 class-body method definitions, matching the '~90 methods' claim. Verified all cited methods exist and do exactly what was described: `_resolve_db_target` (line 182), `_ensure_connection` (860) and `_close_db_connection` (1661) own temp-tunnel/connector lifecycle; `pending_queries`, `_do_commit` (1508), `_do_rollback` (1611), `_on_postgres_transaction_rolled_back` (1114) implement a hand-rolled transactional state machine; `_analyze_query_editability` (1833-1888, 56 lines) is pure regex-based SQL text analysis (JOIN/UNION/GROUP BY/HAVING/DISTINCT/aggregate-function rejection); `_fetch_primary_keys` (1890-1941) runs raw `information_schema`/`key_column_usage` SQL straight off `self.db_connection.cursor()` with engine-specific branches for postgres vs mysql; `_execute_cell_edits_in_txn` (2072-2121) hand-builds UPDATE statements with manual identifier quoting via `_quote_editor_identifier`. This directly contradicts the project's CLAUDE.md mandate that DB auth/schema/SQL execution should run through the Rust `tunnelforge-core` sidecar while Python stays UI/orchestration only.
findings.recommendation: Split into at least three collaborators the dialog composes rather than implements: (1) a connection/transaction controller (e.g. `SqlEditorTransactionSession`) owning temp-tunnel lifecycle, `pending_queries`, commit/rollback, and postgres-rollback-on-error handling; (2) a `ResultEditabilityAnalyzer` module owning `_analyze_query_editability`, `_fetch_primary_keys`, and `_execute_cell_edits_in_txn` (ideally routed through tunnelforge-core rather than raw cursor SQL, per CLAUDE.md's Rust-core mandate); (3) leave SQLEditorDialog as thin UI glue delegating to both. This also makes the PK/editability logic unit-testable without a QApplication.
findings.verification_note: Accurate and well-supported. Only correction: file is 2608 lines, not 2609 (line_end should be 2608). Severity 'high' is justified given the direct contradiction with the project's stated Rust-core architecture mandate plus the sheer scope (90 methods, 5+ distinct responsibility clusters).

findings.title: init_ui is a single 355-line method building the entire dialog layout inline
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read: init_ui() runs from line 233 to line 588 (the next method, setup_shortcuts, begins at line 590). It constructs the connection bar, toolbar (with several 6-10 line inline QSS stylesheet strings for btn_execute_current, btn_execute_all, btn_toggle_message, new_tab_button, tx_status_frame, btn_commit, btn_rollback, pending_list_widget, progress_bar, schema_tree, etc.), the schema tree, editor tabs, result tabs, the transaction status panel, the pending-edits list, the progress bar, and the status bar, all inline with signal wiring interleaved throughout.
findings.recommendation: Break init_ui into per-region builder methods (`_build_connection_bar`, `_build_toolbar`, `_build_editor_panel`, `_build_result_panel`, `_build_transaction_panel`, `_build_status_bar`) that each return a widget/layout, and keep init_ui as a short method that assembles them. Move the repeated QSS blocks into module-level constants (e.g. `PRIMARY_BUTTON_QSS`, `TX_PANEL_QSS`) to shrink the method further.
findings.verification_note: Line range 233-588 is exact. Medium severity is appropriate ‚ÄĒ long but mechanically simple widget-wiring code, not tangled control flow.

findings.title: _do_commit mixes guard-checks, per-schema danger confirmation, transactional SQL execution, error rollback, and history bookkeeping in one method
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read: _do_commit spans exactly lines 1508-1609 (102 lines, next method _do_rollback starts at 1611). It performs: executing-state/rolled-back guard checks; computing pending_count/table_edits/cell_edit_count; grouping edits by schema and invoking ProductionGuard.confirm_dangerous_operation per schema; executing the actual UPDATEs via _execute_cell_edits_in_txn inside a `with self.db_connection.cursor()` block; handling partial failure with rollback plus a formatted multi-line error message; committing on success; batch-updating SQLHistory status; calling _finalize_cell_edits for UI state; and building a user-facing summary message ‚ÄĒ each a distinct responsibility interleaved in one method.
findings.recommendation: Extract the schema-grouping + ProductionGuard confirmation loop into `_confirm_cell_edit_commit(table_edits) -> bool`, and the try/except commit body into `_apply_commit(table_edits) -> CommitResult` (success/failed-rows), leaving _do_commit as: guard checks -> confirm -> apply -> report.
findings.verification_note: Line range 1508-1609 confirmed exact.

findings.title: Pending-change summary ('žŅľŽ¶¨ NÍĪī, žÖÄ ŪéłžßĎ NÍĪī') is built identically in _do_commit and _do_rollback
findings.detail: Confirmed: _do_commit lines 1588-1592 build `parts = []; if pending_count > 0: parts.append(f"žŅľŽ¶¨ {pending_count}ÍĪī"); if cell_edit_count > 0: parts.append(f"žÖÄ ŪéłžßĎ {cell_edit_count}ÍĪī")`, and _do_rollback lines 1626-1631 build the identical sequence (plus `summary = ', '.join(parts)`), verbatim duplicated.
findings.recommendation: Extract a helper `_describe_pending_changes(pending_count: int, cell_edit_count: int) -> str` returning the joined summary string, and call it from both _do_commit and _do_rollback.
findings.verification_note: Line ranges confirmed exact in both methods. Low severity is correctly calibrated ‚ÄĒ trivial, low-risk duplication.

findings.title: SQL preview truncation length is a different unexplained magic number at every call site
findings.detail: Confirmed all four sites: `sql_text[:200]` in _execute_sql (line 992), `query[:60]` in _on_transaction_query_result (line 1065), `pq['query'][:50]` in _update_tx_status (line 1383), and `query[:100]` in SQLTransactionExecutionWorker.run (src/ui/dialogs/sql_editor_workers.py:183). All four perform the same 'truncate SQL text for a preview/log line' operation with no shared constant or documented rationale for why the lengths differ (200/60/50/100).
findings.recommendation: Add a single `_truncate_preview(text: str, length: int = 60) -> str` helper (module-level or on a shared SQL-text-utils module) and use it at all four sites, picking one deliberate length per UI context via a named constant if they truly need to differ (e.g. `DANGER_CONFIRM_PREVIEW_LEN = 200`).
findings.verification_note: All four line numbers and literal values confirmed by direct read.

findings.title: Result-table column width cap (400), row height (28), and elapsed-timer interval (100ms) are unnamed literals
findings.detail: Confirmed in _add_result_table: line 1213-1214 `if header.sectionSize(col) > 400: header.resizeSection(col, 400)` and line 1220 `table.verticalHeader().setDefaultSectionSize(28)`. Also confirmed in _set_executing_state, line 1716: `self._exec_timer.start(100)`. All three are bare literals with no named constant.
findings.recommendation: Introduce module-level constants such as `MAX_AUTO_COLUMN_WIDTH_PX = 400`, `RESULT_ROW_HEIGHT_PX = 28`, and `ELAPSED_TIMER_INTERVAL_MS = 100` and reference them at the call sites.
findings.verification_note: All three literals and their line numbers confirmed by direct read (1213-1214, 1220, 1716).

findings.title: Leftover empty section-divider comments still reference classes that were extracted to other files
findings.detail: Confirmed: lines 72-124 contain nine `# ====...====` section headers ('SQL ÍĶ¨Ž¨ł ŪēėžĚīŽĚľžĚīŪĄį', 'žěźŽŹôžôĄžĄĪ ŪĆĚžóÖ', 'ž§Ą Ž≤ąŪėł žúĄž†Į', 'žĹĒŽďú žóźŽĒĒŪĄį', 'Í≤Äž¶Ě ÍłįŽä•žĚī žěąŽäĒ žĹĒŽďú žóźŽĒĒŪĄį', 'SQL žŅľŽ¶¨ žč§ŪĖČ žõĆžĽ§', 'SQL ŪäłŽěúžě≠žÖė žč§ŪĖČ žõĆžĽ§', 'Ūěąžä§ŪÜ†Ž¶¨ Žč§žĚīžĖľŽ°úÍ∑ł', 'SQL žóźŽĒĒŪĄį ŪÉ≠') with zero code beneath any of them before the SQLEditorDialog class begins at line 125. The imports at the top of the file (lines 38-52) confirm these classes now live in sql_editor_highlighters.py, sql_editor_autocomplete.py, sql_editor_code_editor.py, sql_editor_workers.py, and sql_editor_history_dialog.py ‚ÄĒ the dividers are dead leftovers from the file-splitting refactor.
findings.recommendation: Delete lines 72-124 entirely (or replace with a single short comment pointing readers to the sibling modules where these classes now live).
findings.verification_note: Exact line range and content confirmed by direct read.

findings.title: SQLEditorTab.set_content() and load_file() duplicate the same 'apply text to editor' sequence
findings.detail: Confirmed: set_content (lines 403-410) and load_file (416-430) both perform the identical sequence ‚ÄĒ `_set_large_document_mode_for_text(text)`, `editor.blockSignals(True)`, `editor.setPlainText(...)`, `editor.blockSignals(False)`, `is_modified = False`, `title_changed.emit(...)` ‚ÄĒ differing only in where the text comes from and whether file_path is set.
findings.recommendation: Extract a private `_apply_text(self, text: str)` method containing the shared 5-line block, and have both set_content and load_file call it (load_file additionally sets self.file_path and wraps the file read in try/except).
findings.verification_note: Duplication is real and exactly as described, but severity downgraded from medium to low: the two methods are 13 lines apart in the same class in a single file, the duplicated block is short (5-6 lines), and both call sites are simultaneously visible to anyone editing either ‚ÄĒ the actual risk of silent divergence is low relative to the other 'medium' duplication finding in this batch (the cross-file cursor-to-row-list one), which involves two separate worker classes in different execution modes.

findings.title: IssueSeverity is imported inside the per-issue for-loop instead of at module scope
findings.detail: Confirmed: in SQLValidatorHighlighter._build_issue_map, `from src.core.sql_validator import IssueSeverity` sits at line 152, inside `for issue in self._issues:` (loop starts line 146). Re-executes (module-cache-cheap but still) once per issue on every set_issues() call, and hides a real dependency from the top-of-file import list.
findings.recommendation: Move `from src.core.sql_validator import IssueSeverity` to the top-level imports of sql_editor_highlighters.py alongside the PyQt6 import at line 4.
findings.verification_note: Line number and code confirmed exactly by direct read.

findings.title: Cursor-to-row-list conversion logic is duplicated verbatim between SQLQueryWorker and SQLTransactionExecutionWorker
findings.detail: Confirmed: SQLQueryWorker.run() (lines 108-116) and SQLTransactionExecutionWorker.run() (lines 192-199) both contain the identical block `columns = [desc[0] for desc in cursor.description]; rows = cursor.fetchall(); row_list = []; for row in rows: if isinstance(row, dict): row_list.append([row.get(col) for col in columns]) else: row_list.append(list(row))`. These are two separate classes (autocommit-mode vs persistent-transaction-mode workers) in the same file ‚ÄĒ a fix to the dict/tuple row-handling (e.g. a new row representation from the Rust connector) must be applied in both places with no shared test surface tying them together.
findings.recommendation: Extract a module-level helper `_rows_from_cursor(cursor) -> tuple[list[str], list[list]]` in sql_editor_workers.py and call it from both worker classes.
findings.verification_note: Duplication confirmed verbatim at both line ranges. Medium severity is appropriate given this is real data-conversion logic (not merely cosmetic) spanning two independently-invoked execution modes.

findings.title: load_history() and _do_search() duplicate the list-reset sequence
findings.detail: Confirmed: load_history (218-223) and _do_search (336-341) both execute `self.list_widget.clear()`, `self._history_items.clear()`, `self.current_offset = 0`, differing only in the `_is_searching` flag value (False vs True) and whether `_update_fav_count()` is called afterward.
findings.recommendation: Extract a small `_reset_list_state(is_searching: bool)` helper that clears the widget/items list, resets the offset, and sets `_is_searching`, then call it from both load_history and _do_search.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed exact duplication at both locations. Low severity is correctly calibrated ‚ÄĒ trivial 3-line duplication.

findings.title: SQLQueryWorker.__init__ takes 8 primitive connection parameters instead of a connection-info object
findings.detail: Confirmed: line 33 `def __init__(self, host, port, user, password, database, queries, engine="mysql", schema=None):` takes 8 params, 5-6 of which (host, port, user, password, database/schema, engine) form the same connection tuple reconstructed independently. The identical primitive-obsession pattern is confirmed at `create_sql_editor_connector(engine, host, port, user, password, database=None, schema=None)` (this file, line 14) and `SQLEditorDialog._create_db_connector(self, host, port, user, password, database=None, schema=None)` (sql_editor_dialog.py, line 209) ‚ÄĒ three separate signatures that must all be updated together if a connection parameter (e.g. an SSL flag) is ever added.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a small `@dataclass ConnectionParams(engine, host, port, user, password, database=None, schema=None)` and have create_sql_editor_connector, SQLQueryWorker, SQLTransactionExecutionWorker, and SQLEditorDialog._create_db_connector all accept/pass a single ConnectionParams instance instead of the individual fields.
findings.verification_note: All three cited signatures and their exact line numbers confirmed by direct read.

findings.title: RustDumpWizard.start_export/start_import/start_orphan_check repeat the same connector-acquisition block three times
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read of lines 98-208. start_export (98-135), start_import (137-170), and start_orphan_check (172-208) each repeat: check self.preselected_tunnel -> call _connect_preselected_tunnel(), else open DBConnectionDialog, bail if exec() != Accepted, call get_connector(), bail if falsy. Only start_export additionally captures connection_info via conn_dialog.get_connection_identifier() in the dialog branch (it also gets connection_info from _connect_preselected_tunnel() in the preselected branch, which the other two methods discard with `_`). Any future change to the connection flow must be replicated three times.
findings.recommendation: Extract a private helper `_resolve_connector(self, need_connection_info: bool = False) -> tuple[connector|None, str|None]` encapsulating the preselected-tunnel vs. dialog branching, and have all three start_* methods call it.

findings.title: db_dialogs.py imports ~15 names it never uses itself; some are load-bearing test re-exports, others are pure dead imports
findings.detail: Confirmed none of the 15 imported names (lines 9-32) are referenced anywhere in db_dialogs.py's own code. However the finding's claim that ALL of them are a 'load-bearing re-export shim' for tests is only partially true - I verified both sub-groups directly: (1) Genuinely load-bearing: format_import_row_labels, import_overall_percent, displayed_import_percent, format_import_visible_telemetry, _sanitized_rust_event, _sanitize_plain_rust_line are imported from db_dialogs by tests/test_db_import_dialog.py (lines 11-19), and OrphanAnalysisWorker/OrphanReportWorker are imported from db_dialogs by tests/test_db_orphan_dialog.py (lines 11-14) - removing these would break those tests. (2) Purely dead, not even a re-export shim: cap_incomplete_export_percent, next_export_percent, export_overall_percent, format_export_row_labels, format_export_table_status, format_export_visible_telemetry are re-imported directly from db_export_dialog by tests/test_db_export_dialog.py (not via db_dialogs), and _build_orphan_queries_sql has zero references anywhere outside its own definition and this unused import. These 7 names can simply be deleted from db_dialogs.py's imports with no test impact.
findings.recommendation: Delete the 7 dead imports outright (cap_incomplete_export_percent, next_export_percent, export_overall_percent, format_export_row_labels, format_export_table_status, format_export_visible_telemetry, _build_orphan_queries_sql - verify with a grep before deleting). For the remaining genuinely re-exported names, add an explicit `__all__` plus a one-line comment ('re-exported for backward-compatible test imports'), or better, change tests/test_db_import_dialog.py and tests/test_db_orphan_dialog.py to import directly from db_import_dialog.py / db_orphan_dialog.py.
findings.verification_note: Original finding overstated: it implied all imports serve the re-export shim, but 7 of the 15 (6 export-percent/formatting helpers + _build_orphan_queries_sql) have no downstream reference at all, even via db_dialogs re-export - they are simple dead imports, not a documented compatibility shim. Tightened detail/recommendation to separate the two cases.

findings.title: RustDumpExportDialog mixes UI construction, filesystem-path business logic, worker orchestration, GitHub reporting and file I/O in one 1300-line class
findings.detail: Confirmed: class spans lines 168-1461 (file ends at 1463). Verified it contains init_ui (~390 lines), output-folder naming/uniqueness logic (_generate_output_dir/_unique_output_dir, including path-traversal-safe join logic), Rust worker construction/telemetry parsing (do_export, on_detail_progress, on_raw_output), GitHub issue auto-reporting (_report_error_to_github/_on_github_report_finished), and log-file serialization, all as methods of the same QDialog subclass.
findings.recommendation: Split into: (1) a thin QDialog that only wires widgets to signals, (2) an `ExportOutputPathResolver` (folder naming/uniqueness/safe-join), (3) an `ExportTelemetryReport` module (summaries + save_log content generation), and (4) a shared GitHub-reporting helper (see the separate GitHub-reporting duplication finding). The dialog should call these collaborators instead of implementing them inline.

findings.title: init_ui is a ~390-line method building the whole dialog in one block
findings.detail: Confirmed: init_ui spans exactly lines 212-602 (390 lines), building status/type/schema/table/options/output-folder/progress/table-status/log/button sections sequentially with inline QSS strings and no sub-structure.
findings.recommendation: Break into private builder methods returning widgets/groupboxes, e.g. `_build_status_group()`, `_build_export_type_group()`, `_build_schema_section()`, `_build_output_folder_group()`, `_build_progress_section()`, `_build_button_row()`, and have init_ui assemble them into the splitter/layout.

findings.title: do_export does validation, path generation, state reset, config building and worker wiring in one ~130-line method
findings.detail: Confirmed: do_export spans exactly lines 877-1008. It validates schema/table selection, regenerates/de-duplicates the output directory (898-899), persists config settings (908-910), resets ~15 instance fields (913-932), writes log lines (935-945), toggles UI state (948-956), builds RustDumpConfig (973-979), and constructs/starts one of two RustDumpWorker variants with 6 signal connections (983-1008), all inline in one method.
findings.recommendation: Extract `_reset_export_state()`, `_resolve_output_dir(schema)`, and `_build_worker(schema, output_dir)` so do_export reads as a short sequence of named steps.

findings.title: Directory-traversal-safe path construction is buried as nested closures inside a Qt dialog method
findings.detail: Confirmed: _generate_output_dir spans lines 645-707. safe_component() (656-661) and safe_join() (663-673) are nested functions that strip dangerous filesystem characters and use Path.is_relative_to() to prevent the export folder from escaping base_dir - genuine security-relevant logic - but they live inline inside a QDialog method alongside reads of self.radio_manual_naming/self.chk_name Qt widgets, so they cannot be unit-tested without instantiating the dialog.
findings.recommendation: Move safe_component/safe_join into a standalone, importable function (e.g. `src/core/path_safety.py: safe_output_dir(base_dir, folder_name)`) with its own unit tests, and have the dialog call it with plain strings.

findings.title: Redundant local re-imports of os/datetime/Path that shadow already-imported module-level names
findings.detail: Confirmed: `import os` (module line 16) and `from datetime import datetime` (module line 14) are already present. _get_base_output_dir re-imports `os` locally at line 642, _generate_output_dir re-imports `os`, `datetime`, and `Path` locally at lines 650-652, and browse_base_dir re-imports `os` locally at line 792. These add no behavior and shadow the module-level names for no reason.
findings.recommendation: Delete the three local import statements (lines 642, 650-652, 792) and rely on the module-level imports already present at the top of the file.
findings.verification_note: Category is better described as 'redundant-import' / code smell than 'dead-code' - the imported names ARE used (just redundantly re-imported), so nothing here is literally unreachable/unused code. Content and severity (low) are otherwise accurate.

findings.title: Collapsible-config-panel logic (toggle/collapse/expand + splitter ratios) is duplicated verbatim in db_import_dialog.py
findings.detail: Confirmed byte-for-byte duplication: toggle_config_section/collapse_config_section/expand_config_section at db_export_dialog.py lines 604-634 are identical (same 'ūüĒľ žĄ§ž†ē ŪéľžĻėÍłį'/'ūüĒĹ žĄ§ž†ē ž†ĎÍłį' labels, same 60:40 / 10:90 splitter ratios) to db_import_dialog.py lines 673-703. Any UX tweak to this behavior must be made in two files.
findings.recommendation: Extract a small mixin/base class (e.g. `CollapsibleConfigDialog`) providing these three methods plus the `splitter`/`config_container`/`btn_collapse` attribute contract, and have both RustDumpExportDialog and RustDumpImportDialog inherit/compose it.

findings.title: GitHub error-reporting boilerplate duplicated with db_import_dialog.py
findings.detail: Confirmed: _report_error_to_github's worker-spawning tail (build GitHubReportWorker, append to self._github_workers, connect finished with the lambda, .start()) at lines 1263-1271, and the entirety of _on_github_report_finished at lines 1273-1282, are identical to db_import_dialog.py lines 1496-1516 (only the surrounding `context` dict construction and _report_error_to_github's extra error_count parameter differ).
findings.recommendation: Factor a shared helper, e.g. `report_error_to_github(dialog, error_type, message, context)` in `src/ui/workers/github_worker.py`, that both dialogs call, or a small `GithubReportingMixin` providing `_report_error_to_github`/`_on_github_report_finished`/`_github_workers`.

findings.title: RustDumpConfig construction from a connector, including magic fallback values, is duplicated with db_import_dialog.py
findings.detail: Confirmed: lines 973-979 build `RustDumpConfig(host=getattr(self.connector,'host','127.0.0.1'), port=... if hasattr(...) else 3306, user=... if hasattr(...) else 'root', password=..., engine=getattr(self.connector,'engine','mysql'))`, repeated verbatim in db_import_dialog.py lines 1050-1056, including the unnamed magic fallbacks 3306/'root'/'mysql'/'127.0.0.1'. src/core/constants.py already defines DEFAULT_MYSQL_PORT=3306 and DEFAULT_LOCAL_HOST='127.0.0.1', so those two fallbacks aren't even using the project's existing named constants.
findings.recommendation: Add a factory function, e.g. `build_rust_dump_config(connector) -> RustDumpConfig` in rust_dump_exporter.py, using the existing DEFAULT_MYSQL_PORT/DEFAULT_LOCAL_HOST constants from src/core/constants.py (plus new named constants for the 'root'/'mysql' fallbacks), and call it from both dialogs.

findings.title: Log-line cap of 500 hard-coded four times across two files with no named constant
findings.detail: Confirmed: `if self.txt_log.count() > 500:` appears twice in this file's on_raw_output (lines 1239 and 1246). The same 500 cap reappears in db_import_dialog.py's _add_log (line 920, `if len(self.log_entries) > 500`) and on_raw_output (line 1319, `if self.txt_log.count() > 500`). None reference a shared constant.
findings.recommendation: Introduce `MAX_VISIBLE_LOG_LINES = 500` and `MAX_LOG_ENTRIES = 500` in a shared module (e.g. alongside the existing constants in src/core/constants.py) and reference it from both dialogs' four call sites.

findings.title: Table-status icon map is redefined locally instead of being a shared constant
findings.detail: Confirmed: `status_icons = {'pending': '‚Ź≥', 'loading': 'ūüĒĄ', 'done': '‚úÖ', 'error': '‚ĚĆ'}` is defined inline at lines 1187-1192 (on_table_status). The identical dict is redefined in db_import_dialog.py's on_table_status (lines 1193-1198) and again in on_table_chunk_progress (lines 1273-1278).
findings.recommendation: Hoist a single module-level `TABLE_STATUS_ICONS = {...}` constant (in a shared UI-constants module) and reuse it in all three locations instead of re-declaring the dict per method.

findings.title: RustDumpImportDialog mixes UI construction, timezone-detection SQL policy, production-guard checks, retry orchestration, GitHub reporting and log file I/O in one 1450-line class
findings.detail: Confirmed: class spans lines 222-1667 (file's last line, closeEvent ends there). It contains init_ui (~400 lines, 267-671), MySQL timezone-compensation SQL decisions (do_import 1058-1092, check_timezone_support 899-913), ProductionGuard invocation (do_import 968-983), MySQL 8.4 compatibility analysis via DumpFileAnalyzer (_run_upgrade_check 795-829), retry-table selection (select_failed_tables 1517-1522, do_retry 1524+), GitHub error reporting (_report_error_to_github 1474-1504), and full import-log serialization (save_log).
findings.recommendation: Extract an `ImportTimezoneResolver` (SQL decision logic), keep ProductionGuard invocation but isolate it behind a `_confirm_production_import()` method, and reuse the same GitHub-reporting/telemetry helpers proposed for db_export_dialog.py so the dialog is left with widget wiring only.
findings.verification_note: Corrected line_end from 1668 to 1667 - the file has exactly 1667 lines and the class (via closeEvent) ends on the last line.

findings.title: init_ui is a ~400-line method building the whole import dialog in one block
findings.detail: Confirmed: init_ui spans exactly lines 267-671 (405 lines), constructing rust-core-status, dump-folder, MySQL-8.4-compatibility, target-schema, import-options, timezone, import-mode and progress/log/button sections sequentially with no decomposition.
findings.recommendation: Split into private builder methods (`_build_status_group()`, `_build_input_dir_group()`, `_build_upgrade_check_group()`, `_build_schema_group()`, `_build_timezone_group()`, `_build_import_mode_group()`, `_build_progress_section()`), mirroring the recommendation for db_export_dialog.py's init_ui.

findings.title: do_import embeds raw timezone SQL strings and production-guard business rules directly inside a Qt dialog method
findings.detail: Confirmed: do_import spans lines 946-1128 (finding's original 946-1127 missed the final self.worker.start() line). Within this ~180-line method it mixes UI orchestration (validate input, toggle widgets, collapse config section) with DB policy: literal SQL strings like "SET SESSION time_zone = '+09:00'" / "SET TIME ZONE '+09:00'" chosen via radio-button state (lines 1072-1092), and a ProductionGuard.confirm_dangerous_operation call with inline schema-name derivation (lines 968-983).
findings.recommendation: Extract `_resolve_timezone_sql(engine: str, mode: str) -> Optional[str]` as a standalone, testable function (module-level or in a `timezone_policy.py`), and extract `_confirm_production_guard(input_dir, target_schema) -> bool` so do_import reads as: validate -> confirm production -> resolve timezone -> build config -> start worker.
findings.verification_note: Corrected line_end from 1127 to 1128 - do_import's last statement (self.worker.start()) is on line 1128; the next method (on_progress) begins at 1129.

findings.title: Byte-to-MB/GB size formatting is copy-pasted three times in the same file
findings.detail: Confirmed: the pattern `size_mb = size_bytes / (1024*1024); if size_mb < 1024: size_str = f'{size_mb:.1f} MB' else: size_str = f'{size_mb/1024:.2f} GB'` appears verbatim in on_table_status (lines 1217-1222), on_table_chunk_progress (lines 1286-1290), and on_metadata_analyzed (lines 1366-1370).
findings.recommendation: Extract a helper `_format_bytes(size_bytes: int) -> str` and call it from all three sites (and from db_export_dialog.py if it ever needs the same formatting).

findings.title: Failed/done-table counting over self.import_results is reimplemented five times with inconsistent filtering
findings.detail: Confirmed all five sites and their inconsistencies: on_import_finished (1383-1386) filters `t != 'fk_restore' and isinstance(r, dict) and r.get('status')=='error'`; on_finished (1400-1403) builds `table_results` excluding fk_restore with an isinstance check then counts done/error; _report_error_to_github (1480-1481) counts errors without excluding 'fk_restore' or checking isinstance; select_failed_tables (1519-1522) iterates without isinstance check; save_log (1590-1592) counts done/error again without excluding fk_restore or checking isinstance. Each copy diverges in what it guards against, so success/failure counts shown in the completion dialog, the GitHub issue body, the retry-selection UI, and the saved log file are not guaranteed to agree.
findings.recommendation: Add one helper, e.g. `_table_results(self) -> dict[str, dict]` returning import_results with 'fk_restore' excluded and non-dict values filtered out, plus `_count_by_status(results, status)`, and use them consistently at all five call sites.

findings.title: status_colors dict and _color variable are computed but never used
findings.detail: Confirmed: `status_colors = {...}` (lines 1199-1204) is built and `_color = status_colors.get(status, '#7f8c8d')  # noqa: F841` (line 1208) is assigned, but `_color` is never read anywhere. The `# noqa: F841` comment on the same line documents it as known-unused, planned for 'ŪĖ•ŪõĄ ŪÖĆžĚīŽłĒ ŪĖČ žä§ŪÉÄžĚľŽßĀžóź žā¨žö© žėąž†ē' (future row styling).
