findings.verification_note: Corrected the call-site line citation: the finding said 'line 251/206' but the first assignment (`app_cls = QApplication()`) is actually at line 251 (main()) and line 195 (run_ui_smoke_check()); line 206 is where the second call (`app_cls([...])`) happens for the smoke-check path. The underlying claim (name-shadowing footgun) is accurate regardless.

findings.title: smart_release.py reimplements scripts/versioning.py instead of importing it, and the copies have drifted
findings.detail: Confirmed: `scripts/versioning.py`'s own module docstring states it holds functions 'shared' between `bump_version.py` and `smart_release.py`, and `bump_version.py` does import from it (`from versioning import read_version, write_version, sync_pyproject, sync_installer, bump_version`, line 84-90). `smart_release.py`, however, has zero references to `versioning`/`sync_pyproject`/`sync_installer` (confirmed via grep) and instead redefines `compare_versions` (59-70), `get_local_version` (73-79, duplicate of `read_version` but returns a tuple with file content too), `bump_version` (113-122, duplicate of versioning.py's but with NO `bump_type` validation and no try/except around a malformed version string ‚ÄĒ versioning.py's raises `ValueError` in both cases via lines 135-144), and `update_version_file` (125-132, duplicate of `write_version` but using bare `re.sub` with no check that a substitution actually happened via `count`, so it silently no-ops on a malformed file instead of raising like `write_version` does via its `re.subn`+count check). `smart_release.py` also never calls `sync_pyproject`/`sync_installer`, so a release performed via this documented 'ÍłīÍłČ fallback' manual path leaves `pyproject.toml` and `installer/TunnelForge.iss` version fields out of sync with `src/version.py`.
findings.recommendation: Import and reuse `compare_versions`, `bump_version`, `read_version`, `write_version`, `sync_pyproject`, and `sync_installer` from `scripts/versioning.py` inside `smart_release.py`, deleting the duplicated re-implementations; this removes the validation/robustness drift and gives the manual fallback path the same pyproject/installer sync guarantee as the automated path.

findings.title: Repeated try/except/print pattern for syncing version.py, pyproject.toml, and the installer script
findings.detail: Confirmed: the three file-sync blocks ‚ÄĒ writing `version_file` (122-127), syncing `pyproject_file` (129-137, guarded by `.exists()`), and syncing `installer_file` (140-148, guarded by `.exists()`) ‚ÄĒ all follow the same shape: `try: sync_fn(...); print("[OK] ...")` / `except (ValueError, OSError) as e: print("ERROR: ..."); return 1`, with an `else: print("[SKIP] ...")` branch for the two optional files. Only the function/path/label differ.
findings.recommendation: Factor out a helper, e.g. `_apply_sync(sync_fn, path: Path, new_version: str, label: str, required: bool) -> int | None`, that performs the optional existence check, try/except, and OK/ERROR/SKIP logging once, and call it three times from `main()`.

findings.title: adaptive_dump_parallel_limits_with_avg always returns the non-adaptive baseline
findings.detail: Verified by re-reading lines 2019-2061. `baseline = dump_parallel_limits(threads, table_total)` is computed at line 2026. There are exactly three exit points after that: the early return at 2028-2030 (`table_total <= 1 || row_counts.is_empty()`), the early return at 2054-2056 (`heavy_tables > 1`), the early return at 2057-2059 (`max_estimated_chunks >= thread_budget*2`), and the trailing bare `baseline` at line 2060. Every single one of these returns `baseline` verbatim ‚ÄĒ there is no branch anywhere in the function that returns anything else. The `heavy_tables` computation (2032-2042, iterating `row_counts` and calling `mysql_range_chunk_size_for_avg_row` per table) and `max_estimated_chunks` computation (2043-2053, same iteration + `.max()`) are therefore fully dead work on every call. I additionally checked the test suite (lines 13387-13500): five tests exist that pass different `row_counts`/`avg_row_lengths` combinations expecting to exercise 'adaptive' behavior (e.g. `adaptive_dump_limits_prioritize_range_workers_for_heavy_chunked_tables`, `adaptive_dump_limits_use_byte_chunks_for_wide_tables`), but every asserted `(table_workers, range_workers_per_table)` pair exactly matches what `dump_parallel_limits(threads, table_total)` alone would produce ‚ÄĒ meaning the tests unknowingly validate dead code and would not catch a regression in the intended adaptive logic even if someone tried to add it.
findings.recommendation: Either implement real adaptive behavior ‚ÄĒ e.g. when `heavy_tables <= 1` and `max_estimated_chunks < thread_budget*2` (i.e. exactly one/zero dominant heavy tables), return `DumpParallelLimits { table_workers: 1, range_workers_per_table: thread_budget }` to devote full range-parallelism to the single heavy table instead of falling through to `baseline` ‚ÄĒ or delete the dead computation entirely: remove `adaptive_dump_parallel_limits_with_avg`'s `heavy_tables`/`max_estimated_chunks` logic (and the `#[cfg(test)]` `adaptive_dump_parallel_limits` wrapper) and have `dump_run` (line 1706) call `dump_parallel_limits` directly. Whichever path is chosen, add a unit test asserting a case where the adaptive result differs from `dump_parallel_limits(threads, table_total)` to prevent this dead-code regression from reappearing silently.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate as written; no correction needed. Severity high is justified ‚ÄĒ this masks an intended parallelism-tuning safeguard for large/heavy dumps behind ~40 lines of dead computation, and 5 tests currently give false confidence that adaptive behavior is exercised.

findings.title: Bounded worker-pool dispatch loop duplicated near-verbatim in three dump functions
findings.detail: Verified by re-reading dump_tables_parallel (2083-2199, dispatch loop 2107-2198), dump_tables_global_mysql (2201-2465ish, dispatch loop 2330-2456), and dump_mysql_table_parallel_ranges (2526-2679, dispatch loop 2598-2670). All three independently declare `pending: VecDeque`, `active`, `completed`(_work), `first_error: Option<String>`, `handles: Vec<JoinHandle<_>>`, an `mpsc::channel`, a priming loop (`while active < max_threads { ... }`) that pops from `pending` and spawns, and a `recv()`-driven event loop that on Done/Error decrements `active`, records the first error, and refills from `pending`. The `dump_tables_global_mysql` copy additionally confirmed to differ slightly as described ‚ÄĒ it mutates `state.chunks_done`/`state.rows_dumped` in the `RangeDone` arm (2360-2404) inline per-table, rather than a flat counter, illustrating the copies already drifting.
findings.recommendation: Extract a generic bounded worker-pool helper, e.g. `fn run_bounded_pool<T, E>(work: VecDeque<T>, max_workers: usize, spawn: impl Fn(T, mpsc::Sender<E>) -> thread::JoinHandle<()>, mut on_event: impl FnMut(E) -> PoolAction) -> Result<(), String>` (with `PoolAction::Continue | PoolAction::Fatal(String)`), and have `dump_tables_parallel`, `dump_tables_global_mysql`, and `dump_mysql_table_parallel_ranges` supply only their event type and per-event handling closure. This removes roughly 150 duplicated lines of channel/queue bookkeeping.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate; line range correctly anchors the shared dispatch-loop portion of dump_tables_parallel (whose full function body starts at line 2083) rather than the whole function ‚ÄĒ this is intentional per the finding's own breakdown of the three ranges and is not an error.

findings.title: dump_run mixes option parsing, schema prep, strategy selection, and manifest writing in one ~240-line function
findings.detail: Verified exact function boundaries (1620-1859, 240 lines). Confirmed six distinct phases in sequence: (1) option parsing/validation lines 1621-1665; (2) output dir prep line 1667-1668; (3) schema inspect/filter/dependency-order lines 1670-1681; (4) row/byte stats + adaptive parallel limits lines 1683-1732; (5) four-way strategy dispatch via nested if/else-if on `(endpoint.engine, threads, table_total)` at lines 1733-1800, calling `dump_single_mysql_table_parallel`, `dump_tables_global_mysql`, `dump_tables_parallel`, and `dump_tables_sequential` (the last used twice, once as a fallback inside branch 1 and once as the final else); (6) view collection + `DumpManifest` assembly/write + result JSON at lines 1802-1858.
findings.recommendation: Split into focused helpers: `fn parse_dump_run_options(request: &Request) -> Result<DumpRunOptions, String>` for the option-parsing block (1621-1665); `fn select_dump_strategy(engine: &str, threads: usize, table_total: usize) -> DumpStrategy` returning an enum consumed by a single match at the current 1733-1800 call site; and `fn finalize_dump_manifest(endpoint, schema, table_manifests, ..) -> Result<(DumpManifest, usize), String>` for the view-collection + manifest-write tail (1802-1839). Keep `dump_run` as a short orchestrator (~40-60 lines) that calls these in sequence and builds the final result JSON.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate as written; no correction needed.

findings.title: dump_import is a ~300-line function combining validation, DDL sequencing, two import strategies, and reporting
findings.detail: Verified exact function boundaries (3234-3533, 300 lines) ‚ÄĒ the largest function checked in this pass. Confirmed: payload/manifest validation (mode, data_format, compression, strict_manifest) lines 3235-3293; dependency ordering + chunk validation 3294-3304; MySQL FK preflight + child-first drop-all for replace/recreate at lines 3335-3347 (matching cited range); per-table loop with a fast MySQL TSV path (`import_mysql_tsv_table`, 3375-3403) vs. a generic chunk-by-chunk `insert_rows` path with per-chunk progress events (3405-3433); session-tuning restore, post-load DDL (3458-3476), row-count verification (3482), view import (3486-3497), and report serialization/write (3498-3533).
findings.recommendation: Extract at least: `fn prepare_import_target(mode, tables, adapter, target_schema) -> Result<(), String>` for the FK-preflight + drop-all-then-create-all sequencing (3335-3347); `fn import_table_rows(adapter, table, table_manifest, data_format, compression, threads, ...) -> Result<(u64, u64), String>` for the per-table TSV-fast-path-vs-generic-chunk branch (3369-3445); and `fn finalize_dump_import(...) -> Result<Value, String>` for post-load DDL + verification + view import + report writing (3458-3533), leaving `dump_import` as a thin sequence of these calls plus its top validation block.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate as written; no correction needed.

findings.title: dump_one_table and dump_one_mysql_table duplicate table-dir/progress-event scaffolding around different row-fetch strategies
findings.detail: Verified both functions in full. `dump_one_table` (2945-3064) and `dump_one_mysql_table` (3066-3214) both: emit an identical `table_progress`/`status: dumping` event (2974-2981 vs 3079-3086); build the same `format!("{:04}_{}", index + 1, safe_dump_component(&table.name))` path and `fs::create_dir_all` it (2982-2985 vs 3087-3090); run a chunk loop tracking rows/chunks (using `bounded_dump_chunk_limit` in both, 2996-3042 vs 3111-3192); and finish with an identical `table_progress`/`status: completed` event plus the same `DumpTableManifest` construction (3044-3063 vs 3194-3213). The chunk-loop bodies genuinely differ as described (generic adapter reads + `write_dump_rows` vs. raw MySQL `query_iter` + TSV fast-write), confirming the finding's framing that only the ~15-20-line surrounding scaffolding is copy-pasted, not the core logic.
findings.recommendation: Factor the shared scaffolding into a helper, e.g. `fn run_table_dump_loop<F>(table: &NormalizedTable, index: usize, table_total: usize, output_path: &Path, request_id: Option<String>, emit: &mut F, mut fetch_next_chunk: impl FnMut(u64) -> Result<Option<ChunkOutcome>, String>) -> Result<(DumpTableManifest, u64, u64), String>` (where `ChunkOutcome { rows: u64, chunk_name: String, checksum: String }`), and have both `dump_one_table` and `dump_one_mysql_table` supply only their chunk-fetch closure.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate as written; no correction needed.

findings.title: Dump pipeline functions repeat the same 9-11 positional parameters across many call sites
findings.detail: Verified all four cited signatures directly. `dump_one_table` (2945-2956) has 11 positional params (adapter, endpoint, output_path, table, index, table_total, chunk_size, data_format, compression, request_id, emit). `dump_mysql_table_parallel_ranges` (2526-2537) has 11 params (endpoint, output_path, table, index, table_total, chunk_size, data_format, compression, threads, request_id, emit). `dump_tables_sequential` (1925-1934) has 9 params. Corrected count: `spawn_dump_table_worker` (2878-2889) actually has **11** parameters, not 10 as originally stated ‚ÄĒ I recounted the definition directly (endpoint, output_path, table, index, table_total, chunk_size, data_format, compression, range_threads, request_id, sender = 11 lines/params). Correspondingly, the three call sites at 2109-2121, 2143-2155, and 2164-2176 each pass **11** arguments, not 10 ‚ÄĒ verified each call site line-by-line. The core claim (same-typed `&str`/`String` params for data_format/compression and `usize` for chunk_size/table_total creating swap risk, with the three call sites repeating an identical argument sequence) still holds; only the numeric count needed correction.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a `DumpJobContext` (or `DumpOptions`) struct ‚ÄĒ placed near the existing `DumpParallelLimits` struct ‚ÄĒ bundling `endpoint: Endpoint`, `output_path: PathBuf`, `chunk_size: usize`, `data_format: String`, `compression: String`, and `request_id: Option<String>`, and pass it by shared reference (`&DumpJobContext`) instead of six separate positional args. This cuts `spawn_dump_table_worker`, `dump_one_table`, and `dump_mysql_table_parallel_ranges` down to 5-6 parameters (context + table-specific args) and removes the same-typed-argument ordering risk at the three duplicated call sites in `dump_tables_parallel`.
findings.verification_note: Finding is real but contained a minor factual error (10 vs actual 11 params/args for spawn_dump_table_worker and its call sites) ‚ÄĒ corrected in detail/recommendation above. Severity medium is appropriate given the repeated same-typed-parameter pattern across the dump pipeline.

findings.title: Dump/import thread-count default '8' is a bare repeated literal instead of a named constant
findings.detail: Verified both call sites exactly. `dump_run` line 1640: `.unwrap_or(8)` following `.map(|value| value as usize)` on `request.payload.get("threads")`. `dump_import` line 3273: identical `.unwrap_or(8)` pattern on the same payload field. Confirmed the file declares other dump tunables as named `const`s elsewhere (e.g. `MYSQL_DUMP_TARGET_BYTES_PER_CHUNK`, `MYSQL_DUMP_ZSTD_LEVEL`), so this bare `8` is inconsistent with the surrounding style and has no single source of truth tying the two defaults together.
findings.recommendation: Add `const DEFAULT_DUMP_THREADS: usize = 8;` near the other dump constants (alongside `MYSQL_DUMP_TARGET_BYTES_PER_CHUNK`) and reference it via `.unwrap_or(DEFAULT_DUMP_THREADS)` at both the line 1640 call site in `dump_run` and the line 3273 call site in `dump_import`.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate as written; no correction needed.

findings.title: dump_import_row_progress_event's 12-parameter, same-typed-Option signature invites argument-reuse mistakes
findings.detail: Verified the definition (1568-1618, exactly 12 params: request_id, table, table_rows_done, table_rows_total, overall_rows_before, overall_rows_total, chunk_rows, chunks_done: Option<u64>, chunks_total: Option<u64>, chunk_index: Option<u64>, load_ms: Option<u64>, strategy) and the call site (3419-3432). Confirmed the local variable `chunk_index` (from the `for chunk_index in 1..=table_manifest.chunks` loop at line 3405) is indeed passed twice: as `chunks_done` (`Some(chunk_index)` at 3427) and again as `chunk_index` itself (`Some(chunk_index)` at 3429). This is logically correct here (the loop is strictly sequential and 1-indexed, so 'chunks done so far' equals the current 1-based chunk index), but is genuinely easy to misread as a copy-paste error when scanning the call, exactly as described.
findings.recommendation: Replace the trailing four `Option<u64>` parameters with a small struct, e.g. `struct ChunkProgress { chunks_done: Option<u64>, chunks_total: Option<u64>, chunk_index: Option<u64>, load_ms: Option<u64> }`, constructed with named fields at the call site (`ChunkProgress { chunks_done: Some(chunk_index), chunks_total: Some(table_manifest.chunks), chunk_index: Some(chunk_index), load_ms: None }`) so the double-use of `chunk_index` for two different fields is self-documenting rather than positional.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate as written; no correction needed.

findings.title: Identical 10-line MigrationIssue-for-validation-failure block duplicated verbatim in oneclick_run_streaming and oneclick_validate
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read. In `oneclick_run_streaming` (lines 5656-5668) the code builds a fallback `MigrationIssue` when `inspect_live(&state.endpoint)` fails during the validation phase: `Err(err) => vec![MigrationIssue { issue_type: None, severity: "error".to_string(), location: "validation".to_string(), message: err, suggestion: "Check the database connection and rerun validation.".to_string(), blocking: true, table_name: None, column_name: None }]`. The exact same 10-line literal (identical field values, identical suggestion string) is repeated in `oneclick_validate` at lines 5978-5987, called on `inspect_live(&endpoint)` failure in that function. Both call sites follow the identical pattern: `inspect_live(...)` -> `Ok` maps through `oneclick_issues_from_inspection`, `Err` builds this synthetic issue.
findings.recommendation: Extract `fn issues_from_inspect_result(result: Result<InspectionResult, String>) -> Vec<MigrationIssue>` that performs the Ok -> oneclick_issues_from_inspection / Err -> single validation-error MigrationIssue mapping, and call it from both `oneclick_run_streaming` (replacing lines 5656-5668) and `oneclick_validate` (replacing lines 5976-5988).
findings.verification_note: Confirmed exact byte-for-byte match at the stated line ranges (5656-5668 and 5978-5987). Both call sites and control flow described are accurate.

findings.title: MySQL import chunk functions each take 7-11 positional parameters, with a shared parameter cluster repeated verbatim across most of them
findings.detail: Confirmed: `import_mysql_tsv_table` (line 3669) takes 11 params: `endpoint, conn, input_path, table, table_manifest, compression, threads, request_id, overall_rows_before, overall_rows_total, emit`. `import_mysql_tsv_table_insert_fallback` (line 4135) takes 9: `conn, input_path, table, table_manifest, compression, request_id, overall_rows_before, overall_rows_total, emit`. `import_mysql_tsv_table_parallel` (line 4203) takes 10: `endpoint, input_path, table, table_manifest, compression, threads, request_id, overall_rows_before, overall_rows_total, emit`. These three share the identical 6-param cluster `table, table_manifest, compression, request_id, overall_rows_before, overall_rows_total` verbatim. `spawn_mysql_import_chunk_worker` (line 4330-4369) takes 7 params (`endpoint, input_path, table, table_path, chunk_index, compression, sender`) but only shares 2 of the 6 (`table`, `compression`) with the other three -- it receives `table_path: String` (derived from `table_manifest.path`) rather than the full `table_manifest`, and does not take `request_id`/`overall_rows_before`/`overall_rows_total` at all, so the original claim that 'all sharing the identical 6-parameter cluster' applies to all four functions is not accurate for the worker. The cited comment at lines 4341-4342 ('žõĆžĽ§ÍįÄ ŪäúŽčĚ žóÜžĚī žóįÍ≤įŪēī fk_checks/timeoutžĚī ŽąĄŽĚĹŽŹľ žěąžóąŽč§') is real and confirms a duplicated-setup bug already occurred once in this area.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a `struct MysqlImportChunkContext<'a> { table: &'a NormalizedTable, table_manifest: &'a DumpTableManifest, compression: &'a str, request_id: Option<String>, overall_rows_before: u64, overall_rows_total: u64 }` (Clone-able) and thread it through `import_mysql_tsv_table`, `import_mysql_tsv_table_insert_fallback`, and `import_mysql_tsv_table_parallel` (the three functions that genuinely share all 6 fields). For `spawn_mysql_import_chunk_worker`, keep its narrower signature as-is or introduce a separate smaller struct (e.g. `MysqlImportWorkerContext { table, compression }`) rather than forcing it to take the full context -- do not claim it needs the same 6-field struct.
findings.verification_note: Parameter counts and the shared cluster among the first three functions are confirmed accurate. Corrected: spawn_mysql_import_chunk_worker does NOT share the full 6-parameter cluster as originally claimed (only 2 of 6 fields overlap), so the recommendation was adjusted to not force that function into the same context struct. Also corrected line_end from 4338 (mid-signature) to 4369 (actual end of spawn_mysql_import_chunk_worker's body) since 4338 only covered the function's opening signature line.

findings.title: oneclick_run_streaming is a 214-line orchestrator mixing preflight, analysis, recommendation, a 4-branch dry-run/execute decision returning a 6-tuple, validation, and final reporting
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read (lines 5480-5693, 214 lines). The function inlines the entire One-Click pipeline: preflight state handling (5487-5513), analysis summary (5515-5526), recommendation generation (5528-5550), then an if/else-if/else chain (5567-5625) that computes `(success_count, fail_count, skip_count, disallowed_fix_attempts, applied_fixes, execution_log)` as a bare unnamed 6-tuple across four different scenarios (dry_run at 5574, disallowed fixes at 5583, empty actions at 5592, real execute via `LiveAdapter::connect` at 5601 with its own nested Ok/Err), followed by validation (5651-5679, itself duplicating the fallback-issue block from finding #1) and final result assembly (5685-5692). Each phase is a genuinely distinct responsibility, and the 6-element unnamed tuple returned from four separate branches is easy to get subtly wrong (miscount, wrong order) if a new branch or field is ever added.
findings.recommendation: Split into phase functions (e.g. `oneclick_run_preflight_phase`, `oneclick_run_execution_phase`, `oneclick_run_validation_phase`), each returning a small typed struct instead of a raw tuple -- reuse/extend the existing `OneClickApplyOutcome` struct (already defined at line 6783 with `success_count, fail_count, log, applied_fixes`; would need `skip_count` and `disallowed_fix_attempts` added) for the execution phase's return type, and have `oneclick_run_streaming` just sequence the phase calls and emit events.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate: function spans 5480-5693 (214 lines, close to the originally cited 213), the described 4-branch tuple computation at 5567-5625 matches exactly, and OneClickApplyOutcome (line 6783) exists and is a concrete, reusable target for the recommendation.

findings.title: oneclick_apply_actions and oneclick_dry_run_preview_fixes duplicate the same per-step classification logic, and the real-apply path has an extra sql_template validation the preview path lacks
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read. Both `oneclick_apply_actions` (6797-6908) and `oneclick_dry_run_preview_fixes` (6910-6983) iterate `payload.get("steps")`, extract `issue_type`/`selected_option`/`strategy`, skip on `manual`/`skip` (6823 and 6936), branch on `issue_type == "charset_issue" && strategy == "charset_collation_fk_safe"` calling the same `oneclick_charset_fk_safe_option_from_payload` (6828 and 6941), and on `issue_type == "deprecated_engine" && strategy == "engine_innodb"` calling the same `oneclick_apply_step_table` (6869 and 6956), otherwise pushing to `disallowed`. The real-apply path additionally checks for a `sql_template` mismatch (lines 6878-6887: if the step's `sql_template` is present, non-empty, and differs from the computed ALTER TABLE SQL, the action is pushed to `disallowed` with a `:sql_mismatch` suffix) -- `oneclick_dry_run_preview_fixes` has no equivalent check, so a step that the dry-run preview shows as a plannable fix could actually be rejected as disallowed when really applied.
findings.recommendation: Extract a shared classifier, e.g. `fn classify_oneclick_step(step: &Value, schema: &str) -> OneClickStepClassification` returning an enum (`Skip`, `Disallowed(String)`, `Charset(Value)`, `Engine{table, sql}`) that performs the sql_template check as part of the Engine branch, and have both `oneclick_apply_actions` and `oneclick_dry_run_preview_fixes` build their respective outputs (real `OneClickApplyAction` vs. preview JSON with `dry_run: true`) from the same classification result so the sql_template validation can't silently diverge between preview and real execution again.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate at the stated line range (6797-6983). The sql_template check divergence (6878-6887 present only in oneclick_apply_actions) is real and verified by direct comparison of both function bodies.

findings.title: Identical ad-hoc {"event": "error", "request_id": ..., "message": err} JSON literal repeated across at least 6 functions
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read at all 6 cited locations: `inspect` (4783-4787), `oneclick_preflight` (5714-5718), `oneclick_analyze` (5742-5746), `oneclick_derive_charset_contracts` (5821-5826), `oneclick_apply_fixes` (5923-5928), and `oneclick_validate` (5999-6003). Each is the identical 5-line `json!({"event": "error", "request_id": request.request_id, "message": err})` construction. This is a simple, low-risk literal (only 3 fixed fields, no divergent logic like finding #4), so it's a straightforward style/DRY nit rather than a bug-prone pattern.
findings.recommendation: Add a small helper next to the existing `phase_event` helper, e.g. `fn error_event(request: &Request, message: impl Into<String>) -> Value { json!({"event": "error", "request_id": request.request_id, "message": message.into()}) }`, and replace all 6 call sites with `events.push(error_event(request, err))`.
findings.verification_note: All 6 line ranges confirmed byte-for-byte identical. Downgraded severity from medium to low: unlike the other duplication findings in this batch, there is no logic divergence risk here (only 3 fixed literal fields, no branching), so the impact is purely cosmetic/maintenance, matching a low-severity DRY nit rather than a medium risk.

findings.title: Three independent hand-rolled SQL comment scanners on the View-definition sanitization/validation path, with inconsistent comment-dialect coverage
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read. `strip_leading_comments_and_parens` (4612-4642) skips `--` line comments, `#` line comments, and `/* */` block comments. `mysql_definition_has_residual_definer` (correct location: lines 5271-5304, not 4272-5304 as originally stated) has its own byte-by-byte comment-stripping loop at 5277-5295 that only recognizes `--` and `/* */` -- it does NOT treat `#` as a comment start. `validate_single_view_statement` (5315-5440) has its own comment-skipping match arms at 5361-5375 that likewise only recognize `--` and `/* */`, not `#`. All three exist specifically to reason about untrusted/variable View-definition SQL (residual DEFINER detection and multi-statement rejection for the Postgres batch_execute path per the doc comment at 5308-5311), so having three separately-maintained scanners with different comment coverage is a real DRY/consistency risk: whoever next needs to add a dialect quirk (or fix a comment-parsing edge case) has to remember to touch three places. Note: the practical security impact of the current `#`-comment gap is limited -- since neither residual-DEFINER detection nor the multi-statement validator treats `#...` as a comment, unrecognized `#` content is scanned as literal SQL, which if anything makes the validator MORE conservative (more likely to reject) rather than creating a bypass; it is a maintainability/consistency issue rather than an active exploit path.
findings.recommendation: Extract a single `fn skip_sql_comment(bytes: &[u8], i: usize) -> Option<usize>` (returning the index just past a comment if `bytes[i..]` starts one, recognizing `--`, `#`, and `/* */` consistently) and have all three call sites (`strip_leading_comments_and_parens`, `mysql_definition_has_residual_definer`, `validate_single_view_statement`) use it, so 'what counts as a comment' is defined once.
findings.verification_note: Corrected line_start for mysql_definition_has_residual_definer from the originally stated 4272 to the actual 5271 (typo in original finding -- function does not exist at 4272). All other line citations (4612-4642, 5277-5295, 5315-5440, 5361-5375) confirmed exact. Added a caveat that the current inconsistency is a maintainability risk rather than a demonstrated exploit, since the `#` gap only makes the validators more conservative, not less.

findings.title: inspect_mysql and inspect_postgresql duplicate the same 5-step per-table inspection sequence for each engine
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read. `inspect_mysql` (4828-4921) and `inspect_postgresql` (4967-5080) both: list table names via `inspect_tables_sql(engine)`, then for each table run `inspect_columns_sql`, `inspect_keys_sql`, `inspect_foreign_keys_sql`, `inspect_indexes_sql` (each already parameterized by engine string), apply the same `apply_key_flags`/`group_indexes`/`group_foreign_keys` helpers, and push a `NormalizedTable`. The control flow (loop over tables -> 4 sub-queries -> assemble NormalizedTable -> plus unsupported-objects lookup) is identical between the two functions, even though the underlying driver call styles differ (`conn.exec_map` closures for mysql vs `client.query` + manual `row.get(n)` for postgresql).
findings.recommendation: Introduce a `trait InspectAdapter { fn table_names(&mut self, schema: &str) -> Result<Vec<(String, Option<String>)>, String>; fn columns(&mut self, schema: &str, table: &str) -> Result<Vec<NormalizedColumn>, String>; fn keys(...); fn foreign_keys(...); fn indexes(...); }`, implement it once for a MySQL wrapper (over `mysql::PooledConn`) and once for a PostgreSQL wrapper (over `postgres::Client`), and factor the shared per-table loop into one generic `fn inspect_generic<A: InspectAdapter>(adapter: &mut A, schema: &str) -> Result<InspectionResult, String>` that both `inspect_mysql` and `inspect_postgresql` call after constructing their respective adapter.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate at the stated line ranges. The recommendation is a real but non-trivial refactor given the differing driver APIs (mysql exec_map vs postgres row.get by index) -- feasible but should be scoped as a moderate-effort task, not a quick fix.

findings.title: Bare MySQL error-code and session-timeout literals embedded directly in strings instead of named constants
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read. `is_mysql_local_infile_disabled_error` (line 4001, the check itself at line 4003) does `lower.contains("3948")` -- a raw MySQL error code with no named constant, only understandable via the neighboring `contains("loading local data is disabled")` string check. Separately, `mysql_import_session_tuning_sql` (lines 4066-4089) hardcodes `net_read_timeout = 600`, `net_write_timeout = 600`, and `wait_timeout = 28800` directly inside SQL string literals at lines 4084-4086. Notably, the file already establishes a convention of top-level named constants for exactly this kind of tuning value (e.g. `const MYSQL_INSERT_FALLBACK_BATCH_BYTES`, `const MYSQL_DUMP_ZSTD_LEVEL` at lines 15-27), so these two spots are inconsistent with the codebase's own established pattern.
findings.recommendation: Define named constants near the existing MYSQL_* constants at the top of the file (lines 15-27): `const MYSQL_ERR_LOCAL_INFILE_DISABLED: &str = "3948";` and `const MYSQL_IMPORT_NET_TIMEOUT_SECS: u32 = 600; const MYSQL_IMPORT_WAIT_TIMEOUT_SECS: u32 = 28800;`, then interpolate them into `is_mysql_local_infile_disabled_error`'s `.contains(...)` check and into the `format!`/string-literal construction in `mysql_import_session_tuning_sql`.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed accurate. Adjusted line range slightly (4003-4086 -> 4001-4089) to bound the actual function definitions (is_mysql_local_infile_disabled_error starts at 4001; mysql_import_session_tuning_sql ends at 4089) rather than only the inner literal lines. Strengthened the recommendation by noting the file already has an established MYSQL_* constant convention (lines 15-27) that these two spots should follow.

findings.title: Text-column projection SQL (binary hex-encode / postgres cast / mysql passthrough) is copy-pasted verbatim in 3 functions
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct read: `select_chunk_text_sql` (10336-10397), `select_chunk_text_after_key_sql` (10399-10473), and `select_chunk_text_range_sql` (10475-10527) each contain a byte-for-byte identical 28-line `.map(|column| { if is_binary_type(...) && engine == "postgresql" { encode(...) } else if is_binary_type(...) { HEX(...) } else if engine == "postgresql" { ...::text } else if engine == "mysql" { quote_ident(...) } else { CAST(... AS CHAR) } })` closure. This is correctness-relevant logic (per-engine/per-type SQL casting for cross-engine dump), not cosmetic duplication -- a future fix (e.g. adding a new binary/geometry type, or a postgres version quirk) risks being applied to only 1 or 2 of the 3 copies.
findings.recommendation: Extract `fn projected_text_columns_sql(engine: &str, table: &NormalizedTable) -> String` containing the column-projection match (reusing existing `is_binary_type` at line 10825 and `quote_ident` at line 11494), and have all three functions call it before appending their own WHERE/ORDER BY/LIMIT clauses. Removes ~84 duplicated lines and guarantees single-point maintenance for type-casting rules.

findings.title: migrate_with_adapters_reporting mixes DDL generation, dependency-ordered per-table copy loop, keyset/offset pagination selection, and cancellation logic in one ~168-line function
findings.detail: Confirmed: the function does (1) create_only preflight (8177-8186), (2) dependency ordering + resume-state hydration (8188-8194), (3) schema DDL generation with its own error path (8195-8215), (4) a nested loop over tables (8217-8310, ~94 lines) that decides keyset-vs-offset pagination, calls `target.create_table`, loops chunked reads/inserts with progress events, and checks `cancel_after_chunks`, then (5) applies post-load DDL (8312-8326). The inner per-table loop body is the real unit of 'copy one table' but is inlined rather than named.
findings.recommendation: Extract the per-table body (create table + keyset/offset decision + chunked read/insert loop + progress emission, lines 8217-8310) into a helper such as `fn copy_table_rows<S: MigrationAdapter, T: MigrationAdapter>(table, table_ddl, state, state_index, source, target, chunk_size, cancel_after_chunks, rows_copied, chunks_copied, on_event) -> Result<TableCopyOutcome, MigrationResult>`. `migrate_with_adapters_reporting` then reads as: validate -> build ddl -> for each table call copy_table_rows -> apply_post_load_ddl.

findings.title: verify_with_adapters_reporting mixes row-count checks, digest-based comparison, and keyset-ordered row comparison in one ~168-line function
findings.detail: Confirmed: the function loops over `schema.tables` (8444-8601) and for each table fetches counts from both sides (8450-8473, with per-side error handling), then branches into a whole-table digest comparison path when there's no key column (8492-8532), or an entirely different keyset-based paging loop with its own error handling and progress emission (8535-8595) when there is a key column. Both branches duplicate similar table_progress/row_progress emission bookkeeping around fundamentally different comparison strategies.
findings.recommendation: Split into two named helpers, `fn verify_table_by_digest(source, target, table, chunk_size, emit) -> Vec<Value>` and `fn verify_table_by_keyset(source, target, table, key_columns, chunk_size, total_rows, emit) -> Vec<Value>`, each returning mismatches, and have the outer loop do the count check then dispatch based on `key_columns.is_empty()`. Turns the 168-line function into a short dispatcher plus two independently testable strategies.

findings.title: Identical source/target endpoint-resolution boilerplate (including a defensive unreachable!()) repeated 4 times across migrate_streaming and verify
findings.detail: Confirmed exact duplication at lines 7599-7611 and 7612-7624 in `migrate_streaming`, and again at 7753-7767 and 7768-7782 in `verify`: `match request.payload.get("source"/"target").map(endpoint_from_value).transpose() { Ok(Some(endpoint)) => endpoint, Ok(None) => unreachable!(), Err(err) => { emit/events.push(error); return; } }`, differing only in the payload key, the error-reporting call (`emit(...)` vs `events.push(...)`), and the return value shape. Also confirmed: the `unreachable!()` is currently safe only because both callers have an outer `payload.get("source").is_some() && payload.get("target").is_some()` guard (line 7590 and 7751) before reaching this code -- it is a landmine that would panic if that invariant is ever broken by a future refactor.
findings.recommendation: Extract a shared `fn required_endpoint(payload: &Value, key: &str) -> Result<Endpoint, String>` that does the `.get(key)` + `endpoint_from_value` + `?` chain internally, turning the 'missing key' case into a real `Err(...)` instead of `unreachable!()` (removing the panic-path landmine entirely). Callers then do `let source_endpoint = match required_endpoint(&request.payload, "source") { Ok(e) => e, Err(err) => { emit(...); return; } };`, cutting 4 near-identical ~13-line blocks to 4 one-line calls plus their (already-differing) error-reporting arms.

findings.title: migration_error_result requires a full &NormalizedTable just to read its .name, forcing 2 dummy-table fabrications in migrate_with_adapters_reporting
findings.detail: Confirmed: `migration_error_result` (8336-8359) only ever uses `table.name.clone()` as the issue's `location`. Yet in `migrate_with_adapters_reporting`, lines 8201-8212 and 8314-8325 each fabricate a fake `NormalizedTable { name: "schema_ddl"/"post_data_ddl".to_string(), columns: Vec::new(), indexes: Vec::new(), foreign_keys: Vec::new(), table_collation: None }` purely to satisfy the function signature when the error isn't actually about a specific table row. (The other 4 call sites at 8231, 8254, 8276 already have a real `&NormalizedTable` on hand, so this only affects the 2 fabrication sites.) Corrected line_start/line_end from the originally-reported 8195-8326 (which spanned unrelated code in between) to the actual function definition, since that's the concrete edit target; the two fabrication call sites are 8201-8212 and 8314-8325.
findings.recommendation: Change `migration_error_result`'s signature from `table: &NormalizedTable` to `location: &str`, and update its one internal use (`location: table.name.clone()` -> `location: location.to_string()`). Callers then pass `"schema_ddl"` / `"post_data_ddl"` string literals directly at 8201-8212/8314-8325, and `&table.name` at the other 3 call sites (8231, 8254, 8276), eliminating both dummy-object constructions.
findings.verification_note: Confirmed the core claim (only .name is used, 2 dummy fabrications exist). Corrected the reported line range from 8195-8326 (which included ~130 lines of unrelated per-table-loop code in between) to 8336-8359, the actual migration_error_result function body, since that is the precise location the recommended signature change applies to.

findings.title: One-click fix payload shape is chosen via a raw string-literal comparison instead of a typed variant
findings.detail: Confirmed exact match: `oneclick_applied_fix_payload` (7026-7063) special-cases the entire output shape with `if action.issue_type == "charset_issue" && action.strategy == "charset_collation_fk_safe" { ... }` (building a payload with `tables`/`target_charset`/`fk_order`/etc.), falling through to a generic single-table payload otherwise. Grep confirms this `issue_type == ... && strategy == ...` string-comparison pattern is not an isolated one-off: it recurs at lines 6823/6827, 6936/6940, and 6955 elsewhere in the file, so this is a recognized recurring dispatch style in this codebase, not a hypothetical future risk.
findings.recommendation: Introduce `enum OneClickPayloadShape { CharsetCollationFkSafe, SingleTable }`, compute it once via a small classifier `fn classify_oneclick_payload_shape(action: &OneClickApplyAction) -> OneClickPayloadShape`, and `match` on it in `oneclick_applied_fix_payload` instead of the ad hoc `&&` string comparison, so the compiler flags missing cases when a new multi-table strategy is added.

findings.title: Unexplained 4_096 byte threshold in learned_mysql_range_chunk_size
findings.detail: Confirmed exact match: `if avg_row_bytes >= 4_096 && profile.chunk_rows >= byte_target_size { ... }` (line 9382) uses a bare literal `4_096` to decide whether to trust a previously learned chunk-row profile over the freshly computed byte-target size, with no comment or named constant, unlike the neighboring `sequential_mysql_chunk_size` (9367-9371) which has a 6-line doc comment explaining its reasoning. Confirmed the file already has a block of named `MYSQL_DUMP_*`/`MYSQL_PK_RANGE_*` constants at lines 17-27, so adding one more there is consistent with existing style.
findings.recommendation: Hoist `4_096` into a named constant alongside the existing constants at lines 17-27, e.g. `const LEARNED_PROFILE_LARGE_ROW_BYTES: u64 = 4_096;`, with a one-line comment on why large-row tables (>=4KB avg) should prefer the learned chunk-row profile over the freshly computed byte-target size.

findings.title: is_safe_column_type is a ~198-line hand-rolled byte-level parser doing many distinct jobs in one function
findings.detail: Re-read and confirmed exactly as described: fn is_safe_column_type(type_name: &str) -> bool (lines 11119-11316) is a mini recursive-descent parser over a raw mutable byte index `i`: (1) base identifier scan (11128-11134), (2) an optional '(' ... ')' argument group that branches into a quoted string list for enum/set values with its own escape-handling inner loop (11142-11182) vs a numeric list (11184-11205), and (3) a trailing modifier loop (11216-11313) with per-keyword sub-parsers for `varying` (optional length group, 11237-11262), `with`/`without` ... `time zone` (11263-11276), `charset`/`collate` (11277-11288), and `character set` (11289-11310). Nesting reaches function -> `if '('` (11137) -> `if quote` (11142) -> `loop` (11144) -> inner `loop` (11149) -> `match` (11158), i.e. 4-5 levels deep. Per the comment at 11112-11118 this is the fail-closed guard against DDL/column-definition injection, so its correctness is security-critical, yet the size/nesting make it hard to review or safely modify.
findings.recommendation: Split into small, independently testable parser helpers, e.g. `fn parse_base_ident(bytes: &[u8], i: usize) -> Option<usize>`, `fn parse_quoted_string_list(bytes: &[u8], i: usize) -> Option<usize>`, `fn parse_numeric_list(bytes: &[u8], i: usize) -> Option<usize>`, `fn parse_modifier_word(s: &str, i: usize) -> Option<(&str, usize)>`, plus a small dispatch loop over modifiers, each returning the advanced index (or None on failure) so `is_safe_column_type` becomes a short orchestration function.

findings.title: Cryptic single/double-letter index variables (ds, ws, ws2, is2, ss) reused for different meanings inside is_safe_column_type
findings.detail: Confirmed all five locals exist as described, inside the parser from the previous finding: `let ds = i;` at line 11186 (numeric-list-group start) and again at line 11247 (varying-length-group start, unrelated group); `let ws = i;` at line 11223 (modifier word start); `let ws2 = i;` at line 11268 (the `with`/`without ... time`/`zone` word-start check, a distinct scope from `ws`); `let is2 = i;` at line 11281 (charset/collate value start) and again at line 11303 (character-set-name value start, a different sibling match arm); `let ss = i;` at line 11293 (checking the literal word "set" after "character"). None of these names convey what they capture; a reader must trace byte-index arithmetic through each match arm to recover the meaning.
findings.recommendation: Rename to intention-revealing, scope-specific names, e.g. `numeric_list_start` (11186), `varying_length_start` (11247), `modifier_word_start` (11223), `time_zone_word_start` (11268), `charset_or_collate_value_start` (11281), `set_keyword_start` (11293), `character_set_name_start` (11303). If the is_safe_column_type split from the long-function finding is done first, most of these become locals inside small dedicated parsers and naturally get clearer names without needing abbreviations.

findings.title: insert_rows_literal_sql and insert_rows_literal_sql_for_table duplicate the same INSERT-building logic
findings.detail: Confirmed: both functions (10580-10614 and 10616-10653) build `column_sql` via `columns.iter().map(|c| quote_ident(engine, c)).collect::<Vec<_>>().join(", ")`, then `values_sql` via the same nested-map/join pattern producing `(v1, v2, ...)` per row with `Value::Object` vs fallback-to-NULL handling, then the same `format!("INSERT INTO {} ({}) VALUES {}", ...)`. The only real differences are (a) column source (`&[String]` vs `NormalizedTable.columns`) and (b) the per-value literal function (`sql_literal` vs `sql_literal_for_column`). A fix to one (e.g. NULL handling, quoting) can be missed in the other.
findings.recommendation: Extract a shared private helper `fn insert_values_sql(rows: &[Value], column_names: &[&str], literal_for: impl Fn(&str, &Value) -> String) -> String` and have both public functions build their column-name list plus a closure over `sql_literal`/`sql_literal_for_column`, delegating row/value formatting and the final `INSERT INTO ... VALUES ...` assembly to the shared helper.

findings.title: sql_literal_for_column repeats the identical mysql-json/mysql/fallback branch twice in the same function
findings.detail: Confirmed verbatim: inside the `if let Value::String(text) = value` block, lines 10804-10809 are `if target_engine == "mysql" && is_json_type(&source_type) { return mysql_json_literal(value); } if target_engine == "mysql" { return mysql_sql_literal(value); }`. Immediately after that block, lines 10811-10817 repeat the exact same three-way decision (`is_json_type` check, plain mysql check, then `sql_literal(value)` fallback) for the non-String path. Since the PostgreSQL String case already returns unconditionally at 10801-10803, this is the same tail logic duplicated for the String and non-String paths instead of shared.
findings.recommendation: Factor the shared tail into `fn mysql_or_generic_literal(target_engine: &str, source_type: &str, value: &Value) -> String { if target_engine == "mysql" && is_json_type(source_type) { return mysql_json_literal(value); } if target_engine == "mysql" { return mysql_sql_literal(value); } sql_literal(value) }`, and call it once at the end of the String-specific branch (after the postgres-only special cases) and once for the non-String fallback, removing the duplicated 3-line block at 10811-10816.

findings.title: MySQL and PostgreSQL value/DDL logic is interleaved via repeated engine-name string comparisons instead of per-engine dispatch
findings.detail: Confirmed the pattern in all three cited functions: `copy_csv_field_for_column` (10737-10765, not 10775 as originally stated ‚ÄĒ corrected line_start to include it), `sql_literal_for_column` (10775-10818), and `map_default_literal` (11334-11387) each independently chain `if target_engine == "postgresql" && ...` / `if target_engine == "mysql" && ...` mixed with source-type checks (`source_type.starts_with("tinyint(1)")`, `matches!(source_type.as_str(), "boolean" | "bool")`, `is_json_type`, `is_binary_type`) to re-derive the same engine-specific quirks (tinyint(1)<->boolean casting, JSON handling, bit literals, string escaping) in each function body. Note: the stated 10737-11387 span is wide and includes ~500 lines of unrelated code in between (schema-introspection SQL builders, `is_valid_mysql_collation_ident`/`is_safe_column_type`, `apply_key_flags`, `group_indexes`/`group_foreign_keys`, `generate_table_ddl`) that do NOT exhibit this pattern ‚ÄĒ the actual evidence is confined to the three named functions, not the whole range.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a small dialect abstraction, e.g. `trait SqlDialect { fn literal_for_column(&self, source_type: &str, value: &Value) -> String; fn csv_field(&self, source_type: &str, value: &Value) -> String; fn default_literal(&self, default_value: &str, source_type: &str) -> String; }` with `MysqlDialect`/`PostgresqlDialect` impls, and have `copy_csv_field_for_column`, `sql_literal_for_column`, and `map_default_literal` become thin callers that pick the dialect once via `target_engine` and delegate. This is a design-clarity improvement rather than a bug fix, so it's reasonable to treat as optional/medium priority rather than blocking.
findings.verification_note: Original line_start (10775) excluded copy_csv_field_for_column (10737-10765), which the finding's own detail text cites as evidence ‚ÄĒ corrected line_start to 10737. Also note the 650-line span is not uniformly affected; only the three named functions show the pattern, the rest of the range is unrelated code. Kept severity at medium and verified=true because the core observation (repeated inline engine dispatch instead of a dialect abstraction) is accurate.

findings.title: generate_table_ddl mixes column-DDL building, primary-key collection, and MySQL collation-suffix security validation in one function
findings.detail: Confirmed: generate_table_ddl (11023-11099, 77 lines) does three distinct jobs: (1) per-column loop building type-mapped/defaulted/nullable/auto-increment DDL fragments with an early `return None` via `is_safe_column_type` (11027-11062); (2) collecting `primary_keys` and emitting a `PRIMARY KEY (...)` line (11064-11066); (3) a same-engine-MySQL-only branch deriving a `table_suffix` for `COLLATE=...` with its own fail-closed validation via `is_valid_mysql_collation_ident` and another early `return None` (11071-11091). These are independently reusable/testable concerns currently reachable only as one function.
findings.recommendation: Split into `fn column_ddl_lines(table: &NormalizedTable, source: &str, target: &str) -> Option<(Vec<String>, Vec<String>)>` (DDL lines + primary key column list, covering job 1+2) and `fn mysql_table_collation_suffix(source: &str, target: &str, table: &NormalizedTable) -> Option<String>` (covering job 3), then have generate_table_ddl call both and assemble the final CREATE TABLE string ‚ÄĒ keeping the security-relevant is_safe_column_type/is_valid_mysql_collation_ident checks each in a small, focused unit.

findings.title: sql_literal and mysql_sql_literal duplicate four of five match arms
findings.detail: Confirmed byte-for-byte: sql_literal (10859-10875) and mysql_sql_literal (10877-10891) both match on Value::Null => "NULL", Value::Bool => TRUE/FALSE, Value::Number => v.to_string() ‚ÄĒ identical in both ‚ÄĒ with only Value::String and Value::Array/Object arms differing (`format!("'{}'", ...)` + `'`-doubling vs `mysql_string_literal(...)`).
findings.recommendation: Extract `fn generic_sql_literal(value: &Value, escape: impl Fn(&str) -> String) -> String` that handles Null/Bool/Number directly and calls `escape(...)` for the String/Array/Object text; have `sql_literal` pass a closure that does `'`-doubling and `mysql_sql_literal` pass `mysql_string_literal` as the escape function.

findings.title: Unexplained length bounds (64, 512) in security-critical identifier/type validators
findings.detail: Confirmed: is_valid_mysql_collation_ident (11104-11110) hardcodes `value.len() <= 64` (line 11106), and is_safe_column_type (11119-...) hardcodes `s.len() > 512` rejection (line 11121). Neither uses a named constant. The doc comment above is_valid_mysql_collation_ident (11101-11103) does state the '1~64žěź' bound as part of describing the validation behavior, but doesn't explain why 64 specifically (e.g. MySQL's actual identifier length limit) was chosen; the 512 bound for is_safe_column_type has no comment at all justifying the number. Both are load-bearing for a fail-closed SQL-injection guard.
findings.recommendation: Introduce named constants `const MYSQL_IDENTIFIER_MAX_LEN: usize = 64;` and `const MAX_COLUMN_TYPE_LEN: usize = 512;`, each with a one-line comment stating the rationale (MySQL identifier length limit vs a defensive cap on type-string size), and reference them from both validators instead of inline literals.
findings.verification_note: Minor correction: the 64-char bound already has a nearby doc comment describing the allowed range, though it doesn't explain the numeric origin ‚ÄĒ the finding's core ask (named constants) still stands for both bounds.

findings.title: Two tests with different names are byte-for-byte identical (zero differentiated coverage)
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct re-read: `mysql_json_literal_insert_preserves_json_escape_backslashes` (lines 15940-15968) and `mysql_json_literal_uses_utf8mb4_introducer_for_unicode_json_text` (lines 15970-15997) build the identical `NormalizedTable`/`NormalizedColumn` literal for `ai_phase1_cache.result_json`, use the exact same `json_text` value (`r#"{"facts":[{"content":"Ž¨łžĄú ž†úŽ™©žĚÄ \"Ś∑•šľ§Áģ°ÁźÜŤ°®\"Ž°ú ŪĎúÍłįŽźėžĖī žěąŽč§."}]}"#`), call `insert_rows_literal_sql_for_table("mysql", &table, &[json!({"result_json": json_text})])` identically, and assert the exact same output string including the `_utf8mb4` prefix. A repo-wide grep for `_utf8mb4`/`introducer` confirms these are the ONLY two tests touching the `_utf8mb4` introducer literal path, and both exercise the same input (a string that already contains backslash-escaped quotes and non-ASCII characters together). No test isolates the introducer behavior on its own -- e.g. plain-ASCII JSON text (no escapes, no unicode) still getting `_utf8mb4`, or unicode-only text with zero embedded quotes/backslashes. The second test's name promises exactly that isolation but its body is a verbatim copy of the first.
findings.recommendation: Either delete `mysql_json_literal_uses_utf8mb4_introducer_for_unicode_json_text` as a pure duplicate, or rewrite its body to use a distinct `json_text` with no embedded backslash/quote (e.g. `r#"{"facts":[{"content":"Ž¨łžĄú ž†úŽ™©žĚÄ ŪĎúÍłįŽźėžĖī žěąŽč§"}]}"#"` or plain ASCII like `r#"{"a":1}"#`) so the assertion actually proves the `_utf8mb4` introducer is applied even when there is nothing to escape -- which is what the test name claims to verify.
findings.verification_note: Re-read lines 15900-16000 directly. Line numbers in the original finding are exactly correct (test 1 spans 15940-15968, test 2 spans 15970-15997). Body-for-body comparison confirms they are byte-for-byte identical apart from the fn name. Grep across the whole file confirms no other test covers the utf8mb4-introducer-without-escapes scenario the second test's name implies. Severity medium is appropriate: this is a test-suite integrity/coverage-illusion issue, not a production bug, but it does mean a real edge case (introducer applied to non-escaped/ASCII content) is completely unverified while the test suite implies it is.

findings.title: Widespread copy-pasted `NormalizedTable`/`NormalizedColumn`/`DumpManifest` struct literals instead of shared test builders
findings.detail: Confirmed by direct re-read of all cited ranges. The four single-bigint-PK tables are exact-line matches: `events` at 14113-14126 (test `large_numeric_pk_table_is_range_dump_candidate`, 14112), `sparse_events` at 14133-14146 (test at 14131), `dense_events` at 14159-14172 (test at 14157), `small_events` at 14181-14194 (test at 14179) -- these four differ only by the table `name` field, everything else (single bigint `id` PK column literal, empty indexes/foreign_keys/table_collation) is copy-pasted verbatim. The mysql/postgres auto-increment tables at 14953-14967 and 14969-14984 also confirmed verbatim except `type_name` (`int(11) auto_increment` vs `integer identity`). The status/enabled tables at 15002-15026 and 15029-15042 confirmed as described. The `DumpManifest` pair at 16856-16876 (`dump_manifest_with_views_round_trips`) and 16884-16900 (`empty_views_are_skipped_in_serialization`) confirmed: both build the identical 13-field literal, differing only in the `views` field (populated vs `Vec::new()`). The existing helpers cited (`empty_table`/`fk` at 14016-14020, `single_pk_table_with_collation` at 16069-16084) are confirmed present and used only for a minority of cases, proving the pattern is known but inconsistently applied.
findings.recommendation: Add two small test-only builder functions near the existing `empty_table`/`single_pk_table_with_collation` helpers: `fn single_numeric_pk_table(name: &str, type_name: &str) -> NormalizedTable` (covers the four bigint-PK cases at 14113-14194 and the mysql/postgres auto-increment pair at 14953-14984 by parameterizing name+type_name) and `fn sample_dump_manifest(views: Vec<NormalizedView>) -> DumpManifest` (covers the 16856-16900 pair by parameterizing only `views`, defaulting the other 12 fields to the values already used at 16858-16871). Update the affected ~8 tests to call these builders instead of repeating the full literal, shrinking each test to the 1-2 lines that differ.
findings.verification_note: All cited line ranges (14113-14126, 14133-14146, 14159-14172, 14181-14194, 14953-14967, 14969-14984, 15002-15026, 15029-15042, 16069-16084, 16856-16876, 16884-16900, and the 14016-14020 helper) were individually re-read and match the finding's description exactly -- no line-number corrections needed. Severity medium is fair: this is test-only duplication (no runtime/production impact) but it spans dozens of call sites in a single file slice and the fix is cheap and already has a proven pattern (existing helpers) to extend, so flagging it is worthwhile rather than trivial nitpicking.

findings.title: 17,006-line single-file god-module holding the entire tunnelforge-core service (protocol, adapters, dump, import, inspection, wizard, migration, DDL gen, and tests)
findings.detail: Re-verified directly against the file: `wc -l migration_core/src/lib.rs` confirms 17,006 lines. Structural greps confirm the claimed shape almost exactly: `^(pub )?struct ` = 35 matches, `^(pub )?enum ` = 6 matches, `^impl ` = 7 matches (MemoryAdapter, MigrationAdapter for MemoryAdapter, LiveAdapter, MigrationAdapter for LiveAdapter, CoreService, Default for CoreService, DumpParallelLimits), and `^(pub )?mod ` has exactly one hit: `mod tests {` at line 11689 ‚ÄĒ there are zero other module boundaries in the file. A full listing of every `fn`/`pub fn`/`async fn` (610 matches total) confirms every one of the 11 cluster boundaries claimed in the original finding lines up almost line-for-line with real function groupings, e.g.: MigrationAdapter trait + Memory/LiveAdapter impls end at `prepare_target_schema` (668) with the last adapter-cluster helper (`dump_manifest_consistency_metadata`) at 717, matching the claimed ~280-728 boundary; the JSONL dispatch cluster runs from `impl CoreService` (734) through `job_cancel` (1332), matching ~729-1343; the dump engine runs from `dump_run_streaming` (1344) through `dump_one_mysql_table` (3066-3216), matching ~1344-3215; the import engine runs 3216 (`dump_import_streaming`) through `load_data_local_infile_sql` (4436), matching ~3216-4453; the OneClick wizard cluster (5442-7137) contains **exactly 42** `oneclick_*`-prefixed functions, confirming the '42 oneclick_* functions' claim precisely; and the row-normalization/digest cluster is genuinely split across two ranges (8791-9146 and 11613-11688) exactly as claimed, with `canonical_row`/`canonical_value`/`row_digest`/`compare_digest_rows`/`digest_counts`/`next_table_to_copy` sitting immediately before `mod tests` at 11689. The only imprecision found: the finding's fn-count arithmetic ('347 outside mod tests, 220 test fns inside') doesn't fully reconcile against the raw 610-match total (roughly 379 matches before line 11689, ~223 after) ‚ÄĒ likely because the original count filtered out trait-signature-only lines or non-`#[test]` helper fns (e.g. `schema()`, `empty_table()`, `fk()`, and a mock `MigrationAdapter` impl at lines 11692-11785 that live inside `mod tests` but aren't themselves test cases). This is a cosmetic slip in an illustrative statistic, not a defect in the core finding. Additionally confirmed: many test functions call *private* (non-`pub`) helpers directly ‚ÄĒ e.g. `strip_mysql_definer` (private, defined at 5236) and `sanitize_view_definition` (private, defined at 5200) are called from tests at lines 16746, 16755, 16762, 16769, 16779, 16909, 16918, 16990 ‚ÄĒ which matters for the recommendation below.
findings.recommendation: Confirmed and unchanged from the original recommendation: split `migration_core/src/lib.rs` into a `migration_core/src/` module tree along the verified cluster boundaries ‚ÄĒ `adapters.rs` (1-728), `protocol.rs` (729-1343), `dump.rs` (1344-3215, likely warranting its own `dump/` subdir split into `dump/schedule.rs` + `dump/mysql.rs` + `dump/writer.rs` given its ~1870-line size), `import.rs` (3216-4453), `query.rs` (4454-4657), `schema.rs` (4658-5441), `oneclick.rs` (5442-7137, the verified 42-function wizard), `migrate.rs` (7138-8790), `compare.rs` (8791-9146 + 11613-11688), `dump_format.rs` (9147-10098), and `ddl.rs` (10099-11612). Keep `lib.rs` as a thin `pub mod` re-export root. One correction to the original recommendation: do NOT plan to extract `mod tests` to a `migration_core/tests/` integration-test crate ‚ÄĒ verified that tests call private (non-`pub`) functions directly (`strip_mysql_definer`, `sanitize_view_definition`, and likely others such as `tsv_field`/`mysql_value_to_json`), so an integration-test crate (which only sees the public API) would fail to compile without first widening those functions to `pub(crate)`. Instead, co-locate each `#[cfg(test)] mod tests { ... }` block inside its corresponding new module file (e.g. move the view-sanitization tests at 16744-16995 into `schema.rs`, the dump-manifest tests at 12598-13905ish into `dump_format.rs`/`dump.rs`), which is both idiomatic Rust and naturally partitions the 220+ test functions along the same cluster lines already established. Execute as an incremental, mechanical move+re-export refactor (no logic changes), verifying `cargo build`/`cargo test` after each module extraction ‚ÄĒ this pairs well with the project's already-active decomposition effort visible in recent commits (e.g. 'refactor(schema-diff)', 'refactor(migration-analyzer)', 'refactor(migration-fix-wizard)') for the Python side of the same codebase.
findings.verification_note: Independently re-derived via wc -l and targeted Grep over the actual file (struct/enum/impl/mod counts, full fn listing with line numbers) rather than trusting the original claim. Every cluster boundary, the 35/6/7 struct/enum/impl counts, the single 'mod tests' declaration, and the exact '42 oneclick_* functions' claim all check out against the real file. Only correction made: the recommendation's suggestion to optionally extract tests to a separate integration-test crate is not viable as stated (tests call private functions) and has been narrowed to the co-location approach; the fn-count arithmetic (347/220 vs. the raw 610 total) is a minor, non-material approximation in the original finding. Severity of high is appropriate given the file's role as the entire DB-migration service backend and the fact this is the largest and clearly worst-offending file in the repo by an order of magnitude.
